title: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances Exposure Is Associated with Polycystic Ovary Syndrome Risk among Women Attending a Fertility Clinic
journal: The Science of the total environment
summary: This study demonstrates that serum concentrations of perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) and perfluorohexane sulfonate (PFHxS), environmentally persistent per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS), are significantly associated with increased polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) risk among women attending fertility clinics. The association shows a dose-response relationship, with PFOS identified as the most important contributor to PCOS risk among the PFAS examined, while other PFAS compounds (PFOA, PFNA, PFDA, PFUnDA) showed no consistent associations. The proposed mechanisms underlying this association involve PFOS interference with the hypothalamus-pituitary-ovary axis, leading to inhibition of progesterone, estradiol, and androstenedione secretion, as well as disruption of thyroid hormone synthesis and steroidogenesis—all critical for normal ovarian follicular development. PFAS exposure is also linked to alterations in lipid profiles and metabolic dysfunction that may contribute to PCOS pathogenesis. These findings support the growing evidence that endocrine-disrupting chemical exposure represents an important environmental risk factor for PCOS, a leading cause of anovulatory infertility in reproductive-aged women.

title: A Comprehensive Review of the Contribution of Mitochondrial DNA Mutations and Dysfunction in Polycystic Ovary Syndrome, Supported by Secondary Database Analysis
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive review elucidates how mitochondrial dysfunction serves as a central pathogenic mechanism in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) through interconnected metabolic and endocrine disruptions. Androgen excess and insulin resistance—the defining features of PCOS—synergistically enhance oxidative stress, which damages mitochondrial DNA and impairs the electron transport chain, creating a pathological cycle that perpetuates both metabolic and reproductive dysfunction. Androgens drive this cascade by stimulating reactive oxygen species production through NADPH oxidase activation, suppressing antioxidant defenses, disrupting mitochondrial biogenesis through dysregulation of PGC-1α and AMPK, and promoting lipid accumulation in non-adipose tissues, while simultaneously inducing chronic low-grade inflammation. Insulin resistance exacerbates mitochondrial dysfunction through hyperinsulinemia-triggered androgen secretion and impaired glucose metabolism, creating a bidirectional amplification loop where elevated free fatty acids further compromise mitochondrial capacity and increase oxidative stress. Elevated oxidative stress biomarkers documented in follicular fluid of PCOS patients reflect compromised oocyte quality and follicular development, increasing cancer risk through ROS-induced DNA damage. Mitochondrial-targeted antioxidants like MitoQ10 show promise in reversing these pathologies by reducing pro-apoptotic signaling and improving both insulin resistance and mitochondrial function.

title: Association of Glycemic Index, Glycemic Load, and Carbohydrate Intake with Antral Follicle Counts Among Subfertile Females
summary: This study demonstrates that dietary glycemic index is positively associated with antral follicle count among subfertile women, with those in the highest tertile of GI intake showing 6.3% higher follicle counts compared to the lowest tertile. High-glycemic carbohydrates and refined grain intake are linked to polycystic ovarian morphology and increased risk of PCOS, while whole grain consumption appears protective. The underlying mechanism involves insulin resistance, a pivotal feature of PCOS that affects 75-95% of women with the condition and directly impairs ovarian function through glucose-responsive pathways regulating primordial follicle activation and oocyte competence. Conversely, low-glycemic index diets improve insulin sensitivity, glucoregulatory outcomes, lipid profiles, and androgen status in PCOS patients, with randomized trials showing reductions in follicle number per ovary. These findings suggest that dietary carbohydrate quality significantly influences ovarian reserve and morphology in subfertile women, with therapeutic potential for PCOS management through glycemic index reduction.

title: The association between the follicular distribution pattern of polycystic ovaries and metabolic syndrome development in patients with polycystic ovary syndrome a prospective cohort study
summary: This prospective cohort study examines how ovarian follicular distribution patterns in PCOS patients predict metabolic syndrome development. PCOS is characterized by menstrual disorders, hirsutism, insulin resistance, and obesity, with polycystic ovarian morphology frequently observed in affected women. The peripheral cystic pattern (PCP) follicular distribution is significantly associated with oligomenorrhea and menstrual irregularities, and shows stronger correlation with elevated luteinizing hormone and androgens compared to the general cystic pattern. However, the study's primary finding reveals that follicular distribution pattern itself does not significantly predict metabolic syndrome development, insulin resistance markers, glucose profiles, or lipid profiles—contrary to prior research linking ovarian volume and stromal characteristics to metabolic dysfunction. Instead, stromal density and vascular flow emerge as more predictive markers of PCOS severity and hyperandrogenism. The results suggest that while ovarian morphology correlates with menstrual status and hormonal disturbances, follicular distribution pattern alone has limited utility for identifying PCOS patients at highest metabolic risk.

title: The role of salivary antioxidant level in polycystic ovary syndrome women under assisted reproductive technology treatment: A case-control study
summary:
# Summary

This case-control study examined oxidative stress markers in women with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) undergoing assisted reproductive technology treatment, revealing that PCOS is characterized by elevated reactive oxygen species and reduced antioxidant capacity, which contribute to DNA damage, cell apoptosis, and impaired oocyte quality. Women with PCOS demonstrated paradoxically lower salivary superoxide dismutase (SOD) levels despite elevated serum SOD, serum total oxidant status, and anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH), suggesting a dysregulated antioxidant response specific to the salivary compartment. Salivary SOD emerged as a superior diagnostic marker for PCOS compared to serum SOD, with strong correlations to serum antioxidant capacity, oxidant status, and AMH levels, and demonstrated utility in predicting treatment response in infertility cases. The findings underscore that oxidative stress drives PCOS pathogenesis through multiple mechanisms including inflammation and insulin resistance, while age-dependent increases in oxidant levels further compromise egg quality and fertility. These results suggest that salivary antioxidant profiling, particularly SOD measurement, may improve PCOS diagnosis and predict success in assisted reproduction by providing a noninvasive assessment of the antioxidant imbalance characteristic of this disorder.

title: Shortened Relative Leukocyte Telomere Length Is Associated With Polycystic Ovary Syndrome and Metabolic Traits
summary: This study reveals that women with polycystic ovary syndrome demonstrate significantly shortened relative leukocyte telomere length (rLTL) compared to controls, with each unit increase in rLTL associated with approximately a 50% reduction in PCOS risk. The shortened telomeres in PCOS patients are mechanistically linked to metabolic dysfunction, particularly dyslipidemia and dysglycemia, which together mediate approximately half of the telomere-shortening effect observed in the disease. PCOS patients exhibit characteristic metabolic abnormalities including elevated insulin resistance, fasting glucose, triglycerides, and reduced HDL cholesterol alongside increased central obesity and blood pressure. The inverse association between rLTL and lipid/glucose parameters indicates that telomere length may serve as a biological marker reflecting cumulative oxidative stress and systemic inflammation in PCOS pathophysiology. These findings suggest that telomere attrition in PCOS results primarily from metabolic derangements rather than hormonal abnormalities, positioning shortened telomere length as a potential indicator of cardiometabolic risk in this common reproductive-metabolic disorder.

title: Breast cancer shares many epidemiological, lifestyle, and local hormonal and metabolic underpinnings with endometrial and ovarian cancer: a narrative review
summary: This review identifies shared metabolic and hormonal pathways linking obesity, insulin resistance, and estrogen excess to breast, endometrial, and ovarian cancer risk. Obesity promotes cancer development through multiple mechanisms: visceral adiposity drives insulin resistance and hyperinsulinemia, which activate insulin and IGF-1 receptors on cancer cells; adipose tissue inflammation stimulates aromatase activity, leading to excessive local estrogen synthesis that promotes hormone receptor-positive breast cancer; and pro-inflammatory adipokines modify the tumor microenvironment to enhance chemoresistance. Reproductive and lifestyle factors such as early menarche, late menopause, nulliparity, alcohol consumption, and smoking further increase cancer risk across these three malignancies, primarily by extending estrogen exposure or enhancing circulating estrogen levels. The review emphasizes that insulin and estrogen signaling pathways crosstalk synergistically to drive cancer development, particularly in conditions like polycystic ovarian syndrome and type 2 diabetes where insulin resistance and anovulation are prominent features. Protective factors including parity, oral contraceptive use, breastfeeding, and diets rich in vegetables and fish may reduce cancer risk by modulating hormonal and metabolic milieu.

title: Correlation between the follicular fluid extracellular-vesicle-derived microRNAs and signaling disturbance in the oocytes of women with polycystic ovary syndrome
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies dysregulated microRNAs in follicular fluid-derived extracellular vesicles (FF-EVs) as key contributors to impaired oocyte quality in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS). Using qRT-PCR and bioinformatic analysis, the researchers found that miR-93-3p and miR-152-3p are elevated while miR-625-5p and miR-17-5p are reduced in PCOS patients' FF-EVs compared to controls. These differentially expressed miRNAs impair oocyte developmental competence by dysregulating critical signaling pathways including PI3K/Akt, MAPK, and Ras pathways, with miR-93-3p specifically targeting growth factors such as EGF and IGF. The study demonstrates that FF-EVs can traverse the zona pellucida to directly influence oocyte signaling, and enrichment analysis revealed that dysregulated miRNAs silence genes involved in mitochondrial function and ubiquitin-mediated proteolysis. These findings establish FF-derived exosomal miRNAs as potential biomarkers and therapeutic targets for understanding and treating PCOS-related infertility.

title: Associations of maternal per- and polyfluoroalkyl substance plasma concentrations during pregnancy with offspring polycystic ovary syndrome and related characteristics in Project Viva
summary:
# Summary

This Project Viva study demonstrates that maternal per- and polyfluoroalkyl substance (PFAS) plasma concentrations during pregnancy are associated with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) and related characteristics in offspring, with EtFOSAA showing the strongest association with self-reported PCOS and PFNA associated with moderate-to-severe acne in daughters. PFAS likely exerts these effects by disrupting ovarian function through multiple mechanisms, including impacts on folliculogenesis, steroidogenesis, and androgenic activity, potentially mediated by peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor signaling, oxidative stress, and thyroid hormone disruption. The study found that prenatal PFOS and PFOA exposure correlates with elevated androgenic hormones in cord blood, and PFAS can cross the placental barrier and enter breastmilk, suggesting critical windows for fetal exposure. PCOS itself is characterized by ovulatory dysfunction, hyperandrogenism, and irregular menstrual cycles, and carries significant long-term health risks including infertility, endometrial cancer, and cardiovascular disease. These findings highlight how endocrine-disrupting chemicals during prenatal development may predispose offspring to PCOS through alterations in oogenesis and hormonal regulation, establishing an important link between environmental chemical exposure and reproductive endocrine disorders.

title: Physiopathology of polycystic ovary syndrome in endocrinology, metabolism and inflammation
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is a multifactorial endocrine disorder characterized by elevated androgen levels, impaired ovulation, and ovarian cysts, with pathophysiology involving dysregulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian (HPO) axis, insulin resistance, obesity, and chronic low-grade inflammation. The disease features a pathological feedback loop wherein elevated luteinizing hormone (LH) stimulates excessive androgen production by ovarian theca cells, while suppressed follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) prevents dominant follicle development, perpetuating anovulation and an elevated LH/FSH ratio. Elevated androgen levels trigger endoplasmic reticulum stress in granulosa cells, leading to cell apoptosis and further disruption of follicular development. Additionally, elevated anti-müllerian hormone (AMH) levels in PCOS patients inhibit FSH sensitivity and aromatase activity while activating hypothalamic pathways, contributing to reproductive dysfunction and fertility complications including infertility and miscarriage. Beyond reproductive consequences, PCOS is strongly associated with metabolic and cardiovascular complications, including type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease, driven primarily by insulin resistance and hyperandrogenemia-induced androgen-dependent signaling abnormalities.

title: Insomnia and Female Reproductive Diseases: A Cross-Sectional and Mendelian Randomization Study
summary: This Mendelian randomization study demonstrates that genetic liability to insomnia significantly increases risk for PCOS (odds ratio 1.67), ovarian cysts (OR 1.44), and endometriosis (OR 1.43), with body mass index mediating approximately 10% of the insomnia-PCOS and insomnia-ovarian cyst associations. The mechanism appears to involve insomnia-induced elevation in BMI, which subsequently promotes these reproductive conditions, while insomnia may also trigger dysbiosis (reduced lactobacilli) that independently contributes to endometriosis risk through chronic inflammation and hormonal dysregulation. Notably, insomnia showed no significant association with other female reproductive conditions including premature ovarian insufficiency, ovarian cancer, uterine fibroids, endometrial cancer, or infertility. The findings suggest that sleep disturbance represents a modifiable risk factor for the PCOS-endometriosis-insulin resistance cluster, potentially operating through metabolic and microbiota-mediated inflammatory pathways, which could inform therapeutic interventions targeting sleep quality and microbial composition in affected women.

title: Ultrasound Assessment in Polycystic Ovary Syndrome Diagnosis: From Origins to Future Perspectives—A Comprehensive Review
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is characterized by a complex interplay of hormonal and metabolic dysfunction, where hyperandrogenism and insulin resistance are key drivers of associated metabolic disorders, obesity, and impaired folliculogenesis. Clinically, PCOS manifests with diverse symptoms including menstrual irregularities, hirsutism, acne, and alopecia, while substantially elevating risks for type 2 diabetes, endometrial hyperplasia, endometrial cancer, cardiovascular disease, infertility, and mood and cognitive disorders. Transvaginal ultrasound assessment has become the gold standard for PCOS diagnosis, identifying polycystic ovarian morphology through characteristic features such as increased follicle number (≥20–25 follicles per ovary), elevated ovarian volume, and increased stromal density, with the stromal area-to-ovarian area ratio (>0.32) correlating strongly with androgen levels. Advanced imaging technologies, including three-dimensional ultrasound and Doppler vascular assessment, provide superior sensitivity and reproducibility compared to two-dimensional imaging, while emerging machine learning algorithms and artificial intelligence-driven analysis promise to minimize observer bias and enhance diagnostic speed and accuracy. Recent refinements in ultrasound criteria account for probe frequency variations and developmental changes in adolescents, where PCOM may represent a transient phase rather than pathology, highlighting the importance of age-appropriate diagnostic thresholds for accurate PCOS identification.

title: Association of Trace Elements with Polycystic Ovary Syndrome in Women—A Case-Control Study
summary:
# Summary

This case-control study reveals critical associations between trace element imbalances and polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) pathogenesis. Women with PCOS demonstrated significantly reduced molybdenum levels in whole blood and serum, while elevated serum copper was detected, with copper positively correlating with leukocyte count, suggesting a pro-inflammatory state. Molybdenum deficiency was negatively associated with luteinizing hormone and inversely linked to kidney and liver function markers, whereas molybdenum acts as an insulin-mimetic compound potentially protective against obesity and insulin resistance. Conversely, toxic heavy metals—including cadmium, lead, and mercury—promote PCOS development through oxidative stress and lipid peroxidation, disrupting hormonal signaling and reproductive function, while selenium supplementation demonstrated therapeutic benefits by improving inflammatory and reproductive markers in PCOS women. Dietary and environmental factors significantly influenced trace element exposure, with beef consumption increasing copper, cereal and boiled vegetables increasing molybdenum, and proximity to waste disposal sites inversely affecting molybdenum levels, highlighting the importance of nutritional and environmental modification in PCOS management.

title: Association of age at menarche and different causes of infertility: a retrospective study of 7634 women undergoing assisted reproductive technology
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective study of 7,634 women undergoing assisted reproductive technology examined how age at menarche (AAM) associates with different infertility etiologies. Earlier menarche (≤12-13 years) significantly increased risk of diminished ovarian reserve (DOR), with each additional year of delayed menarche reducing DOR risk by approximately 16.8%, whereas later menarche (≥15 years) substantially elevated polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) risk by 16.7% per year in primary infertility cases. PCOS patients exhibited characteristic endocrine disruptions including elevated luteinizing hormone, estradiol, progesterone, testosterone, and anti-müllerian hormone with suppressed follicle-stimulating hormone, while DOR patients showed elevated FSH and LH with reduced AMH and testosterone. Early menarche moderately increased endometriosis risk, though the association between AAM and endometriosis did not reach statistical significance overall. The findings demonstrate that timing of menarche, as a marker of hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis development, significantly influences the risk profiles for multiple endocrine-related infertility conditions, with distinct dose-response relationships for each condition.

title: Transcriptomic analysis of the effects exerted by curcumin on dihydrotestosterone-induced ovarian granulosa cells
summary:
# Summary

This transcriptomic study demonstrates that curcumin reverses dihydrotestosterone (DHT)-induced dysfunction in ovarian granulosa cells by targeting androgen signaling and metabolic pathways. High androgens impair granulosa cell development through multiple mechanisms: they activate androgen receptors, suppress estrogen synthesis enzymes (CYP19A1 and CYP11A1), disrupt lipid metabolism, and trigger apoptosis via NLRP3 inflammasome activation and oxidative stress. Curcumin counteracts these effects by inhibiting androgen receptor expression, upregulating estrogen synthesis-related enzymes, reducing reactive oxygen species and endoplasmic reticulum stress markers (p-IRE1α and XBP1s), and improving cell viability and mitochondrial function. Additionally, curcumin decreases anti-mullerian hormone (AMH) and progesterone secretion while modulating steroid hormone metabolism genes, effectively correcting the follicular dysplasia characteristic of PCOS. The enrichment of PI3K-Akt and JAK-STAT signaling pathways in curcumin-treated cells suggests these pathways mediate the compound's protective effects on granulosa cell function.

title: Hormones and thrombosis: risk across the reproductive years and beyond
journal: Translational research : the journal of laboratory and clinical medicine
summary:
# Summary

This article examines the complex relationship between reproductive hormones and venous thromboembolism (VTE) risk across women's lifespan and in related conditions. Both endogenous estrogen and progesterone promote hypercoagulability and are associated with significantly elevated VTE risk during pregnancy, postpartum period (60-fold increased risk in the first 6 weeks), and in women with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), where combination oral contraceptives further increase risk nearly 4-fold. Exogenous hormonal contraception, particularly combination formulations and third-generation progestins like desogestrel and drospirenone, substantially increases VTE risk, though progestin-only methods carry minimal risk; notably, these same agents effectively reduce dysmenorrhea and endometriosis symptoms. Pregnancy-associated thrombotic risk stems from elevated coagulation factors (VII, VIII, X), fibrinogen, and von Willebrand factor coupled with downregulated fibrinolysis, creating a prothrombotic state that is further intensified by PCOS-associated hypercoagulability. Women undergoing assisted reproductive technologies face heightened VTE risk due to supraphysiologic hormone levels, particularly with ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome and unsuccessful IVF cycles, managed through anticoagulation with LMWH or UFH.

title: Upregulation of PD-L1 contributes to improving the apoptosis of granulosa cells via the PI3K/AKT pathway in PCOS
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that upregulation of PD-L1 improves granulosa cell apoptosis in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) through activation of the PI3K/AKT signaling pathway. PCOS, a leading cause of anovulatory infertility, is characterized by abnormal granulosa cell apoptosis that impairs oocyte quality and development; the authors found that PD-L1 expression is significantly reduced in PCOS patients and DHEA-induced PCOS mouse models. Treatment with PD-L1 Fc suppressed granulosa cell apoptosis by activating downstream PI3K/AKT signaling, which increased anti-apoptotic factors like Bcl-2 while reducing pro-apoptotic markers such as caspase-3 and Bax. These findings establish the PD-1/PD-L1 pathway as a critical regulator of granulosa cell survival in PCOS and suggest that restoring PD-L1 signaling may represent a therapeutic strategy to improve fertility outcomes in PCOS patients who have increased risks of pregnancy complications including preeclampsia, preterm birth, and miscarriage.

title: Reproductive Endocrine Disorders: A Comprehensive Guide to the Diagnosis and Management of Infertility, Polycystic Ovary Syndrome, and Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive review examines three major reproductive endocrine disorders affecting fertility: polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), endometriosis, and ovulatory dysfunction. PCOS pathogenesis involves androgen excess, insulin resistance, and chronic inflammation that collectively impair follicle development, reduce oocyte quality, and cause anovulation, ultimately leading to infertility in affected women. Endometriosis results from ectopic endometrial tissue colonizing the ovaries and fallopian tubes, where it triggers chronic inflammation, adhesion formation, and impaired ovarian function that compromise fertility through multiple mechanisms including decreased endometrial receptivity. Diagnostic approaches include transvaginal ultrasonography and Rotterdam criteria for PCOS, while laparoscopy remains the gold standard for endometriosis confirmation. Management strategies span lifestyle modifications and oral contraceptive pills for PCOS, whereas in vitro fertilization provides effective treatment for moderate-to-severe endometriosis-related infertility. Together, these disorders represent interconnected pathophysiological processes affecting millions globally, with overlapping metabolic and inflammatory mechanisms that substantially increase infertility risk.

title: Comparative Analysis of Psychosocial Outcomes and Quality of Life Among Nulligravida, Primigravida, and Multigravida Women Diagnosed With Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS)
summary: This study examined psychosocial outcomes and quality of life across nulligravida, primigravida, and multigravida women with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), a condition characterized by hyperandrogenism, ovulatory dysfunction, and insulin resistance. PCOS causes multiple physical symptoms including hirsutism, acne, weight gain, menstrual irregularities, and infertility, which collectively contribute to psychological disturbances such as anxiety, depression, body image dissatisfaction, and reduced self-esteem. The study revealed differential psychological burdens across reproductive groups: nulligravida women experienced the highest rates of severe depression (71.26%), likely reflecting the psychological burden of infertility, while primigravida women reported the most severe anxiety (77.29%), particularly pregnancy-related, and multigravida women reported the poorest quality of life and highest stress levels alongside the most severe PCOS symptoms including hirsutism and acne. Notably, severe anxiety and depression were strongly associated with poor quality of life across all groups, with high stress levels creating a cyclical exacerbation of these psychological symptoms. Additional contributing factors include obesity, which increases metabolic syndrome and type 2 diabetes risk, and hormonal fluctuations that worsen physical symptoms like hair thinning and may contribute to insulin resistance severity. These findings underscore that PCOS represents not merely a medical condition but a significant psychosocial burden requiring comprehensive management addressing both physical manifestations and psychological wellbeing across different reproductive stages.

title: Circulating cytokines levels and the risk of polycystic ovary syndrome: A Mendelian randomization analysis
summary: This Mendelian randomization study identifies circulating cytokines as genetic risk factors for polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), revealing a complex inflammatory landscape underlying the disorder. Elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines—including monocyte chemoattractant protein-1, interleukin-6, tumor necrosis factor-α, and MIP-1α/β—are associated with increased PCOS risk, while protective factors include CXCL11, which acts as a natural antagonist of the pro-inflammatory receptor CCR5, and CD6, a T-cell adhesion molecule. Elevated androgen levels in PCOS patients trigger systemic inflammation and disrupt cytokine regulation, contributing to the disorder's pathophysiology alongside genetic predisposition and metabolic dysregulation. The chemokine CXCL11 demonstrates particular promise as a protective factor through its ability to inhibit CCR5-mediated inflammation, suggesting potential therapeutic targets. PCOS itself has broad clinical consequences, including insulin resistance, hyperandrogenism, and significantly increased risk of type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and reproductive complications such as infertility and endometrial cancer. These findings establish circulating cytokines as key mediators linking immune dysfunction to PCOS pathogenesis and identify potential biomarkers for disease risk stratification.

title: Elucidating the chrononutrition patterns and sleep quality among subfertile patients with different vitamin D levels
summary:
# Summary

This study examined vitamin D status in subfertile patients and identified multiple interconnected factors affecting fertility outcomes. Vitamin D deficiency was significantly associated with subfertility and was particularly prevalent in females (odds ratio 5.186) and those with poor evening eating habits during workdays (odds ratio 25.778), highlighting the importance of chrononutrition patterns. Vitamin D exerts critical reproductive effects through sex hormone interactions: estrogen upregulates vitamin D receptors and promotes active vitamin D metabolites, while vitamin D itself directly regulates ovarian function, oocyte quality, and implantation efficacy in women, with receptors present in ovaries and uterus. In males, vitamin D supports sperm quality by regulating calcium channels (CatSper), promoting mitochondrial energy production, and providing antioxidant protection against oxidative stress. The study revealed that circadian rhythm disruptions and poor sleep quality impair both hormone production and vitamin D metabolism, while elevated BMI and high-fat diets further compound deficiency by impairing vitamin D synthesis and activation through downregulation of vitamin D-25-hydroxylase. These findings underscore vitamin D deficiency as a modifiable risk factor linking chrononutrition, sleep patterns, and the fertility-relevant conditions of PCOS, endometriosis, and insulin resistance.

title: Deciphering the association between the Life’s Essential 8 and infertility: insights into depression, inflammation, and metabolic mediation
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that the Life's Essential 8 (LE8), a comprehensive cardiovascular health metric, inversely associates with female infertility, with better cardiovascular health scores protective against reproductive failure. Among the LE8 components, sleep health, body mass index, and blood glucose control emerged as particularly significant factors, with lower scores in these domains correlating with increased infertility risk. The relationship between LE8 and infertility is substantially mediated through three pathways: depression (measured by PHQ-9), systemic immune inflammation index, and elevated uric acid levels, with higher depression and uric acid independently linked to infertility while inflammatory markers showed inverse associations. Multiple infertility etiologies contribute to this relationship, including endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, premature ovarian insufficiency, and conditions involving anovulation, all of which share common metabolic and inflammatory underpinnings with cardiovascular disease. Obesity serves as a critical mechanistic hub, triggering oxidative stress and ovarian inflammation that stimulates androgen production, ultimately disrupting oocyte maturation and causing follicular atresia and anovulatory cycles. These findings underscore that lifestyle-related cardiovascular risk factors fundamentally overlap with reproductive dysfunction, suggesting that interventions improving overall cardiovascular health through sleep optimization, metabolic control, and inflammation reduction may offer therapeutic benefits for infertile populations.

title: Diagnosis and management of infertility in patients with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS): guidelines from the Italian Society of Human Reproduction (SIRU) and the Italian Centers for the Study and Conservation of Eggs and Sperm (CECOS Italy)
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome is a multifaceted reproductive disorder strongly associated with infertility through multiple mechanisms including ovulatory dysfunction, endometrial abnormalities, and impaired oocyte competence. Diagnosis requires at least two of three criteria: irregular menstrual cycles, clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism (assessed via total testosterone, SHBG, and free testosterone), and polycystic ovarian morphology (detected through follicle number per ovary on ultrasound). The primary therapeutic innovations for treating PCOS-related infertility are letrozole and GnRH antagonists for ovulation induction, with clomiphene citrate and metformin serving as additional options to improve reproductive outcomes. Beyond reproductive concerns, women with PCOS face significantly elevated risks for cardiovascular disease, impaired glucose tolerance, type 2 diabetes, and endometrial hyperplasia/cancer, necessitating comprehensive metabolic and cardiovascular risk assessment including lipid profiles and glucose tolerance testing. Comprehensive evaluation should include semen analysis and tubal patency testing in partners, and hysteroscopic assessment may be warranted to identify endometrial pathology contributing to infertility.

title: Vitamin D in Reproductive Health Disorders: A Narrative Review Focusing on Infertility, Endometriosis, and Polycystic Ovarian Syndrome
summary:
# Summary

Vitamin D plays a multifaceted role in reproductive health by regulating ovarian steroidogenesis, folliculogenesis, and endometrial receptivity through binding to vitamin D receptors widely distributed across female reproductive tissues including the ovaries, uterus, and placenta. Vitamin D deficiency is significantly linked to three major reproductive disorders—polycystic ovarian syndrome, endometriosis, and infertility—and is associated with insulin resistance, hormonal imbalances, and chronic inflammatory processes that impair fertility. In women with PCOS, vitamin D supplementation has been shown in randomized controlled trials to improve insulin sensitivity, reduce androgen levels, and enhance ovulatory function, while in endometriosis, vitamin D's anti-inflammatory and immune-modulating properties may alleviate pelvic pain and reduce inflammatory markers. Additionally, adequate vitamin D levels correlate with improved ovarian reserve and sperm quality, whereas deficiency is linked to reduced anti-Müllerian hormone levels and decreased fertility potential. Vitamin D supplementation has demonstrated clinical benefits across multiple reproductive disorders by improving menstrual regularity, enhancing endometrial receptivity, and reducing serum testosterone and inflammatory markers, suggesting it is a critical nutrient for maintaining reproductive function and treating these overlapping fertility disorders.

title: Enhancing understanding of endometrial function in patients with PCOS: clinical and immunological insights
summary:
# Summary

PCOS is associated with significantly elevated risks of adverse pregnancy outcomes including miscarriage, hypertensive disorders, preterm birth, and fetal malformations, with endometrial dysfunction emerging as a key contributing mechanism beyond traditional ovulatory defects. The endometrial pathology in PCOS involves a shift in T cell balance characterized by reduced Th1 cells and elevated Th2 cells, alongside systemic and local low-grade inflammation with altered macrophage and natural killer cell populations that collectively compromise endometrial receptivity. PCOS patients undergoing fresh embryo transfer experience particularly poor outcomes due to combined effects of ovarian stimulation and endometrial dysfunction, though frozen embryo transfer shows improved clinical pregnancy and live birth rates across PCOS phenotypic subgroups. Among PCOS phenotypes, those with combined polycystic ovarian morphology and anovulation exhibit the most severe reproductive dysfunction, including reduced fertilization and embryo quality rates, yet paradoxically show improved outcomes with frozen embryo transfer, suggesting that maternal endometrial factors rather than gamete quality drive adverse outcomes. The hyperandrogenic and anovulatory phenotypes are particularly associated with elevated miscarriage risk and endometrial dysfunction, highlighting the importance of comprehensive immunological assessment and personalized treatment strategies targeting endometrial health in PCOS management.

title: Association of Rs13405728, Rs12478601, and Rs2479106 single nucleotide polymorphisms and in vitro fertilization and embryo transfer efficacy in patients with polycystic ovarian syndrome: A case control genome‐wide association study
journal: The Kaohsiung Journal of Medical Sciences
summary: This genome-wide association study identified three single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs)—rs13405728 in LHCGR, rs12478601 in THADA, and rs2479106 in DENND1A—as independent risk factors for polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) in a case-control cohort, with family history of diabetes mellitus also significantly associated with disease susceptibility. The LHCGR rs13405728 TT genotype, THADA rs12478601 CC genotype, and DENND1A rs2479106 AG/GG genotypes were each associated with poor in vitro fertilization-embryo transfer (IVF-ET) outcomes in PCOS patients, characterized by elevated abortion rates and reduced clinical gestation rates despite increased oocyte retrieval. Notably, the THADA rs12478601 CC genotype carriers displayed elevated serum low-density lipoprotein cholesterol levels, linking the polymorphism to metabolic dysfunction and cardiovascular risk beyond reproductive phenotypes. These findings suggest that PCOS-associated SNPs in genes regulating ovarian function (LHCGR), pancreatic beta-cell function (THADA), and Rab protein trafficking (DENND1A) serve as molecular markers predicting reproductive outcome after assisted reproduction, with broader implications for understanding the metabolic and endocrine pathophysiology underlying PCOS-related subfertility.

title: miR-34a-5p modulation of polycystic ovary syndrome via targeting the NOTCH signaling pathway
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies miR-34a-5p as a key regulatory microRNA in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) that modulates granulosa cell function through the NOTCH signaling pathway. The researchers found that miR-34a-5p is significantly elevated in both serum and follicular fluid of PCOS patients, where it directly targets JAG1 mRNA and protein to suppress NOTCH pathway activation. Functionally, elevated miR-34a-5p inhibits granulosa cell proliferation while promoting apoptosis through caspase-dependent mechanisms (upregulating Caspase-3, Caspase-9, and Bax while downregulating Bcl-2), whereas inhibition of miR-34a-5p reverses this dysfunction. The findings suggest that miR-34a-5p serves as a potential therapeutic target for PCOS-associated infertility and may function as a biomarker for disease diagnosis, with broader relevance to related reproductive conditions including endometriosis and pregnancy complications.

title: A retrospective study “myo-inositol is a cost-saving strategy for controlled ovarian stimulation in non-polycystic ovary syndrome art patients.”
summary:
# Summary

Myo-inositol supplementation improves outcomes in assisted reproductive technology (ART) patients by enhancing insulin sensitivity and reducing gonadotropin requirements during controlled ovarian stimulation. As an insulin-sensitizing molecule and second messenger of FSH, myo-inositol facilitates cellular glucose uptake, regulates FSH-mediated granulosa cell proliferation and maturation, and modulates anti-müllerian hormone production, thereby improving oocyte quality and embryo development. In this retrospective study comparing patients receiving folic acid alone (control) versus folic acid combined with myo-inositol and alpha-lactalbumin (treatment group), myo-inositol supplementation significantly reduced recombinant FSH requirements, shortened the duration of controlled ovarian stimulation, increased viable oocytes and grade I embryos, and lowered the risk of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome. These improvements translated to a substantial cost-saving benefit, reducing the average cost per pregnancy from €14,148 to €13,001, demonstrating that myo-inositol represents an effective and economically advantageous strategy for ART protocols. The findings support the use of myo-inositol supplementation across fertility care, particularly for addressing metabolic dysfunction and optimizing reproductive outcomes in non-PCOS populations undergoing assisted reproduction.

title: The mitochondrial DNA copy number and ovary‐related reproductive disorders: A bidirectional two‐sample Mendelian randomization study
journal: International Journal of Gynaecology and Obstetrics
summary: This bidirectional Mendelian randomization study investigated the causal relationship between mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA) copy number and ovary-related reproductive disorders, with particular focus on polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) and ovarian endometriosis. The analysis revealed that genetically predicted higher mtDNA copy number causally increases the risk of both PCOS (OR = 1.16) and ovarian endometriosis, supporting the involvement of mitochondrial dysfunction in these conditions' etiology. Mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative stress, which influence mtDNA copy number, are recognized as primary contributors to multiple reproductive disorders. The study identified that mtDNA copy number shows reduced levels in women with endometriosis and PCOS patients, with specific mtDNA mutations (including MT-tRNALeu variants) linked to increased PCOS risk, and notably demonstrated that mtDNA copy number could serve as a non-invasive biomarker for early detection of these conditions. Conversely, the reverse analysis found no causal association between ovary-related reproductive disorders and mtDNA copy number, nor any causal link between mtDNA copy number and ovarian dysfunction, ovarian cysts, or premature ovarian failure, suggesting the relationship is directional and specific to PCOS and ovarian endometriosis.

title: High AMH Levels Are Associated With Gestational Hypertension in Patients With PCOS who Underwent IVF/ICSI-ET
journal: The Journal of Clinical Endocrinology and Metabolism
summary: This study demonstrates that elevated prepregnancy anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) levels are significantly associated with gestational hypertension in PCOS patients undergoing IVF/ICSI-ET, with adjusted odds of gestational hypertension increasing by up to 4.75-fold in women with the highest AMH levels. The mechanistic basis involves AMH's role as a TGF-β superfamily member that may aberrantly regulate trophoblast invasion and placental development through Smad phosphorylation signaling, and AMH also impairs placental function by reducing aromatase activity, thereby decreasing estrogen and progesterone synthesis necessary for healthy pregnancy. PCOS patients with gestational hypertension exhibited higher BMI, fasting blood glucose, blood pressure, lower serum estradiol, and earlier delivery compared to those without hypertension, reflecting the metabolic complexity of the condition involving insulin resistance, obesity, and hyperandrogenism. The study identified AMH as a predictive biomarker for gestational hypertension risk in this population, with an area under the curve of 0.654, suggesting clinical utility for early pregnancy screening and risk stratification. These findings underscore how hyperandrogenic PCOS phenotypes may predispose to hypertensive disorders of pregnancy through AMH-mediated placental dysfunction, thereby increasing maternal, fetal, and neonatal morbidity in assisted reproductive technology outcomes.

title: Healthy Moms, Healthy Babies: Culinary and Lifestyle Medicine for PCOS and Preconception Health
summary:
# Summary

This article examines lifestyle and culinary medicine approaches for managing polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) and improving preconception health. PCOS is characterized by insulin resistance, systemic inflammation, and metabolic dysfunction affecting 75-95% of patients, and carries significant psychological burden including anxiety, depression, and eating disorders alongside weight management difficulties. Dietary interventions—particularly Mediterranean and whole-food plant-based diets rich in high-fiber carbohydrates, low glycemic index foods, whole grains, and pulses—combined with regular exercise and behavioral strategies such as cognitive-behavioral therapy and mindfulness-based stress reduction improve PCOS symptoms, restore ovulation, and reduce cardiovascular disease and diabetes risk. Evidence-based lifestyle modifications achieving as little as 5% weight loss restore metabolic health, improve body composition, and enhance quality of life, while culinary medicine approaches that teach practical cooking skills enhance dietary adherence and facilitate sustainable behavior change. The authors emphasize that comprehensive preconception care must address both the metabolic and mental health dimensions of PCOS and infertility, recognizing that food insecurity and weight stigma represent significant barriers to appropriate evaluation and treatment.

title: Menstrual disorder is associated with blood type in PCOS patients: evidence from a cross-sectional survey
summary: This cross-sectional study reveals that ABO blood type is independently associated with menstrual disorder patterns in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) patients. Blood type O individuals with PCOS demonstrate significantly higher menstrual bleeding levels, elevated estradiol (E2) levels, reduced follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) and luteinizing hormone (LH) levels, and higher body mass index compared to other blood types, while blood types A, B, and AB are associated with lighter menstrual bleeding. The study suggests these associations may be mediated through differential antigen expression affecting endocrine and metabolic function, with genetic factors including ABO blood group genes and TRAF2 gene polymorphisms implicated as potential etiological mechanisms. Additionally, blood type O's known association with reduced von Willebrand factor and factor VIII levels may contribute to abnormal menstrual bleeding through coagulation dysfunction. These findings suggest that ABO blood type could serve as a genetic marker for identifying PCOS patients at risk for specific menstrual and endocrine phenotypes, potentially enabling more personalized clinical management approaches.

title: Unilateral or Bilateral Laparoscopic Ovarian Drilling in Polycystic Ovary Syndrome: A Meta-analysis of Randomized Trials
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), a condition affecting 6-10% of women globally, causes anovulatory infertility through disruption of the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis and elevation of insulin and androgen levels. Laparoscopic ovarian drilling (LOD) represents an effective second-line treatment that induces ovulation in 80% of patients by improving follicular fluid flow, normalizing hormone synthesis, and restoring ovarian sensitivity to stimulation drugs, achieving pregnancy rates of 60-80%. However, LOD carries significant risks including periovarian adhesions, premature ovarian failure, and ovarian reserve damage, with unilateral LOD potentially reducing complications while maintaining comparable reproductive outcomes to bilateral LOD. Alternative medical approaches including clomiphene citrate, letrozole, and insulin sensitizers address the metabolic and reproductive dysfunction in PCOS, with letrozole offering advantages through improved endometrial thickness and reduced miscarriage risk. The evidence supports LOD as an important surgical option for PCOS-related infertility in clomiphene-resistant cases, though careful patient selection and technique optimization are needed to balance efficacy against adverse effects.

title: The Molecular Basis of Polycystic Ovary Syndrome and Its Cardiometabolic Correlates: Exploring the Intersection and Its Clinical Implications—A Narrative Review
summary: Polycystic ovary syndrome significantly increases cardiometabolic disease risk through multiple interconnected pathways involving insulin resistance, hyperandrogenism, and genetic factors. Elevated androgens in PCOS women promote cardiovascular dysfunction by activating the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system, inducing chronic low-grade inflammation and oxidative stress, and promoting visceral adiposity—mechanisms that collectively lead to atherosclerosis, hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and subclinical vascular pathologies including coronary artery calcification and increased carotid intima-media thickness. Genome-wide association studies have identified 14 independent genetic loci affecting PCOS pathogenesis, with key genes including THADA and INSR contributing to insulin resistance, FSHB involved in gonadotropin signaling, and others regulating lipid metabolism and cardiac remodeling. Epigenetic modifications, particularly hypomethylation in granulosa cells and altered methylation of metabolic genes like LEP, LEPR, and ADIPOR2, further dysregulate insulin and adipokine signaling. Sex hormone-binding globulin deficiency, a hallmark of PCOS, correlates with higher bioavailable androgens and increased coronary calcification risk. These findings underscore that PCOS pathophysiology integrates genetic predisposition, hormonal dysregulation, and metabolic dysfunction to precipitate both reproductive complications and severe long-term cardiometabolic sequelae requiring comprehensive clinical management.

title: Predicting factors of ovarian responses in infertile women with polycystic ovary syndrome undergoing IVF/ICSI
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary:
# Summary

In infertile women with polycystic ovary syndrome undergoing IVF/ICSI, this study identified key predictive factors for ovarian response outcomes. Elevated body mass index (BMI) and follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) emerged as significant risk factors for poor and sub-optimal ovarian responses, while higher anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) levels predicted hyper-response. BMI negatively correlated with oocyte number and pregnancy outcomes and was positively associated with longer infertility duration and elevated testosterone, indicating metabolic dysfunction in PCOS patients. Age and BMI were strong negative predictors of ovarian response and pregnancy success, with older maternal age linked to accelerated follicle loss. The study demonstrates that multivariate assessment of BMI, FSH, and AMH can effectively stratify PCOS patients into poor, normal, or hyper-responders, enabling personalized gonadotropin dosing and cycle management to optimize reproductive outcomes while minimizing complications such as ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome.

title: BMAL1 improves assisted reproductive technology outcomes in patients with polycystic ovary syndrome by targeting BMP6 and regulating ovarian granulosa cell apoptosis
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary: This study identifies BMAL1, a circadian clock gene, as a critical regulator of assisted reproductive technology (ART) outcomes in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) patients through a BMP6-dependent mechanism. BMAL1 expression is significantly reduced in PCOS patients compared to controls, whereas BMP6 is correspondingly elevated, and these factors show a strong negative correlation. BMAL1 promotes ovarian granulosa cell proliferation and inhibits apoptosis by downregulating BMP6, effects that directly correlate with improved embryo quality and quantity, including increased 2PN fertilized oocytes, available embryos, and high-quality embryos. BMP6 elevation in PCOS inversely correlates with these same reproductive outcomes, suggesting that restoring BMAL1 expression to suppress excessive BMP6 signaling could improve oocyte and embryo quality. Beyond the BMP6 axis, BMAL1 broadly regulates female reproductive function at multiple physiological levels, including sex hormone synthesis and follicle development, with BMAL1 deficiency impairing fertilization rates, embryonic development, and implantation potential. The findings suggest that targeting the BMAL1-BMP6 axis represents a novel therapeutic strategy to enhance ART success rates in PCOS patients.

title: Hormonal and metabolic influences on outcomes in PCOS undergoing assisted reproduction: the role of BMI in fresh embryo transfers
summary: This study examined hormonal and metabolic profiles in PCOS patients undergoing assisted reproduction and their association with pregnancy outcomes. PCOS patients exhibited significantly elevated insulin, luteinizing hormone, anti-Müllerian hormone, testosterone, and dyslipidemia (elevated LDL, total cholesterol, and triglycerides with reduced HDL), alongside insulin resistance measured by HOMA-IR. Despite producing more high-quality blastocysts and Day 3 embryos, PCOS patients paradoxically experienced significantly higher miscarriage rates and lower live birth rates, which were positively correlated with BMI, LH levels, and total testosterone. Among high-BMI PCOS patients, miscarriage rates were substantially elevated, suggesting that metabolic dysfunction and hormonal imbalances—including reduced endometrial thickness—impair pregnancy maintenance independently of embryo quality. The findings reveal a critical BMI-dependent phenotype: obese PCOS patients exhibited greater metabolic dysfunction, while normal-BMI PCOS patients showed more pronounced hormonal imbalances. These results underscore that factors beyond oocyte and embryo quality, particularly endometrial receptivity and hormonal-metabolic homeostasis, are essential determinants of reproductive success in PCOS patients undergoing fresh embryo transfer.

title: Waist circumference mediates the relationship between atherogenic index of plasma and infertility
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that the Atherogenic Index of Plasma (AIP), a lipid-based metabolic marker, is strongly associated with female infertility, with each one-unit increase in AIP linked to a 94% increase in infertility odds. Waist circumference acts as a critical mediator of this relationship, accounting for 54.49% of the AIP-infertility association, particularly in the context of abdominal obesity. The mechanism appears to involve dysregulated lipid metabolism and insulin resistance, which impair oocyte quality and endometrial receptivity through oxidative stress and inflammatory pathways including TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6 production. These findings are particularly relevant to PCOS and endometriosis, conditions characterized by elevated triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, and systemic inflammation that collectively compromise female reproductive function. The study provides evidence for a metabolic pathway linking atherogenic dyslipidemia to infertility through central adiposity, suggesting that metabolic interventions targeting lipid profiles and waist circumference could improve fertility outcomes.

title: Regulatory risk loci link disrupted androgen response to pathophysiology of Polycystic Ovary Syndrome
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies regulatory risk loci that disrupt androgen response pathways underlying polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) pathophysiology. PCOS is characterized by dysregulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis, resulting in abnormal LH:FSH ratios, elevated androgens, and anovulation through impaired folliculogenesis and enhanced steroidogenesis. Genome-wide association studies revealed that PCOS-associated genetic variants, particularly in loci such as THADA, DENND1A, IRF1, and FTO, predominantly locate within intronic regions and disrupt binding sites of key transcription factors including NR4A1/2, WT1, NR5A1, and FOXA1 that regulate steroidogenesis and folliculogenesis. The analysis demonstrates that these regulatory disruptions affect multiple tissues including the fetal brain, pancreas, adipocytes, and endothelial cells, mechanistically linking obesity and hyperinsulinemia—which worsens hyperandrogenism—to PCOS development. Additionally, HPG axis disruption is associated with endometriosis, indicating a shared pathophysiological mechanism between these reproductive disorders. These findings elucidate how disrupted androgen-mediated signaling and metabolic dysregulation converge to produce the multifaceted reproductive abnormalities characteristic of PCOS.

title: Is There An Association between Dyslipidemia and The Risk
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates the association between dyslipidemia and assisted reproductive technology (ART) outcomes in non-obese women with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS). Dyslipidemia was found to significantly upregulate pro-inflammatory cytokines TNF-α and IL-18 in both serum and follicular fluid, which negatively correlate with oocyte retrieval, maturation, fertilization, and embryo development rates. These elevated inflammatory cytokines disrupt oocyte nuclear and cytoplasmic maturation, compromise follicular steroidogenesis, and impair overall oocyte quality. Dyslipidemia-induced ovarian inflammation and oxidative stress create a hostile microenvironment that compromises reproductive outcomes, and these effects on ART success are independent of lipid status alone. Notably, women with normal lipid profiles paradoxically exhibited higher ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS) risk, suggesting dyslipidemia's complex role in reproductive pathophysiology. The findings establish dyslipidemia as a critical metabolic factor linking PCOS pathology to female infertility through lipid-induced inflammatory cascades.

title: Follicular Fluid Metabolomics: Tool for Predicting IVF Outcomes of Different Infertility Causes
summary:
# Summary

Follicular fluid metabolomics emerges as a powerful tool for predicting IVF outcomes across different infertility etiologies by revealing metabolic signatures associated with oocyte developmental competence and embryo viability. In polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), a metabolic disorder characterized by anovulation and infertility, follicular fluid shows distinctive alterations including decreased amino acids (leucine, threonine), reduced energy metabolites (acetate, β-hydroxybutyrate, lactate), elevated glucose and creatine, and markedly diminished lipid species (fatty acids, ceramides, phosphatidylcholines, sphingomyelins). These lipid dysfunctions impair oocyte competence through dysregulation of glycerolipid, glycerophospholipid, and sphingolipid metabolism, with elevated prostaglandins (PGE2, PGJ2) further correlating with reduced embryo quality. In endometriosis, an estrogen-dependent inflammatory disorder affecting 25-40% of infertile women, the follicular fluid metabolome displays opposing lipid abnormalities, including elevated sphingolipids and phosphatidylcholines, increased lysophosphatidylcholines, and enhanced energy metabolites (lactate, glucose, pyruvate). These distinct metabolomic profiles in PCOS and endometriosis enable both diagnostic and prognostic biomarker identification, offering personalized treatment strategies to improve assisted reproductive technology outcomes.

title: Multiomics and Systematic Analyses Reveal the Roles of Hemoglobin and the HIF‐1 Pathway in Polycystic Ovary Syndrome
summary:
# Summary

This multiomics study reveals that elevated hemoglobin levels are causally associated with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) and its key manifestations, including hyperandrogenism, ovulatory dysfunction, and polycystic ovarian morphology, with each 10 g/L increase in hemoglobin conferring a 22% higher odds of PCOS. The HIF-1 signaling pathway emerges as a central mechanism, with three testosterone-associated genes—NFKB1, INSR (insulin receptor), and PRKCA—significantly upregulated in both blood and ovarian granulosa cells of PCOS patients, showing strong positive correlations with testosterone levels. Elevated hemoglobin may drive PCOS pathogenesis through multiple interconnected mechanisms: chronic hypoxia leading to oxidative stress, inflammation, and insulin resistance; increased blood viscosity reducing blood flow and endothelial function; and altered oxygen delivery to the ovarian microenvironment affecting cellular autophagy and luteinization. These findings establish hemoglobin as a novel risk factor for PCOS and identify the HIF-1 pathway as a key therapeutic target, with implications for understanding the metabolic complications that increase cardiovascular disease and type 2 diabetes risk in PCOS patients.

title: The Impact of Polycystic Ovary Syndrome on Gestational Diabetes Mellitus, Disease Knowledge, and Health Behaviors
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome significantly increases the risk of gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) through underlying insulin resistance and metabolic dysfunction, with PCOS patients showing a 2.94-fold elevated GDM risk and associated complications including preterm birth, fetal overgrowth, and cesarean delivery. PCOS is characterized by elevated androgens, hormonal imbalances (reflected in elevated LH/FSH ratios and AMH levels), and metabolic dysregulation manifested in higher BMI, triglycerides, LDL cholesterol, and HbA1c levels, alongside classic symptoms including hirsutism, acne, menstrual irregularities, and infertility. The pathophysiology involves chronic low-grade inflammation and insulin resistance that compromise placental function and promote preterm labor, while abdominal obesity further exacerbates hormonal imbalances and symptom severity. Lifestyle interventions including high-intensity exercise combined with balanced nutrition significantly improve PCOS outcomes and reduce postpartum type 2 diabetes risk, with greater disease knowledge correlating strongly with healthier health behaviors. Pre-pregnancy weight management and targeted dietary modifications emerge as critical preventive strategies for reducing GDM susceptibility in PCOS patients, while improved nutritional knowledge and physical activity levels correlate with better maternal outcomes.

title: Body image disturbances in women of childbearing age with polycystic ovary syndrome: a cross-sectional study
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study examined body image disturbances in women of childbearing age with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), which is characterized by hyperandrogenism, anovulation, and polycystic ovary morphology. Excessive androgen production in PCOS leads to multiple clinical manifestations including acne, hirsutism, and anovulation, ultimately contributing to infertility and psychological distress. The study identified multiple factors significantly associated with body image disturbance, including physical symptoms (acne, hirsutism, hair loss, elevated BMI), sociodemographic factors (occupation), and psychosocial variables (intimate relationship quality, family support, and psychological resilience). Notably, BMI emerged as a particularly strong predictor, with patients having BMI ≥28 showing significantly greater body image impairment than those with lower BMI. Interestingly, biochemical hormonal markers (follicle-stimulating hormone, luteinizing hormone, testosterone, estrogen) and metabolic indicators showed no significant association with body image disturbance, suggesting that the psychological impact stems primarily from visible clinical symptoms rather than underlying endocrine abnormalities. These findings highlight the multifactorial nature of body image disturbance in PCOS, encompassing both visible physical manifestations and psychosocial support systems.

title: Comprehensive Assessment of Genital Infections and Reproductive Health in Women Visiting a Fertility Clinic in Warangal, India—A Case‐Control Study
summary: This case-control study demonstrates that genital infections represent a major threat to reproductive health, with multiple infection types—including vaginitis, cervicitis, pelvic inflammatory disorder, and tuberculosis—showing strong associations with female infertility in an Indian fertility clinic population. Genital infections initiate a cascade of reproductive pathology by directly causing endometriosis, endometrial disorders, PCOS, ovarian dysfunction, and tubal/uterine blockage, while untreated infections progress to chronic inflammation and reproductive tract scarring that irreversibly compromise fertility. Notably, specific pathogens like Chlamydia trachomatis and Ureaplasma urealyticum, along with bacterial vaginosis, substantially reduce pregnancy outcomes by increasing pelvic inflammatory disorder risk and reducing conception chances by 40%. The study reveals previously underappreciated cross-gender effects, with female genital infections strongly correlating with male semen abnormalities including teratozoospermia, pyospermia, and asthenozoospermia. Menstrual irregularities and hormonal perturbations associated with genital infections further disrupt ovulation and ovarian function. Early detection combined with antimicrobial treatment and anti-inflammatory adjunct therapies substantially improves fertility outcomes, highlighting the critical importance of infection screening in women with infertility.

title: The effect of evening primrose oil on adolescent girl patients with PCOS: A double-blind placebo-controlled randomized study
summary:
# Summary

Evening primrose oil (EPO) demonstrates significant therapeutic effects in adolescent girls with polycystic ovary syndrome through multiple hormonal and metabolic mechanisms. In a 12-week double-blind placebo-controlled trial, EPO treatment regulated irregular menstrual cycles and reduced key biomarkers of PCOS pathology: insulin levels decreased (p=0.01), testosterone levels decreased (p=0.03), and sex hormone binding globulin (SHBG) increased (p=0.04), resulting in lower free androgen index (p=0.03). These improvements likely stem from the active components alpha-linoleic and gamma-linoleic acid in EPO, which exert anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects that enhance insulin sensitivity and improve folliculogenesis. By increasing SHBG and reducing insulin and androgen levels, EPO addresses the fundamental hormonal dysregulation underlying PCOS, offering a phytotherapeutic alternative to conventional pharmacological treatments for managing menstrual irregularities, hyperandrogenism, and associated metabolic complications in adolescent patients.

title: Insulin Resistance in Pediatric Obesity: From Mechanisms to Treatment Strategies
summary:
# Summary

Insulin resistance in pediatric obesity results from convergent mechanisms including lipid metabolism disorders, oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, inflammation, and genetic factors affecting key signaling proteins like GLUT4 and the insulin receptor substrate. Obesity, particularly central obesity and excessive visceral adiposity, drives insulin resistance through multiple pathways including elevated free fatty acids and endoplasmic reticulum stress, ultimately triggering a pathological cycle of hyperinsulinemia, beta cell damage, and progression to type 2 diabetes mellitus. Hormonal regulation during puberty significantly modulates insulin sensitivity through the growth hormone/insulin-like growth factor-1 axis and sex steroids, with leptin linking adipose tissue function to hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis activation and reproductive timing. Beyond glycemic control, insulin resistance contributes to cardiovascular disease, hypertension, and dyslipidemia, with early childhood insulin resistance at age 13 predicting hypertension and hypertriglyceridemia by age 19. White adipose tissue dysfunction emerges as a central pathogenic mechanism, highlighting the importance of preventing leptin resistance and restoring normal adipose tissue metabolism as therapeutic targets in obese pediatric populations.

title: The Association Between Sleep Disorder and Female Infertility: A Mediation Analysis of Inflammatory and Oxidative Markers
summary: Sleep disorders in reproductive-age women increase infertility risk through multiple inflammatory and oxidative stress pathways, with infertility affecting ovulation and contributing to conditions including endometriosis. Sleep disruption elevates systemic inflammatory markers including high-sensitivity C-reactive protein (hs-CRP), systolic and diastolic blood pressure index (SII), and systemic inflammation response index (SIRI), which mediate the sleep-infertility relationship and directly increase infertility risk by 2.5–3.4% per unit increment. Elevated gamma-glutamyl transferase (GGT) acts as a detrimental factor for infertility, while total bilirubin provides protective effects, suggesting hepatic function indices may modulate reproductive outcomes. Sleep disruption compromises the antioxidant and anti-inflammatory protective effects of melatonin in follicular fluid, reducing oocyte quality and ovarian function while simultaneously increasing proinflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-17A) and chemokines. The association is particularly pronounced in women over 30 years or with BMI ≥30 kg/m², indicating age and obesity amplify sleep-related reproductive vulnerability. These findings establish sleep disorder as a modifiable risk factor for female infertility through interconnected inflammatory and oxidative mechanisms relevant to endometriosis-associated infertility.

title: Elastography in Reproductive Medicine, a Game-Changer for Diagnosing Polycystic Ovary Syndrome, Predicting Intrauterine Insemination Success, and Enhancing In Vitro Fertilization Outcomes: A Systematic Review
summary: Elastography, an ultrasound-based imaging technique that measures tissue stiffness, emerges as a promising non-invasive tool for assessing reproductive function across multiple dimensions. In polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), increased ovarian stiffness caused by stromal fibrosis contributes to anovulation and impaired ovarian function, making elastography valuable for diagnosis. The technique also evaluates critical uterine factors for fertility: excessive or irregular uterine contractions reduce intrauterine insemination (IUI) success, while greater elasticity index and lower contraction frequency are associated with improved pregnancy rates. In in vitro fertilization, endometrial stiffness measured by shear wave elastography inversely correlates with implantation potential, with increased stiffness linked to poor blood perfusion and reduced receptivity in unexplained infertility cases. PCOS-related metabolic alterations including insulin resistance and hyperandrogenism contribute to abnormal uterine contractility patterns that compromise reproductive outcomes. Overall, elastography demonstrates potential as a comprehensive predictive and diagnostic tool by quantifying biomechanical properties of ovarian and endometrial tissues that directly influence fertility success.

title: Evaluating the Impact of rs4025935, rs71748309, rs699947, and rs4646994 Genetic Determinants on Polycystic Ovary Syndrome Predisposition—A Case-Control Study
summary: This case-control study investigated genetic determinants of polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) susceptibility, identifying four key single-nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) in glutathione S-transferase (GST) and vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) genes as significant risk factors. Null genotypes at rs4025935 and rs71748309, insertion-deletion variants at rs4646994, and the A allele at rs699947 in VEGF were strongly associated with increased PCOS predisposition. The underlying mechanisms involve impaired oxidative stress defense, as GST enzyme polymorphisms reduce reactive oxygen species detoxification capacity, and dysregulation of angiogenic pathways critical for ovarian follicle development and ovulation. PCOS patients demonstrated characteristic endocrine and metabolic abnormalities including elevated testosterone, altered gonadotropins (LH and FSH), increased fasting glucose and HOMA-IR indices, and dyslipidemia with elevated cholesterol, triglycerides, and LDL. The findings also highlight PCOS's significant associations with serious comorbidities including Type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and endometrial cancer, positioning these genetic variants as potential biomarkers for risk stratification and early intervention in PCOS management.

title: Fertility-Enhancing Potential of P. amygdalas and J. regia Oil Mixture in Wistar Rats: Male/Female Infertility Models Assessment
summary: A mixture of almond (Prunus amygdalas) and walnut (Juglans regia) oils significantly improves fertility outcomes in both male and female Wistar rats by counteracting oxidative stress and restoring reproductive hormone levels. In males, the oil mixture substantially enhanced sperm parameters and restored luteinizing hormone, follicular stimulating hormone, and plasma testosterone while boosting antioxidant enzymes superoxide dismutase and glutathione peroxidase. In females, the treatment promoted enhanced folliculogenesis and restored hormonal balance in animals with polycystic ovary syndrome induced by estradiol valerate. The therapeutic effects are attributed to the high polyunsaturated fatty acid content—particularly docosahexaenoic acid—and micronutrients like zinc and vitamins that reduce oxidative stress, a key pathogenic factor underlying both male infertility and female reproductive disorders including PCOS and endometriosis. These findings suggest that almond and walnut oil supplementation may represent a promising natural therapeutic option for treating male and female infertility associated with hormonal imbalances and oxidative damage.

title: The relevance of female overweight in infertility treatment: a position statement of the Italian Society of Fertility and Sterility and Reproductive Medicine (SIFES-MR)
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary:
# Summary

Female obesity is strongly associated with infertility through multiple interconnected mechanisms affecting both ovarian and endometrial function. Obesity triggers hormonal imbalances including insulin resistance, hyperinsulinemia, elevated androgens, and increased leptin, which collectively cause hypothalamic dysfunction, aberrant gonadotropin secretion, and reduced folliculogenesis, ultimately leading to anovulation and ovulatory dysfunction. At the oocyte level, obesity impairs egg quality through mitochondrial dysfunction, oxidative stress, and altered adipokine signaling within the ovarian follicular environment, resulting in increased cycle cancellation rates and abnormal oocyte morphology. Obesity also compromises endometrial receptivity by altering gene expression patterns, reducing leukemia inhibitory factor, and elevating endometrial haptoglobin expression, which contributes to higher miscarriage rates and reduced live birth outcomes even with euploid embryos. Women with elevated BMI require higher FSH dosages due to gonadotropin resistance and demonstrate reduced ovarian sensitivity, though GnRH antagonist protocols offer superior outcomes. Notably, even metabolically healthy obesity associates with reproductive dysfunction, and the combined effects of advanced maternal age with overweight further increase reproductive risks, highlighting the clinical importance of weight management in fertility treatment.

title: Exploring the Factors Associated with Attrition in a Lifestyle Intervention Trial amongst Women with Polycystic Ovarian Syndrome Desiring Fertility - An Interview based Study
summary: Polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS) affects fertility and metabolic health, particularly in obese women, where obesity is present in approximately 50% of PCOS cases and contributes to adverse metabolic, psychological, and reproductive outcomes. The condition manifests through irregular menstrual cycles, excessive hair growth, and reduced conception ability. Lifestyle interventions targeting diet modification and physical activity represent first-line treatment for PCOS, with weight loss demonstrating beneficial effects on hormonal parameters and reproductive outcomes. This study examined factors contributing to participant attrition in a lifestyle intervention trial among women with PCOS seeking fertility, recognizing that understanding dropout patterns is critical for improving intervention efficacy. The findings underscore the importance of designing sustainable lifestyle programs that address both the physiological improvements and participant retention challenges in managing PCOS-related infertility.

title: Cytogenetic Evaluation and Clinical Correlation: A Retrospective Analysis of East Indian Patients with Diverse Amenorrhea Profiles
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cytogenetic analysis of East Indian patients with amenorrhea identified multiple chromosomal and endocrine mechanisms underlying menstrual disorders. X chromosome structure and genes are critical for normal ovarian development and reproduction, with X monosomy (Turner syndrome) being the most prevalent chromosomal abnormality in primary amenorrhea cases, often presenting with streak ovaries and hypoplastic uterus. Both primary and secondary amenorrhea arise from diverse etiologies including hormonal imbalances (elevated testosterone, endocrine dysfunction), chromosomal abnormalities (numerical alterations predominating in primary amenorrhea, structural aberrations in oligomenorrhea), and conditions such as PCOS, endometriosis, primary ovarian insufficiency, and hypothalamic-pituitary dysfunction. Specific X-linked pathologies including Xq deletions causing gonadal dysgenesis, critical region deletions affecting genes like XPNPEP2, and X-autosome translocations with breakpoints in essential regions were identified as contributing factors. The study emphasizes that karyotyping and chromosomal microarray analysis are essential diagnostic tools for detecting both microscopic and submicroscopic chromosomal abnormalities in infertile women with amenorrhea, enabling early intervention and management.

title: Cost-Effectiveness of Interventions Related to the Treatment of Women With Polycystic Ovary Syndrome: A Scoping Review
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome is a multifaceted endocrine disorder strongly associated with excess body weight, which leads to type 2 diabetes, and causes reproductive dysfunction including menstrual irregularities, hirsutism, and infertility through anovulation. The condition also increases pregnancy-related complications such as gestational diabetes mellitus, hypertensive disorders, and preeclampsia.
