Treatment approaches are varied and stratified by disease severity: lifestyle modifications including weight loss serve as first-line therapy to improve reproductive, metabolic, and psychological health, while pharmacological interventions for infertility include letrozole for ovulation induction, clomiphene citrate (the lowest-cost option despite associations with anxiety), gonadotropins, and advanced assisted reproductive techniques such as in vitro fertilization for resistant cases. Metformin improves glucose regulation and represents cost-effective treatment for gestational diabetes prevention in PCOS patients, while surgical interventions like laparoscopic ovarian drilling reduce PCOS symptoms in select populations.

title: Unveiling Therapeutic Potential of Yoga Mitigating Oxidative Stress and Mitochondrial Dysfunction in PCOS: A Randomized Controlled Trial
summary:
# Summary

This randomized controlled trial demonstrates that yoga provides significant therapeutic benefits for polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS) by addressing oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction, two key pathogenic mechanisms in the condition. Oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction both contribute substantially to PCOS pathogenesis, with environmental factors including sedentary lifestyle, air pollutants, endocrine-disrupting chemicals, and high BMI acting as major triggers. Dysfunctional mitochondria produce reactive oxygen species that perpetuate oxidative damage to mitochondrial and nuclear DNA, telomeres, and germ cells, while simultaneously impairing critical ovarian functions including steroidogenesis and follicle development, thereby driving ovulatory dysfunction and infertility. The yoga intervention reversed these pathological processes, significantly increasing mitochondrial DNA copy number and upregulating transcripts responsible for mitochondrial integrity and respiratory chain function, while simultaneously reducing lipid peroxidation, oxidative stress markers, inflammatory cytokines, and telomere shortening. Yoga also improved hormonal balance and reduced depression symptoms, highlighting its efficacy as an adjunct therapy that addresses both the metabolic and psychological complications associated with PCOS.

title: Impact of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome on intracytoplasmic sperm injection outcomes in poly-cystic ovarian syndrome women: A cross-sectional study
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study examines how ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS) affects intracytoplasmic sperm injection outcomes in women with polycystic ovarian syndrome, a condition characterized by endocrine, metabolic, and reproductive dysfunction. Controlled ovarian hyperstimulation in assisted reproductive technology increases oocyte retrieval and initially improves embryo quality; however, it paradoxically reduces oocyte maturity, embryo quality, endometrial receptivity, and pregnancy outcomes while simultaneously increasing OHSS risk. PCOS women face particular vulnerability to OHSS due to elevated antral follicle counts, and OHSS development is associated with high estradiol levels and increased fluid extravasation driven by vascular endothelial growth factor signaling. Notably, women who developed OHSS demonstrated significantly higher estradiol levels on trigger day, shorter stimulation duration, and greater numbers of retrieved oocytes and mature MII oocytes, suggesting that an exaggerated ovarian response, while initially producing more gametes, creates serious systemic complications including pulmonary embolism, deep vein thrombosis, and acute renal failure in severe cases.

title: Autoantibodies Targeting the Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Ovarian Axis in Polycystic Ovary Syndrome: Emerging Key Players in Pathogenesis?
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome is a prevalent endocrinopathy characterized by reproductive abnormalities, metabolic dysfunction, hyperandrogenism, and irregular ovulation, frequently accompanied by obesity, insulin resistance, and increased cardiovascular and autoimmune disease risk. Emerging evidence suggests that autoantibodies targeting hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis components play a previously underappreciated role in PCOS pathogenesis. The condition involves complex dysregulation of the gonadotropin axis, where elevated luteinizing hormone and reduced follicle-stimulating hormone disrupt normal follicle development, exacerbated by insulin resistance and hyperinsulinemia that amplify androgen production and reduce sex hormone-binding globulin. Multiple genetic variants (LHCGR, FSHR, INSR, THADA, HMGA2) and environmental factors—including endocrine-disrupting chemicals, smoking, and fetal androgen exposure—contribute to pathogenesis through epigenetic and developmental mechanisms. The resulting hormonal imbalance, characterized by elevated testosterone and anti-Müllerian hormone with progesterone deficiency, creates a self-perpetuating cycle where elevated estrogen and reduced progesterone modulate immune responses, potentially promoting the autoimmune phenomena increasingly recognized in PCOS pathophysiology.

title: The Particularities of Arterial Hypertension in Female Sex: From Pathophysiology to Therapeutic Management
summary:
# Summary

This article examines the sex-specific pathophysiology of arterial hypertension in women, emphasizing the critical role of estrogen and progesterone in cardiovascular regulation. Estrogens exert potent vasoprotective effects by increasing nitric oxide bioavailability, stimulating adenosine and prostacyclin synthesis, suppressing the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system, and reducing sympathetic nervous system activity, while estrogen depletion leads to endothelial dysfunction, oxidative stress, and impaired sodium handling. Multiple reproductive and gynecological conditions increase hypertension risk in women: irregular menstrual cycles and premenstrual syndrome elevate blood pressure and arterial stiffness; pregnancy-associated insulin resistance and weight gain enhance sympathetic activity and vascular resistance; and gestational hypertension and preeclampsia predict long-term cardiovascular disease. Additionally, polycystic ovary syndrome contributes to hypertension through hyperandrogenism, hyperinsulinemia, sympathetic activation, and RAAS activation, while endometriosis and uterine fibroids are independently linked to elevated hypertension risk through systemic inflammatory pathways. These findings highlight that hormonal alterations across the female lifespan—from menarche through menopause—fundamentally shape blood pressure regulation and cardiovascular risk in women.

title: GnRH-a use before programmed frozen embryo transfer cycles for women with PCOS: a retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study investigated whether gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonist (GnRH-a) pretreatment improves outcomes in women with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) undergoing programmed frozen embryo transfer. GnRH-a pretreatment was associated with significantly higher live birth rates, clinical pregnancy rates, and singleton live birth rates, while reducing miscarriage rates in patients who received both treatment protocols. The proposed mechanism involves GnRH-a suppression of the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis and enhancement of endometrial receptivity, counteracting the adverse effects of elevated estrogen and luteinizing hormone levels in PCOS that impair embryonic development and endometrial function. However, when analyzing the entire PCOS population, GnRH-a pretreatment did not significantly alter overall pregnancy or perinatal outcomes compared to non-GnRH-a protocols, suggesting the benefit may be limited to specific subgroups such as women who failed to achieve live birth in their first cycle. These findings support selective use of GnRH-a pretreatment in programmed FET cycles for carefully selected PCOS patients while emphasizing that endometrial thickness and receptivity may contribute to improved reproductive success.

title: Assessment of Eating Disorders Among Patients with Polycystic Ovary Syndrome Presenting to a Specialty Hospital in Eastern India: A Cross-Sectional Study
journal: Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology of India
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study examined the prevalence of eating disorders in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) patients and found significantly elevated rates compared to the general population, with binge eating disorder affecting 52% of PCOS patients, followed by bulimia nervosa (14%) and anorexia nervosa (2%). The pathophysiology appears multifactorial: insulin resistance in PCOS drives carbohydrate cravings, elevated androgens promote overeating, and body dissatisfaction with self-appearance exacerbates eating disordered behaviors, while eating disorders themselves may perpetuate hormonal imbalances through altered insulin secretion. Eating disorders in PCOS patients carry serious reproductive consequences, including infertility, recurrent miscarriage, and unplanned pregnancy complications. Notably, weight loss interventions—though beneficial for improving fertility and reducing hirsutism—paradoxically risk triggering or worsening eating disorders through weight-related stigma. The study highlights that cognitive behavioral therapy combined with lifestyle modifications proved more effective than lifestyle changes alone, suggesting integrated psychiatric and metabolic management is essential for optimizing outcomes in this complex patient population.

title: Amaranthus spinosus Linn. Extract as an Innovative Strategy to Regulate Biomarkers for Ovarian Hyperthecosis via Circular RNA (hsa‐circ‐0001577): Evidence From Biochemical, Metabolomics, Histological, and Phytochemical Profiling
summary:
# Summary

This study investigated Amaranthus spinosus extract as a therapeutic intervention for ovarian hyperthecosis, a condition characterized by excessive androgen production and related to polycystic ovary syndrome and endometriosis. The researchers induced ovarian hyperthecosis in rats using dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA), which elevated circulating luteinizing hormone and testosterone while reducing follicle-stimulating hormone and triggering oxidative stress markers (reduced glutathione and elevated malondialdehyde). Treatment with the ethanol extract of Amaranthus spinosus dose-dependently reversed these pathological changes by downregulating the dysregulated circular RNA hsa-circ-0001577, which is implicated in granulosa cell function and folliculogenesis. Metabolomic analysis revealed that DHEA-induced hyperthecosis altered tryptophan, nicotinate, and arginine metabolism pathways; Amaranthus spinosus treatment normalized these metabolic disturbances while reducing elevated metabolites like docosahexaenoic acid. The extract's therapeutic effects were attributed to its rich phytochemical content, including triterpenoid saponins, phenolic acids (vanillic acid, protocatechuic acid), and flavonoids with potent antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. These findings suggest that Amaranthus spinosus represents a novel plant-based strategy for managing hyperandrogenic ovarian disorders through epigenetic and metabolic regulation.

title: Exploring the mechanism of action of aspirin in improving endometrial receptivity in PCOS rats based on uterine lavage fluid metabolomics
summary: This study demonstrates that low-dose aspirin improves endometrial receptivity in a letrozole-induced PCOS rat model through multiple integrated mechanisms. PCOS impairs endometrial structure and function by reducing hormone levels (estradiol and progesterone), decreasing endometrial thickness, glands, blood vessels, and pinopodes, ultimately compromising blastocyst implantation. Aspirin reverses these pathological changes by enhancing glucose metabolism, reducing insulin resistance, promoting angiogenesis, and improving vascular permeability while simultaneously restoring hormone levels and endometrial architecture. Metabolomic analysis identified five biomarkers (2,6-dihydroxypurine, gluconolactone, Oxaceprol, and two phosphatidylcholines) associated with improved endometrial receptivity, with aspirin's therapeutic effects mediated through modulation of the pentose phosphate pathway, arginine and proline metabolism, and glycerophospholipid metabolism. These findings support aspirin as a therapeutic intervention to enhance endometrial receptivity and fertility outcomes in women with PCOS, addressing both the metabolic dysfunction and endometrial impairment characteristic of this condition.

title: The emerging roles of N6-methyladenosine (m6A) deregulation in polycystic ovary syndrome
summary:
# Summary

N6-methyladenosine (m6A), the most prevalent mRNA epigenetic modification in eukaryotes, plays a critical role in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) by regulating reproductive development and metabolic homeostasis. Key m6A-modifying enzymes—including METTL3, METTL14, WTAP, KIAA1429, and FTO—control essential reproductive processes such as follicle development, oocyte maturation, and dominant follicle selection; dysregulation of these enzymes in PCOS disrupts ovulation and contributes to the syndrome's characteristic features of hyperandrogenemia, insulin resistance, obesity, and type 2 diabetes. METTL3 upregulation in PCOS promotes granulosa cell inflammation and pyroptosis through m6A-mediated enhancement of FOSL2, NLRP3, and inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α), while elevated WTAP stabilizes ASC mRNA to activate the NLRP3 inflammasome and drive caspase-1-dependent pyroptosis. Additionally, elevated FTO in PCOS associates with granulosa cell dysfunction and obesity, whereas reduced KIAA1429 impairs meiosis resumption and folliculogenesis. These findings establish m6A deregulation as a central epigenetic mechanism linking reproductive dysfunction and metabolic abnormalities in PCOS, with potential therapeutic implications for anti-inflammatory interventions such as butyric acid.

title: Therapeutic Potential of Lythrum salicaria L. Ethanol Extract in Experimental Rat Models of Streptozotocin-Induced Diabetes Mellitus and Letrozole-Induced Polycystic Ovary Syndrome
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluated the therapeutic potential of Lythrum salicaria L. ethanol extract (LSEE) in treating streptozotocin-induced diabetes mellitus and letrozole-induced polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) in rat models, two conditions linked by shared pathophysiology centered on oxidative stress, inflammation, and insulin resistance. LSEE, rich in polyphenolic compounds including ellagitannins and flavonoid glycosides, reduced oxidative stress by lowering oxidant levels and enhancing antioxidant defenses, while simultaneously suppressing pro-inflammatory pathways that drive both disease states. In PCOS rats specifically, LSEE decreased hormonal dysregulation by reducing testosterone, luteinizing hormone, and follicle-stimulating hormone levels, while increasing estrogen and lowering insulin—key metabolic improvements that address the hyperandrogenism and insulin resistance characteristic of the syndrome. The extract also improved metabolic parameters including blood glucose levels, lipid profiles, and liver injury markers in diabetic animals, with efficacy comparable to standard treatments metformin and trolox. These findings suggest that LSEE's polyphenolic compounds address the interconnected pathogenic mechanisms of diabetes and PCOS through potent antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activity, offering potential therapeutic value for managing these metabolically linked disorders.

title: The Relationship Between Obesity, Bariatric Surgery, and Infertility: A Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary

Obesity impairs female fertility through multiple interconnected mechanisms involving the hypothalamic–pituitary–ovarian axis, metabolic dysfunction, and chronic inflammation. Excess adipose tissue produces elevated estrogen levels that suppress FSH production and disrupt HPO axis feedback, while obesity-induced insulin resistance promotes androgen synthesis and reduces sex hormone-binding globulin, leading to hyperandrogenism and ovulatory failure characteristic of PCOS. Additionally, adipose tissue secretes inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, CRP) that impair follicular development, increase oxidative stress, and damage oocytes, while elevated inflammatory markers correlate with implantation failure and early pregnancy loss. Bariatric surgery effectively reverses obesity-related infertility in over 75% of cases by restoring insulin sensitivity, normalizing reproductive hormone levels, and restoring ovulatory function and menstrual regularity in previously anovulatory women. Post-surgical pregnancies demonstrate significantly improved outcomes including reduced miscarriage rates, decreased gestational diabetes, preeclampsia, and preterm delivery, though early conception carries risks of nutritional deficiencies and small-for-gestational-age infants. This systematic review demonstrates that bariatric surgery represents a highly effective treatment for obesity-associated infertility, particularly in women with PCOS and metabolic syndrome.

title: A cohort study on the association between metabolic/inflammatory status and pregnancy complications in PCOS patients after IVF/ICSI treatment
summary: This cohort study demonstrates that polycystic ovary syndrome is characterized by pronounced metabolic and inflammatory dysregulation, including insulin resistance, glucose metabolism impairment, lipid disorders, and chronic low-grade inflammation that collectively predispose to pregnancy complications. Among PCOS patients undergoing IVF/ICSI treatment, insulin resistance and its worsening during pregnancy significantly increase the risk of gestational diabetes, hypertension, and placental dysfunction. The PCOS-IVF/ICSI cohort exhibited substantially elevated metabolic markers including fasting glucose, insulin levels, and HOMA-IR index, alongside markedly elevated inflammatory cytokines (C-reactive protein, IL-2, IL-4, IL-6, IL-8, IL-12, IL-18) compared to naturally conceived pregnancies. These metabolic and inflammatory perturbations correlated with significantly higher rates of miscarriage, pregnancy complications, and reduced neonatal outcomes including lower birth weight and Apgar scores in PCOS patients. These findings highlight how the interplay between insulin resistance and chronic inflammation in PCOS creates an adverse intrauterine environment that compromises pregnancy outcomes even after successful assisted reproductive techniques.

title: Pharmacological and Non-Pharmacological Interventions for Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS) in Indian Women: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is a complex endocrine disorder characterized by chronic anovulation and hyperandrogenism, with significant associated comorbidities including obesity, dyslipidemia, diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and psychological conditions such as stress, depression, and anxiety. Pharmacological interventions target multiple pathways: metformin acts as an insulin sensitizer by activating glucose transporters, thereby reducing insulin levels and subsequently decreasing ovarian androgen production, which alleviates symptoms including hirsutism, acne, and irregular menstrual cycles while also improving metabolic parameters (blood glucose, cholesterol, triglycerides, and LDL levels). For menstrual irregularity, oral contraceptive pills and anti-androgens such as cyproterone acetate and drospirenone are commonly employed, while letrozole and clomiphene citrate effectively induce ovulation in infertile PCOS patients, with letrozole demonstrating superior pregnancy rates. The meta-analysis emphasizes that non-pharmacological interventions including lifestyle modification, dietary changes, physical activity, exercise, yoga, and herbal remedies represent the first-line management, often combined with pharmacological approaches for optimal treatment outcomes in Indian PCOS populations.

title: Proteomic analysis of human follicular fluid based on the 4D label free method to identify proteins that may affect oocyte quality in hyperandrogenic PCOS patients
summary: This proteomic study identifies key follicular fluid proteins that mediate the negative effects of hyperandrogenism on oocyte quality in PCOS patients. Using label-free mass spectrometry, researchers found that hyperandrogenic PCOS patients exhibit significantly elevated IGFBP5 and LAMP2 levels alongside reduced CDH5 levels compared to non-hyperandrogenic PCOS and control groups. Notably, elevated IGFBP5 and LAMP2 negatively correlate with multiple markers of embryo quality, including normal cleavage rates, high-quality embryo formation, and blastocyst development, while increased CDH5 positively correlates with these same outcomes. The dysregulation of these proteins appears mechanistically linked to broader PCOS pathology, including enhanced autophagy via LAMP2, dysregulated insulin-like growth factor signaling through IGFBP5, and impaired vascular cell adhesion through reduced CDH5. These findings suggest that IGFBP5, LAMP2, and CDH5 are candidate biomarkers for oocyte quality in hyperandrogenic PCOS and potential therapeutic targets to improve reproductive outcomes in this population.

title: Predictive value of serum sortilin, HMGB1, and galanin-like peptide for gestational diabetes mellitus in women with polycystic ovary syndrome
summary:
# Summary

Women with polycystic ovary syndrome face significantly elevated gestational diabetes mellitus risk through interconnected metabolic and inflammatory pathways. Three serum biomarkers—sortilin, high-mobility group box 1 (HMGB1), and galanin-like peptide (GALP)—emerged as predictors of GDM development in PCOS patients. HMGB1, a proinflammatory mediator, exacerbates insulin resistance by activating the TLR4/NF-κB pathway and impairing insulin signaling while inducing pancreatic β-cell dysfunction and placental inflammation. Sortilin, a multifunctional regulator of glucose homeostasis and lipid metabolism, shows elevated expression in insulin resistance states and may contribute to GDM pathogenesis through glucose metabolism abnormalities. GALP, a neuroendocrine peptide regulating energy metabolism and gonadotropin secretion, enhances insulin sensitivity, and its reduction in obese PCOS patients promotes chronic inflammation and lipid dysregulation. A predictive panel integrating all three biomarkers achieved an AUC of 0.84 for first-trimester GDM risk stratification in PCOS women, enabling early intervention before pregnancy-induced hormonal changes further compromise insulin sensitivity.

title: CNOT6L regulates energy metabolism in the ovarian granulosa cells associated with polycystic ovary syndrome
journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies CNOT6L as a key regulator of energy metabolism dysfunction in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS). CNOT6L is significantly upregulated in ovarian granulosa cells from both PCOS patients and animal models, where its overexpression shifts cellular metabolism away from glycolysis toward mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation, resulting in reduced production of lactate and pyruvate—critical energy substrates normally supplied to developing oocytes. The metabolic shift causes decreased ATP levels, impaired cell proliferation, reduced estrogen synthesis, and ultimately compromised oocyte quality and follicle development. CNOT6L expression is activated by testosterone and correlates negatively with the number of mature oocytes, suggesting a pathogenic role in PCOS-associated infertility. These findings provide mechanistic insight into how excessive CNOT6L activity mediates the metabolic disturbances and reproductive dysfunction characteristic of PCOS, potentially offering new therapeutic targets for this metabolic endocrine disorder.

title: The relationship between insulin resistance and recurrent pregnancy loss in assisted reproductive technology: A retrospective case-control study
summary: This retrospective case-control study demonstrates that insulin resistance (IR) is an independent risk factor for recurrent pregnancy loss (RPL) in assisted reproductive technology patients, with significantly elevated fasting insulin, 1-hour insulin, 2-hour insulin, HOMA-IR, and HOMA-β values in the RPL group compared to controls. The pathophysiological mechanisms linking IR to pregnancy failure involve hyperinsulinemia's direct impairment of endometrial function and implantation, coupled with IR-induced oxidative stress, mitochondrial damage, and chronic inflammation that collectively compromise oocyte quality and decidualization—the critical endometrial process necessary for successful pregnancy. IR commonly co-occurs with obesity, polycystic ovary syndrome, and type 2 diabetes, all conditions that similarly elevate miscarriage risk through chronic inflammatory and metabolic dysregulation. Elevated insulin levels in the reproductive microenvironment promote inflammatory pathways and reactive oxygen species production, which further impair oocyte developmental potential and create a hostile endometrial environment. The authors identify metformin, an insulin sensitizer, as a promising pretreatment intervention that reduces IR and improves ART outcomes by lowering miscarriage rates. These findings emphasize the clinical importance of screening for and treating insulin resistance prior to assisted reproduction in at-risk populations.

title: Assessing the impact of PCSK9 and HMGCR inhibitor on reproductive endocrine diseases: A drug-target Mendelian randomization analysis
summary:
# Summary

This Mendelian randomization study reveals that lipid-lowering therapies targeting the mevalonate-cholesterol synthesis pathway offer protective effects against reproductive endocrine diseases, particularly PCOS and endometriosis. HMGCR inhibitors (statins) demonstrate a protective effect against PCOS development through multiple mechanisms, including reduction of ovarian androgen synthesis, improvement of insulin sensitivity, and suppression of inflammation and dyslipidemia. PCSK9 inhibitors reduce endometriosis risk by lowering LDL cholesterol and potentially suppressing endometrial cell proliferation. The study establishes that dyslipidemia and elevated triglycerides drive both PCOS pathogenesis (intertwined with insulin resistance and obesity) and endometriosis progression through chronic macrophage activation, endothelial dysfunction, and immune cell-mediated inflammatory responses that increase prostaglandin and leukotriene secretion. These findings suggest that lipid metabolism represents a common mechanistic link between PCOS, endometriosis, and female infertility, supporting the use of cholesterol-lowering agents as adjunctive therapies for reproductive endocrine diseases alongside conventional treatments.

title: Exploring the potential association and experimental validation of disrupted circadian rhythms with polycystic ovary syndrome via meta-analysis and bioinformatics: a possible pathogenic mechanism
summary:
# Summary

Disrupted circadian rhythms are closely associated with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) pathogenesis, involving dysregulation of core clock genes including CLOCK, BMAL1, and NPAS2, which are significantly reduced in PCOS patients and animal models. The normal circadian feedback loop—wherein CLOCK/BMAL1 heterodimers activate the transcription of Period and Cryptochrome genes, which then inhibit their own transcription—becomes imbalanced in PCOS, with elevated negative feedback genes (CRY, REV, PER2) and reduced positive regulators. This circadian disruption contributes to characteristic PCOS features including elevated testosterone and luteinizing hormone levels, irregular estrous cycles, and sleep disturbances. Quercetin treatment effectively reverses circadian core oscillator dysfunction by upregulating BMAL1 and NPAS2 while downregulating inhibitory genes, simultaneously improving metabolic parameters (fasting glucose, insulin, HOMA-IR), reducing hyperandrogenism, and enhancing ovarian follicle development through reduced oxidative stress. These findings suggest that circadian rhythm disruption represents a fundamental pathogenic mechanism in PCOS, with restoration of normal clock gene expression offering therapeutic potential for managing both reproductive and metabolic dysfunction in affected patients.

title: Impact of obesity on proteomic profiles of follicular fluid-derived small extracellular vesicles: A comparison between PCOS and non-PCOS women
summary:
# Summary

This proteomic study reveals that obesity significantly exacerbates inflammatory and metabolic disturbances in PCOS patients through alterations in follicular fluid small extracellular vesicles (sEVs). Overweight and obese PCOS women exhibited elevated sEV protein concentrations alongside 180 upregulated and 256 downregulated proteins compared to lean PCOS women, with particular prominence of S100 Calcium-binding Protein A8 associated with obesity-driven inflammation. In contrast, non-PCOS women demonstrated protective metabolic reprogramming and anti-inflammatory responses, including elevated Heat Shock Protein 90 Beta Family Member 1, highlighting a differential biological response to obesity between groups. Obesity simultaneously impairs oocyte development and reproductive outcomes by increasing inflammation and oxidative stress in the follicular microenvironment, contributing to reduced embryo quality and poor IVF/ICSI results. These findings establish sEV proteomic signatures as biomarkers of obesity-related reproductive dysfunction and suggest that obesity-induced alterations in follicular sEV composition compromise oocyte competence through dysregulated hormonal and metabolic signaling pathways.

title: Molecular variants of multiple genes were revealed by whole-exome sequencing in PCOS patients with diabetes
summary:
# Summary

This study employed whole-exome sequencing to identify molecular variants in PCOS patients with comorbid diabetes, revealing a complex genetic architecture centered on androgen receptor (AR) dysregulation. Mutations in the AR gene, particularly CAG repeat expansions, were associated with both insulin resistance and abnormal follicular development, with AR transcriptional activity inversely correlated with CAG repeat number. The protein kinase CK2A emerges as a critical regulator, phosphorylating and stabilizing AR to increase expression of both androgen synthesis and ovulation-related genes, thereby promoting the hyperandrogenic and anovulatory phenotypes characteristic of PCOS. Additionally, altered expression of serine/arginine-rich splicing factors (SRSFs), which are regulated by microRNAs, disrupts AR alternative splicing and impairs AR function, leading to androgen accumulation in ovarian tissue. Clinical management with insulin sensitizers (metformin), alpha-glucosidase inhibitors (acarbose), and GLP-1 receptor agonists successfully ameliorated hyperglycemia and restored menstrual function in treated patients. These findings suggest that PCOS pathogenesis in diabetic patients involves interconnected genetic and epigenetic mechanisms affecting AR signaling, with implications for understanding the metabolic and reproductive complications of the disorder.

title: A Comprehensive Case Report on Managing PCOS-Related Infertility: Successful Pregnancy Achieved via Controlled Ovarian Stimulation and Intracytoplasmic Sperm Injection (ICSI)
summary: This case report demonstrates successful management of polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS)-related infertility through a multimodal approach combining assisted reproductive technologies with supportive interventions. PCOS, an endocrine disorder characterized by hormonal abnormalities including elevated luteinizing hormone, follicle-stimulating hormone, testosterone, and anti-Mullerian hormone levels, causes infertility through multiple mechanisms including insulin resistance, hyperandrogenism, and abnormal gonadotropin-releasing hormone pulsatility that collectively impair ovulation and oocyte competence. The therapeutic strategy employed controlled ovarian stimulation with gonadotropins, intracytoplasmic sperm injection for fertilization, and frozen embryo transfer with progesterone support, all of which successfully reversed infertility and achieved pregnancy. Adjunctive treatments including dietary modification, exercise, and supplementation with coenzyme Q10, dehydroepiandrosterone, melatonin, alfacalcidol, and astaxanthin addressed the underlying pathophysiology by enhancing insulin sensitivity and suppressing androgen production. This integrated approach illustrates that PCOS-related infertility can be effectively treated through synchronized management of the endocrine dysfunction, metabolic derangements, and reproductive complications characteristic of the syndrome.

title: Exploring Intraovarian Platelet-Rich Plasma (PRP) Therapy: A New Hope for Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS) Patients
summary:
# Summary

This article examines intraovarian platelet-rich plasma (PRP) injection as a therapeutic intervention for polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), which causes reduced ovarian reserve, poor egg quality, and infertility through mechanisms involving hyperandrogenism-induced insulin resistance and oxidative stress. Intraovarian PRP therapy works by delivering multiple growth factors—including vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), basic fibroblast growth factor (bFGF), epidermal growth factor (EGF), and transforming growth factor-β—that activate dormant oocytes and enhance ovarian function. Clinical evidence demonstrates that PRP instillation significantly improves antral follicle count (AFC), anti-müllerian hormone (AMH) levels, oocyte maturation, and egg quality, while also increasing endometrial thickness and implantation rates in PCOS patients. The study additionally highlights that myo-inositol supplementation enhances ovarian function, reduces hyperandrogenism symptoms, and improves fertility outcomes through its role in glucose metabolism. This combined approach of intraovarian PRP and inositol therapy represents a promising treatment strategy for PCOS-related infertility and poor ovarian response, with reported improvements in clinical pregnancy rates.

title: Environmental exposures and polycystic ovary syndrome: a review
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome is characterized by oligomenorrhea, androgen excess, and polycystic ovary morphology, with dysfunction of the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian (HPO) axis playing a central role in disrupting the normal pulsatile release of gonadotropin-releasing hormone, which stimulates follicle-stimulating hormone and luteinizing hormone production and subsequently impairs ovarian sex steroid secretion. PCOS is tightly linked to insulin resistance and metabolic dysfunction, leading to obesity, type 2 diabetes mellitus, dyslipidemia, hypertension, and increased cardiovascular disease risk, while menstrual cycle irregularity itself associates independently with these same metabolic complications. Environmental exposures including air pollutants (particulate matter 2.5, nitrogen dioxide, sulfur dioxide, and carbon monoxide), active and maternal smoking, and emerging exposures such as microplastics have been identified as significant contributors to PCOS development and menstrual cycle disruption. These environmental factors represent an important and expanding research area that may explain the etiology of menstrual irregularities and PCOS beyond traditional genetic and lifestyle factors, with dose-dependent responses observed for maternal smoking during pregnancy on offspring PCOS risk. The review demonstrates that PCOS pathogenesis involves complex interactions between HPO axis dysfunction, insulin resistance, and environmental exposures, with implications for infertility, metabolic syndrome, and long-term cardiovascular complications.

title: Brain‐Specific Gata4 Downregulation in Greywick Female Mice Models the Metabolic Subtype of Polycystic Ovary Syndrome
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that brain-specific downregulation of GATA4, a transcription factor previously associated with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) in human genetic studies, models the metabolic subtype of PCOS in Greywick female mice. Decreased Gata4 expression in hypothalamic neurons and glia leads to misexpression of genes controlling fertility and food intake, revealing GATA4's central role in reproductive regulation. The affected Greywick mice exhibit key PCOS hallmarks including subfertility, oligoanovulation, follicular cysts, atretic follicles, and elevated androgens and luteinizing hormone, while heterozygous and male mice remain unaffected, indicating a female-specific, gene-dose-dependent mechanism. Notably, brain-derived Gata4 dysfunction occurs independently of ovarian GATA4 expression, distinguishing the hypothalamic contribution to PCOS pathogenesis from peripheral ovarian effects. These findings highlight how central nervous system dysregulation of GATA4 can precipitate the reproductive and metabolic abnormalities characteristic of PCOS, providing mechanistic insight into a previously underappreciated genetic component of the disorder.

title: The role of lncRNA HUPCOS in androgen metabolism and follicle growth arrest in polycystic ovary syndrome
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies long non-coding RNA HUPCOS as a key regulator of hyperandrogenism and follicle arrest in polycystic ovary syndrome. HUPCOS is significantly elevated in PCOS patients and positively correlates with elevated testosterone and reduced estradiol levels. Mechanistically, HUPCOS promotes ubiquitination and degradation of the RNA-binding protein RBPMS, which normally interacts with SMAD4 to activate CYP19A1 transcription—the gene encoding aromatase, the enzyme that converts androgens to estrogens. By disrupting the RBPMS-SMAD4 axis, HUPCOS suppresses aromatase expression and estradiol production while promoting androstenedione accumulation, thereby driving hyperandrogenemia. In vivo, HUPCOS overexpression in mice recapitulates PCOS-like symptoms including estrous cycle disturbances, hormonal imbalances, and reduced CYP19A1 and SMAD4 expression in ovaries. These findings establish the HUPCOS-RBPMS-SMAD4 regulatory axis as a critical mechanism underlying PCOS pathogenesis and identify HUPCOS as a potential therapeutic target for treating hormonal imbalance and restoring follicle maturation.

title: Single-cell profiling of the human endometrium in polycystic ovary syndrome
summary: This single-cell profiling study reveals that polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) impairs endometrial function through unopposed estrogen stimulation and progesterone resistance, which together impair decidualization, alter immune cell function, and dysregulate critical genes including SEMA3E, NEAT1, and PAEP that collectively reduce endometrial receptivity and increase miscarriage and endometrial cancer risk. The PCOS-affected endometrium also exhibits dysfunction in integrin-mediated adhesion and extracellular matrix organization pathways, implicating alterations in genes like ITGA2, ROBO2, and MDK that are linked to implantation failure and pregnancy complications. Both metformin and lifestyle management substantially reverse PCOS-driven endometrial pathology by restoring estrogen receptor signaling, alleviating insulin resistance and hyperandrogenism, and normalizing expression of integrin adhesion molecules and ovulation cycle pathway genes including ESR1, ROBO2, MDK, and MAP2K6. These findings demonstrate that the PCOS endometrial phenotype is partially reversible through pharmacological and behavioral interventions, providing mechanistic rationale for treating PCOS-related infertility and pregnancy complications by targeting endometrial dysfunction directly.

title: Phosphorylation-stabilized CRTC1 cooperates with CBP and androgen receptor to transactivate AMH expression and drive polycystic ovary syndrome
summary: This study identifies a novel molecular mechanism driving polycystic ovary syndrome through phosphorylation-stabilized CRTC1. The researchers demonstrate that HIPK2-mediated phosphorylation of CRTC1 at Ser36 prevents its ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation, allowing CRTC1 to interact with the histone acetyltransferase CBP and androgen receptor to form a transcriptional complex that upregulates anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) expression. When HIPK2 is inhibited, CRTC1 becomes unstable and is degraded by the E3 ubiquitin ligase RNF121, leading to dissociation of the CRTC1-CBP-AR complex and reduced AMH expression. Notably, HIPK2 inhibitors effectively suppressed AMH levels and attenuated PCOS symptoms in a DHEA-induced mouse model, suggesting a therapeutic target. The elevated AMH characteristic of PCOS then perpetuates a pathological cycle by inhibiting follicle-stimulating hormone-mediated follicular recruitment and enhancing androgen biosynthesis through CYP17A1 upregulation, effects further amplified by hyperinsulinemia. These findings provide mechanistic insight into how CRTC1 stabilization drives PCOS pathogenesis and identify HIPK2 inhibition as a potential therapeutic strategy.

title: Glycolytic Dysfunction in Granulosa Cells and Its Contribution to Metabolic Dysfunction in Polycystic Ovary Syndrome
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome causes severe glycolytic dysfunction in granulosa cells through dysregulation of energy-sensing pathways including AMPK and mTOR, which reduce glucose uptake and ATP generation while enhancing oxidative stress and mitochondrial impairment. This disrupted glycolysis directly impairs follicular development and compromises oocyte quality, contributing to the infertility and ovulatory dysfunction characteristic of PCOS. The metabolic disturbance is compounded by elevated androgen levels that suppress estrogen synthesis, interfere with FSH receptor expression, and impair gap junction communication through reduced Cx43 expression, while inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-1β, TNFα) accumulate in follicular fluid to promote granulosa cell apoptosis. Therapeutically, agents including metformin, resveratrol, mogroside V, and nicotinamide mononucleotide restore glycolytic balance and improve insulin sensitivity and ovarian function, offering potential interventions to address the metabolic and reproductive dysfunction underlying PCOS pathogenesis.

title: Combined Multiomics Analysis Reveals the Role of
summary:
# Summary

This multiomics study identifies key epigenetic and molecular mechanisms underlying polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) using a prenatal androgenization (PNA) mouse model combined with human patient samples. The research reveals that PCOS pathology involves dysregulation of multiple epigenetic layers—DNA methylation, histone acetylation, and non-coding RNAs—with promoter hypomethylation activating metabolic and lipid catabolic pathways while promoter hypermethylation silences insulin signaling genes. Two novel PCOS biomarkers emerged: ANXA1, which is hypomethylated and elevated in PCOS and suppresses DNA methylation, and LDLR, which is downregulated by the upregulated microRNA has-miR-106a-5p and contributes to metabolic dysfunction and hyperandrogenemia. The study demonstrates that PCOS-associated insulin resistance, ovulatory dysfunction, and elevated androgen levels stem from aberrant epigenetic regulation of steroidogenic and metabolic pathways, with therapeutic options including pharmacological interventions (metformin, statins, clomiphene, letrozole) and minimally invasive surgical approaches. These findings underscore the epigenetic basis of PCOS and provide potential diagnostic and therapeutic targets for managing this complex endocrine disorder.

title: Harnessing Microbiome, Bacterial Extracellular Vesicle, and Artificial Intelligence for Polycystic Ovary Syndrome Diagnosis and Management
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome involves a complex interplay between gut dysbiosis, bacterial extracellular vesicles (BEVs), and metabolic dysfunction. Gut dysbiosis in PCOS patients is characterized by reduced microbial diversity and altered bacterial composition—including elevated Fusobacterium, Ruminococcus gnavus, and Bacteroidaceae, alongside reduced beneficial species like Faecalibacterium prausnitzii and Bifidobacterium—which correlates strongly with elevated testosterone and insulin resistance. BEVs mediate the gut-ovary axis by carrying pro-inflammatory miRNAs and molecules that trigger immune responses and chronic inflammation, while also modulating glucose and lipid metabolism; notably, BEV composition differs in endometriosis, suggesting shared pathophysiological mechanisms across reproductive disorders. Increased intestinal permeability markers (zonulin and diamine oxidase) in PCOS patients indicate compromised gut barrier function contributing to systemic inflammation and metabolic dysfunction. Artificial intelligence shows promise for improving PCOS diagnosis and management by integrating microbiome and BEV data with clinical parameters. Overall, targeting gut dysbiosis and modulating BEV-mediated immune responses represent emerging therapeutic strategies for PCOS and associated comorbidities including type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease.

title: Association of relative fat mass with female infertility: a cross-sectional study based on NHANES 2017–2020
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study using NHANES data demonstrates that relative fat mass (RFM), a superior measure of visceral fat distribution compared to BMI, is significantly associated with female infertility, with risk increasing progressively across RFM quartiles. Obesity and excessive visceral fat impair reproductive capacity through multiple interconnected mechanisms, including insulin resistance, chronic inflammation, and hormonal imbalances that disrupt ovarian function and promote ovulatory disorders. The findings underscore how visceral fat accumulation interferes with fertility pathways implicated in common conditions such as polycystic ovary syndrome and endometriosis, while psychological factors including stress, anxiety, and depression further compound infertility risk. RFM's superior predictive value for both metabolic disorders and infertility suggests it offers a more clinically relevant anthropometric measure than traditional BMI for identifying women at reproductive risk. The study represents the first examination of RFM and female infertility association, highlighting body fat distribution as a modifiable target for fertility preservation and reproductive health interventions.

title: Investigation of the Relationship Between Clinical Features and the
summary: This study investigates the role of systemic inflammation in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) pathophysiology and its relationship to clinical and morphological features. Women with PCOS demonstrated significantly elevated inflammatory markers including delta neutrophil index, neutrophil/lymphocyte ratio, and platelet/lymphocyte ratio compared to controls, indicating chronic low-grade inflammation contributes to PCOS development. PCOS patients also exhibited characteristic morphological changes including increased ovarian volume, elevated ovarian stromal area ratios, and higher antral follicle counts. The inflammatory markers showed correlations with specific clinical manifestations, particularly acne, suggesting inflammation directly influences certain PCOS symptoms while also promoting metabolic and cardiovascular complications through widespread systemic effects. These findings establish inflammation as a central mechanism in PCOS pathogenesis and suggest that anti-inflammatory therapeutic approaches may be beneficial for managing this syndrome.

title: Primary versus secondary infertility: Epidemiology and characteristics from a referral hospital in Saudi Arabia
summary: This hospital-based study characterizes the epidemiological differences between primary and secondary infertility, identifying distinct etiological patterns in each group. Primary infertility is predominantly associated with irregular menstrual cycles and combined multifactorial causes including ovulatory dysfunction and male factors, with PCOS being significantly more prevalent in this population compared to secondary infertility. Secondary infertility, conversely, is associated with advanced maternal age, previous surgical history including caesarean sections, and unexplained causes or uterine abnormalities. Among the broader infertility population, PCOS (19.2%), hypothyroidism (16%), leiomyomas (10.67%), diabetes mellitus (9.6%), and hyperprolactinemia (8.27%) emerge as the five most common comorbid conditions. Fallopian tube obstruction represents a major cause of infertility, with endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, sexually transmitted diseases, and prior abdominal/pelvic surgery implicated as contributing mechanisms. The study underscores that infertility etiology is multifactorial and age-dependent, with marriage duration also influencing the prevalence of specific causes.

title: Polycystic ovary syndrome and excessive body weight impact independently and synergically on fertility treatment outcomes
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) and obesity independently and synergistically impair female reproductive function through distinct but overlapping mechanisms. PCOS is characterized by elevated AMH levels, increased LH:FSH ratios, hyperandrogenism, and oligo-anovulation, while obesity primarily drives insulin resistance and reduced SHBG levels, leading to exacerbated androgen bioavailability. Both conditions independently reduce fertility treatment outcomes, with higher BMI associated with poor ovarian stimulation response and reduced implantation rates. The combination of PCOS with obesity produces particularly detrimental effects on reproductive hormones and follicular function, as evidenced by even lower follicular fluid progesterone levels in obese PCOS women compared to normal-weight PCOS women. While PCOS patients produced more oocytes and cumulus complexes during IVF stimulation, this numerical advantage did not translate to improved fertilization or blastocyst development rates. Weight loss emerges as a critical intervention strategy, as it ameliorates PCOS symptoms, whereas weight gain exacerbates the condition and its reproductive consequences.

title: Obstructive sleep apnea as a predictive indicator for in vitro fertilization and embryo transfer outcomes in patients with polycystic ovary syndrome: a prospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This prospective cohort study demonstrates that obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) significantly impairs reproductive outcomes in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) patients undergoing in vitro fertilization. Among PCOS patients with concurrent OSA, clinical pregnancy rates and live birth rates were substantially reduced compared to PCOS patients without OSA, with OSA independently predicting diminished fertility outcomes. The metabolic dysregulation associated with PCOS-OSA overlap—including elevated insulin resistance, higher HbA1c and HOMA-IR levels, increased triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, and metabolic syndrome—correlates with reduced ovarian reserve (lower anti-Müllerian hormone levels), fewer oocytes retrieved, and reduced fertilization rates. Additionally, PCOS-OSA patients required higher gonadotropin doses and longer stimulation periods for fertility treatment. These findings establish OSA as an independent predictor of poor IVF outcomes in PCOS patients and suggest that the metabolic and endocrine disturbances common to both conditions contribute mechanistically to reproductive failure, including associations with preterm labor and miscarriage.

title: Association between mitochondrial SIRTs (SIRT3, SIRT4, and SIRT5) and PCOS
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that mitochondrial sirtuins SIRT3 and SIRT5 are significantly downregulated in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) patients across multiple cell types including peripheral blood mononuclear cells, granulosa cells, and follicular fluid. SIRT3 and SIRT5 function as critical regulators of mitochondrial metabolism, oxidative stress homeostasis, and ATP production; their reduced expression in PCOS directly correlates with impaired antioxidant capacity (decreased catalase and TFAM) and metabolic dysfunction characterized by elevated fasting insulin and insulin resistance (HOMA-IR), particularly in hyperandrogenic and insulin-resistant PCOS subgroups. Higher SIRT3 expression inversely correlates with age, testosterone, and insulin resistance markers, while SIRT5 promotes glycolytic metabolism through GAPDH activation and PDC/PKM2 inhibition while simultaneously protecting against oxidative stress. The findings suggest that diminished mitochondrial sirtuin activity contributes to the pathogenesis of PCOS through impaired mitochondrial function and exacerbated oxidative stress, implicating mitochondrial biosynthesis enhancement as a potential therapeutic strategy to ameliorate PCOS-associated metabolic and hormonal abnormalities.

title: Late bedtime was associated with increased androgen and reduced lean mass in women with polycystic ovary syndrome: a cross-sectional study
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study demonstrates that late bedtime (after midnight) in women with polycystic ovary syndrome is independently associated with elevated androgens, including increased total testosterone and androstenedione, alongside reduced lean muscle mass. Late bedtime correlates with impaired sleep quality metrics including prolonged sleep latency, short sleep duration, and increased daytime dysfunction, establishing a mechanistic link between circadian misalignment and metabolic dysregulation. The elevated androgenic hormones consequent to late bedtime drive characteristic PCOS manifestations including hirsutism, acne, and androgenetic alopecia, while reduced lean mass impairs insulin-dependent glucose uptake and contributes to cardiometabolic dysregulation. Notably, the intermediate bedtime group showed elevated anti-Müllerian hormone, and women with late bedtime exhibited fewer annual menstrual cycles, indicating sleep timing influences reproductive endocrine function. These findings underscore that sleep-related factors significantly regulate sex hormone production and skeletal muscle maintenance, suggesting that sleep hygiene and circadian alignment represent modifiable therapeutic targets in PCOS management to mitigate hormonal imbalance and metabolic complications.

title: Proinflammatory Cytokines in Women with PCOS in Atypical Pathogen Infections
summary:
# Summary

Women with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) exhibit a markedly elevated susceptibility to atypical reproductive tract infections, particularly *Chlamydia trachomatis* (17.7% infection rate), *Ureaplasma* species (10%), and *Mycoplasma hominis* (4.9%), driven by dysbiosis and immune dysregulation associated with the disorder. PCOS-related microbiome dysbiosis, characterized by elimination of protective *Lactobacillus* species and chronic low-grade inflammation, creates conditions favoring colonization by these pathogenic organisms. These atypical pathogens activate robust proinflammatory cytokine responses, with *C. trachomatis* infection producing particularly elevated levels of TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6, which exacerbate tissue damage in the reproductive tract including fallopian tube obstruction and fibrosis. The proinflammatory cytokines TNF-α and IL-6 directly modulate ovarian steroidogenesis, further compounding the androgen excess and ovarian dysfunction characteristic of PCOS, thereby creating a pathogenic cycle that increases infertility risk. This integrated mechanism reveals how PCOS-associated immune dysregulation and microbiome dysfunction synergistically increase susceptibility to atypical pathogen infections, which amplify systemic inflammation and reproductive tract damage, ultimately contributing to reduced fertility in affected women.

title: Connecting the Dots: Mitochondrial Dysfunction, PCOS, and Insulin Resistance—Insights and Therapeutic Advances
summary:
# Summary

Mitochondrial dysfunction represents a central pathogenic mechanism in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), linking metabolic and reproductive abnormalities through interconnected cellular pathways. Insulin resistance, frequently observed in PCOS, promotes hyperinsulinemia and elevated blood glucose levels that generate reactive oxygen species and oxidative stress, damaging mitochondrial DNA and impairing mitochondrial function. This mitochondrial deterioration exacerbates insulin resistance through accumulation of incompletely oxidized lipid metabolites such as diacylglycerol and ceramide, which suppress insulin signaling by activating protein kinase C and inhibiting the insulin receptor pathway. Simultaneously, hyperinsulinemia directly stimulates androgen synthesis and luteinizing hormone function, driving the elevated androgen production characteristic of PCOS, while reduced sex hormone-binding globulin levels further promote hyperandrogenism and metabolic complications including obesity, type 2 diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. The article emphasizes that restoration of mitochondrial homeostasis through activation of PGC-1α and its downstream effectors—which enhance mitochondrial biogenesis, oxidative capacity, and quality control via fusion-fission dynamics and mitophagy—represents a promising therapeutic strategy to interrupt the PCOS pathogenic cycle. Emerging approaches including mitochondrial transfer show potential to improve reproductive outcomes in obese and insulin-resistant women with PCOS.

title: Morbidity Pattern in Young Unmarried Women Attending Gynecology Clinics at King Abdulaziz Medical City in Riyadh, Saudi Arabia
summary: This study examined gynecological morbidity patterns in young unmarried women in Saudi Arabia, with PCOS emerging as the most prevalent endocrine disorder identified. Menstrual irregularities including irregular cycles, amenorrhea, and menorrhagia were the primary presenting complaints and were strongly associated with PCOS diagnosis. Body mass index and obesity showed significant associations with PCOS and exacerbated its clinical features such as hirsutism, implicating insulin resistance in the pathophysiology of the condition. Beyond menstrual dysfunction, PCOS was identified as a leading cause of both infertility and metabolic dysfunction in the studied population. Oral contraceptive pills emerged as the established first-line treatment for non-fertility-seeking PCOS patients, effectively reducing symptoms including hirsutism and acne. The findings underscore the importance of screening for metabolic and reproductive complications in young women with PCOS, particularly those with elevated BMI.

title: Ameliorative effects of Sesamum Indicum aqueous extract on Letrozole-induced polycystic ovary syndrome in adult female rats and formulation of sesame syrup
journal: Journal of Traditional and Complementary Medicine
summary: This study investigated the therapeutic potential of *Sesamum indicum* (sesame) aqueous extract in treating letrozole-induced polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) in female rats. Letrozole successfully induced PCOS by disrupting hormonal balance, causing irregular estrous cycles, elevated luteinizing hormone (LH) levels, and increased testosterone while suppressing estradiol and progesterone. Sesame extract treatment comparable to metformin significantly ameliorated PCOS pathology by restoring estrous cyclicity, reducing LH levels and the LH/FSH ratio, decreasing testosterone, and normalizing estradiol and progesterone levels. Histological analysis confirmed that sesame extract restored normal follicular development while reducing cystic follicles, demonstrating reversal of ovarian damage. The therapeutic effects were attributed to phytoestrogens and phytosterols (β-sitosterol, campesterol, stigmasterol, and sesamin) present in sesame, which likely counteracted the oxidative stress and altered steroidogenesis underlying PCOS pathogenesis. These findings suggest that sesame-based formulations could serve as a promising natural therapeutic option for PCOS management with metabolic and reproductive benefits.

title: Comparison of the effects of laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy and endoscopic intragastric balloon placement on hormone profile and menstrual cycle in obese women with polycystic ovary syndrome
summary:
# Summary

Obesity and polycystic ovary syndrome frequently coexist through shared pathophysiological mechanisms including insulin resistance and chronic inflammation, with obesity-driven hormonal dysregulation promoting hyperinsulinemia and excessive androgen production that manifests as menstrual irregularities, hirsutism, and acne. Both laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy and endoscopic intragastric balloon placement improve hormonal profiles and metabolic parameters in obese women with PCOS, though through different mechanisms: LSG more effectively modulates adipokine secretion and gut hormone signaling (including leptin, adiponectin, and glucagon-like peptide-1) to restore hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis function and enhance insulin sensitivity. Both interventions significantly reduced free androgen index and total testosterone while increasing sex hormone-binding globulin levels, improvements that correlated with restoration of menstrual regularity and reduced clinical hyperandrogenism. Notably, LSG produced more pronounced decreases in anti-Müllerian hormone levels and affected ovarian function more substantially than the less invasive balloon approach, suggesting greater modulation of follicular dynamics and ovarian reserve. These findings demonstrate that weight loss and metabolic improvement through bariatric or endoscopic interventions can interrupt the obesity-PCOS-insulin resistance cycle and ameliorate reproductive dysfunction in this patient population.

title: Beneficial Effects of Alchemilla vulgaris in DHEA-Induced Rat Model of Polycystic Ovary Syndrome
summary: Alchemilla vulgaris extract (AVE) effectively treats polycystic ovary syndrome in a DHEA-induced rat model by addressing multiple pathophysiological mechanisms underlying the disease. PCOS is characterized by ovarian dysfunction, insulin resistance, hyperandrogenism, low-grade chronic inflammation, and vascular endothelial dysfunction, which AVE mitigates through its anti-inflammatory and vasculoprotective properties. Treatment with AVE normalized hormonal imbalances by reducing elevated DHT and AMH while restoring estradiol levels, reversed metabolic dysfunction by decreasing insulin, leptin, and cholesterol, and improved vascular function by restoring endothelium-dependent relaxation and reducing TNF-α levels in both aortic and ovarian tissues. At the ovarian level, AVE enhanced folliculogenesis by reducing atretic follicles and increasing Graafian follicles and corpus luteum, while also normalizing urinary protein elevation associated with PCOS-related renal dysfunction. These findings support the therapeutic potential of Alchemilla species, traditionally used for gynecological conditions, as a phytotherapeutic agent targeting the interconnected metabolic, hormonal, and vascular pathologies of PCOS.

title: Mass Spectrometry-Based Untargeted Metabolomics Identifies Distinct Metabolic Signatures in Infertility: A Comparative Analysis of PCOS, POR, and NOR
summary: This mass spectrometry-based metabolomic study identified distinct metabolic signatures differentiating polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) and poor ovarian response (POR), two conditions representing opposite ends of the ovarian function spectrum. PCOS is characterized by hyperandrogenism, ovulatory dysfunction, insulin resistance, hyperinsulinemia, and chronic inflammation that impair oocyte quality, while POR presents with inadequate gonadotropin response and reduced ovarian reserve markers including low antral follicle count and decreased anti-Müllerian hormone levels. Both conditions feature alterations in the follicular microenvironment but show markedly different metabolic profiles: PCOS exhibits elevated N,N-dimethylglycine, argininosuccinate, and trehalose-6-phosphate (disrupting purine metabolism and glycolysis), whereas POR shows increased taurocholate, mycalemide, and minocycline with decreased methylated compounds (reflecting alterations in lipid metabolism, amino acid profiles, and steroid biosynthesis). Key metabolites including N,N-dimethylglycine, taurocholate, and mycalemide demonstrated clinically significant discriminatory power (AUC >0.71) between PCOS and POR groups. Pathway analysis revealed that progesterone-mediated oocyte maturation, oocyte meiosis, and prolactin signaling underlie the metabolic differences between these conditions, and hormone-metabolite correlations suggest that altered vitamin biosynthesis and L-methionine metabolism contribute to ovarian dysfunction in both pathologies.

title: Impact of CYP2A6 genetic polymorphism on letrozole efficacy in Iraqi women with polycystic ovary syndrome
journal: Journal of Taibah University Medical Sciences
summary: This study investigated how CYP2A6 genetic polymorphism affects letrozole efficacy in Iraqi women with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS). Letrozole, an aromatase inhibitor that blocks estrogen biosynthesis, is used to treat PCOS-related infertility by promoting ovulation in patients who don't respond to clomiphene citrate. The CYP2A6 enzyme mediates over 85% of letrozole's primary hepatic metabolism, and the CYP2A6∗2 (1799T>A) polymorphism was hypothesized to alter drug metabolism and therapeutic response. Letrozole treatment significantly improved ovulation rates (65% overall), increased follicle size and endometrial thickness, and elevated luteinizing hormone and estradiol while reducing prolactin and testosterone levels in women with PCOS. Notably, the CYP2A6∗2 genetic variant showed no significant association with letrozole efficacy or resistance in this Iraqi population, despite the mutation being present in 45.74% of study participants. These findings suggest that while CYP2A6 is critical for letrozole metabolism, this particular genetic polymorphism may not be a reliable predictor of treatment response in PCOS-related infertility.

title: Effect and mechanism of resveratrol against polycystic ovary syndrome: a review
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome is a complex endocrine disorder characterized by hyperandrogenism, ovulation dysfunction, and polycystic ovary morphology, with pathogenesis driven by insulin resistance and hyperandrogenism alongside genetic, metabolic, and endocrine factors. PCOS is associated with significant metabolic comorbidities including obesity, insulin resistance, type 2 diabetes, and hepatic steatosis, which lead to reproductive complications such as menstrual irregularities, anovulatory infertility, and increased endometrial cancer risk. Resveratrol, a natural polyphenolic compound from plants such as grapes and peanuts, improves glucose, lipid, and endocrine metabolism while regulating sex hormones and gonadotropins, with clinical evidence showing that three-month supplementation reduces fasting insulin by 31.8% and improves insulin sensitivity indices. The therapeutic mechanism of resveratrol operates through dual activation of the SIRT1 and AMPK pathways, wherein resveratrol activates SIRT1 which binds to transcription factors FOXO1, FOXO3a, and FOXO4, while simultaneously activating AMPK through LKB1-mediated phosphorylation and phosphodiesterase inhibition. These pathways converge to enhance the PI3K/Akt insulin signaling pathway, thereby ameliorating insulin resistance and normalizing estrous cyclicity and ovarian histomorphology in preclinical models. Collectively, resveratrol represents a promising therapeutic candidate for managing PCOS-associated insulin resistance and metabolic dysfunction through well-characterized molecular mechanisms.

title: Menstrual cycle characteristics across the reproductive lifespan and cognitive function in midlife women
journal: American journal of obstetrics and gynecology
summary: This study demonstrates that menstrual cycle characteristics across the reproductive lifespan serve as markers of hormonal exposure and predict cognitive function in midlife women. Women with irregular cycles during their late reproductive years (29-46 years) or short cycles (≤25 days) in early adulthood (18-22 years) exhibited lower learning and working memory scores in midlife, likely mediated by reduced lifetime estrogen exposure. The research reveals a mechanistic pathway whereby oocyte depletion leads to declining anti-Müllerian hormone levels, shortened cycle length, and eventually irregular menstruation—a pattern indicating accelerated reproductive aging and compromised estrogen exposure. Conditions associated with reproductive dysfunction, particularly polycystic ovary syndrome characterized by hyperandrogenism and irregular cycles, showed associations with poor cognitive performance. The findings suggest that menstrual cycle regularity patterns may identify women at risk for cognitive decline and dementia, conditions that disproportionately affect women, potentially reflecting the cumulative neuroprotective effects of sustained estrogen exposure throughout reproductive life.

title: Effect of L-carnitine supplementation on fertility outcomes among patients with polycystic ovary syndrome: a systematic review and dose-response meta-analysis of randomized clinical trials
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and dose-response meta-analysis examined L-carnitine supplementation as a treatment for polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), a condition characterized by hyperandrogenism, insulin resistance, oxidative stress, and infertility. L-carnitine, a fatty acid metabolism regulator, significantly improved multiple fertility outcomes in PCOS patients, including chemical and clinical pregnancy rates, ovulation rate, menstrual regularity, serum progesterone and estrogen levels, endometrial thickness, and preovulatory follicle development. The mechanism underlying these improvements involves L-carnitine's ability to enhance insulin sensitivity, reduce testosterone and oxidative stress, improve lipid and glucose metabolism, and enhance endometrial blood flow and receptivity. Notably, younger patients (ages 21–30) and obese individuals (BMI >30 kg/m²) showed the greatest benefit in chemical pregnancy rates with L-carnitine treatment. These findings suggest L-carnitine supplementation represents an effective therapeutic strategy for improving fertility outcomes in PCOS patients, particularly in select populations.

title: Exploring Adipokines in Assessing the Role of Dehydroepiandrosterone in Polycystic Ovary Syndrome-Linked Infertility
summary: This study demonstrates that dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA) supplementation improves fertility outcomes in women with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) by rebalancing adipokine levels and reproductive hormones. PCOS is characterized by insulin resistance, elevated androgens, and an unfavorable adipokine profile with elevated leptin and reduced adiponectin, both of which impair ovulation and metabolic function. Twelve weeks of DHEA treatment increased anti-metabolic markers (AMH and estradiol) while simultaneously decreasing leptin and increasing adiponectin, thereby improving adipose tissue function and reducing the LH/FSH ratio. The favorable shift in the leptin-to-adiponectin ratio appears to restore ovarian function and uterine receptivity, as high leptin levels inhibit ovulation while adiponectin promotes follicle growth and estrogen production. These findings suggest DHEA enhances fertility in PCOS through a dual mechanism: directly augmenting ovarian reserve and FSH receptor activity, and indirectly improving insulin sensitivity and metabolic homeostasis through adipokine modulation.

title: Hyperandrogenism-mediated YAP activation drives ovarian inflammation and pyroptosis in PCOS: implications for follicular dysfunction
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that hyperandrogenism in PCOS drives follicular dysfunction through a testosterone-mediated YAP activation pathway that promotes ovarian inflammation and pyroptosis. In DHEA-treated mice (a PCOS model), elevated androgens upregulate nuclear YAP, which in turn activates NF-κB signaling and triggers the NLRP3 inflammasome cascade, leading to increased production of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-18) and pyroptotic cell death in ovarian tissues. This aberrant inflammation and pyroptosis disrupt normal follicular development, resulting in cystic and atretic follicles, increased ovarian weight, and metabolic dysfunction including abdominal fat accumulation. Mechanistically, YAP knockdown in granulosa cells substantially curtailed testosterone-induced inflammation and pyroptosis, confirming YAP as a critical mediator of androgen-driven ovarian pathology. Treatment with verteporfin, a pharmacological YAP inhibitor, successfully reversed DHEA-induced phenotypes including follicular abnormalities and metabolic dysfunction, suggesting that targeting the Hippo-YAP pathway may represent a novel therapeutic strategy for PCOS-related infertility and follicular dysfunction.

title: Sustained Metabolic Improvements with Low-Dose Metformin Combined with Oral Contraceptives in Female Adolescents with PCOS: A Single-Center Retrospective Cohort Study
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that low-dose metformin combined with oral contraceptives effectively treats insulin resistance and hyperandrogenism in adolescents with PCOS by interrupting the pathogenic cycle linking insulin excess to androgen production. Metformin significantly improves insulin sensitivity, reduces fasting glucose and insulin levels, and decreases the HOMA insulin resistance index, while simultaneously lowering testosterone and the free androgen index. The hyperinsulinemia-driven mechanism in PCOS involves insulin directly stimulating androstenedione secretion from ovarian thecal cells, which is further amplified by luteinizing hormone, and this axis is effectively suppressed by metformin therapy. Beyond hormonal improvements, metformin reduced ovarian volume and body mass index while oral contraceptives alone failed to adequately address insulin resistance, highlighting the critical contribution of the insulin-sensitizing agent. These metabolic and clinical improvements in adolescent patients suggest that early intervention with combined metformin and oral contraceptive therapy may provide sustained benefits in preventing long-term PCOS complications including type 2 diabetes and infertility.

title: PCOS endometrium-derived epithelial organoids as a novel model to study endometrial dysfunction
summary: This study developed endometrial epithelial organoids (EEOs) from women with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) to model endometrial dysfunction and identify molecular mechanisms underlying PCOS-associated subfertility and pregnancy complications. PCOS EEOs exhibited hallmark endometrial abnormalities including elevated inflammation with increased expression of adhesion molecules (ICAM1, OSMR) and complement components (C3, C6, CCL2, VNN1), coupled with reduced expression of receptivity-related genes such as leukemia inhibitory factor and progestagen-associated endometrial protein. Additionally, PCOS EEOs displayed impaired steroid hormone responses and reduced mitochondrial gene expression (MT-CO2, MT-RNR1, MT-ND2), which may contribute to impaired decidualization and embryo implantation. When exposed to progesterone mimetics, PCOS EEOs showed aberrant responses characterized by decreased expression of implantation-critical genes (LIF, PAEP) but paradoxically increased expression of adhesion and chemokine molecules (SPP1, CXCL14), suggesting a dysregulated secretory phase phenotype. Notably, androgen signaling appeared dispensable in this model, as direct DHT exposure had minimal effects on gene expression despite elevated androgens characteristic of PCOS. These findings establish EEOs as a valuable platform for dissecting endometrial dysfunction in PCOS and identifying therapeutic targets.

title: Women's fertility and vitamin D: Could hypovitaminosis D biomarkers correlate with the disease, and explain the unexplained female factor infertility?
summary:
# Summary

Vitamin D deficiency is prevalent in reproductive-age women and represents a significant biomarker associated with female infertility, particularly in cases of PCOS and unexplained etiology. The active metabolites of vitamin D—calcidiol and calcitriol—are markedly reduced in infertile patients compared to healthy controls, with calcitriol emerging as the optimal predictor of both infertility status and its underlying causes. Vitamin D regulates critical reproductive processes through its receptor (VDR), which controls over 1000 genes involved in insulin secretion, estrogen production, HOXA10 expression, and anti-müllerian hormone production; VDR knockout studies demonstrate that vitamin D signaling is essential, as its loss causes uterine hypoplasia and anovulation. Vitamin D deficiency is mechanistically linked to PCOS, endometriosis, and reduced ovarian reserve, while supplementation improves fertility outcomes by reducing androgens and inflammation while enhancing sex hormone-binding globulin. These findings suggest that vitamin D status, measured by biomarker levels, could serve as a diagnostic tool to identify and potentially explain cases of otherwise unexplained female infertility, offering a therapeutic target for intervention.

title: Sleep disorders and psychological comorbidities in women with polycystic ovary syndrome – a cross-sectional study
summary: This cross-sectional study demonstrates that women with polycystic ovary syndrome experience a substantial burden of sleep disturbances and psychological comorbidities, with obstructive sleep apnea being the most prevalent sleep disorder reported. The research identifies body mass index and insulin resistance as independent risk factors for obstructive sleep apnea in PCOS patients, with overweight and obese women showing significantly elevated prevalence compared to normal-weight controls. Beyond metabolic complications, PCOS is associated with elevated rates of depression and anxiety, both of which correlate with body mass index and demonstrate significant positive associations with one another. Chronic sleep disturbance emerges as a central pathological feature, independently associated with both anxiety and depression disorders while also increasing the probability of obstructive sleep apnea. The findings suggest that PCOS initiates a cyclical pattern wherein metabolic dysfunction and sleep pathology collectively worsen psychiatric symptoms and perpetuate declining health outcomes. These results underscore the systemic nature of PCOS and highlight the necessity of addressing sleep and psychological health as integral components of comprehensive clinical management.

title: Integrating Bibliometrics and Bioinformatics to Map Knowledge Structure, Trends, and Genetic Insights in Polycystic Ovary Syndrome and Tumors (2015–2024)
summary:
# Summary
