This bibliometric and bioinformatics analysis reveals a substantial overlap between polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) and endometrial cancer, with 250 shared genes identified through integrated genomic approaches. PCOS, characterized by ovulatory dysfunction, hyperandrogenism, and polycystic ovarian morphology, is strongly associated with increased risk of endometrial, breast, and ovarian cancers, with dysregulation of the PI3K/AKT signaling pathway and upregulation of the TP53 tumor suppressor gene implicated in both conditions. Beyond reproductive pathology, the research highlights PCOS as a systemic metabolic and endocrine disorder with significant comorbidities including insulin resistance, type 2 diabetes, obesity, cardiovascular disease, and psychological disturbances. Emerging research hotspots encompass oxidative stress, inflammation, and granulosa cell dysfunction, while temporal trend analysis identifies shifting investigative focus toward anti-müllerian hormone, bone mineral density, and fertility preservation in cancer patients. The integrative findings underscore that understanding PCOS-related malignancy risk requires examining shared molecular pathways and metabolic dysfunction rather than viewing PCOS solely as a reproductive disorder.

title: Adipokine Dysregulation in Obese and Non-Obese Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS) Patients: Association With Visceral Adiposity Index and Metabolic Risk
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that obesity in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) patients is characterized by profound dysregulation of adipokines and severe metabolic dysfunction. Obese PCOS patients exhibited significantly elevated leptin, resistin, and pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β), coupled with markedly reduced adiponectin levels, creating a pro-inflammatory adipokine profile. These adipokine alterations directly correlate with insulin resistance markers, as reflected by increased fasting glucose, insulin, and HOMA-IR, alongside dyslipidemia characterized by elevated triglycerides and reduced HDL cholesterol. Obesity also exacerbates hyperandrogenism and increases systemic inflammation through elevated CRP, oxidized LDL, and MCP-1. Notably, leptin emerged as the strongest adipokine predictor of obesity in PCOS with diagnostic accuracy (AUC=0.85), showing strong positive correlations with BMI, visceral adiposity index, and insulin resistance markers, while adiponectin exhibited inverse associations with metabolic dysfunction. These findings underscore how obesity-driven adipokine dysregulation amplifies the metabolic and inflammatory complications of PCOS, even in non-obese PCOS patients who still display abnormal adipokine profiles.

title: Unravelling the molecular landscape of polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) and role of inflammation through transcriptomics analysis of human ovarian granulosa cells
summary:
# Summary

This transcriptomic analysis of human ovarian granulosa cells reveals that chronic low-grade inflammation is a central pathogenic mechanism in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), mediated through dysregulation of multiple inflammatory genes including SPI1, HSPB1, MNDA, and ITGA, which stimulate lymphocyte and leukocyte activation. The study identifies several key molecular signatures of PCOS: upregulation of androgen-mediated genes (SPI1 and ETS transcription factors) contributing to hyperandrogenism, elevated expression of metabolic genes (TNFRSF1B, SERPINA1) associated with insulin resistance and hyperlipidemia, and activation of cellular pathways including autophagy, endocytosis, SNARE complex assembly, and mast cell activation. Notably, downregulation of SEMA3F impairs GnRH neuron migration while upregulation of Alas2 promotes oxidative stress, apoptosis, and mitochondrial dysfunction in granulosa cells. PCOS demonstrates strong associations with multiple comorbidities including type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, non-alcoholic fatty liver disease, and notably, increased risk of endometriosis and endometrial cancer. Elevated pro-inflammatory markers (C-reactive protein, interleukin-6, tumor necrosis factor-α) and oxidative stress mediators (NF-κB, reactive oxygen species) characterize PCOS pathophysiology, establishing inflammation as a major effector that fuels metabolic complications and reproductive dysfunction.

title: The triglyceride glucose-body mass index predicts adverse reproductive outcomes in women with polycystic ovary syndrome undergoing frozen embryo transfer
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that the triglyceride glucose-body mass index (TyG-BMI), a composite metabolic marker combining triglycerides, fasting glucose, and BMI, independently predicts adverse reproductive outcomes in women with polycystic ovary syndrome undergoing frozen embryo transfer. Elevated TyG-BMI was significantly associated with increased miscarriage risk, decreased live birth rates, and higher incidence of gestational diabetes mellitus and large-for-gestational-age infants, with miscarriage rates rising from 8.9% in the lowest quartile to 27.9% in the highest quartile. These findings reflect the underlying pathophysiology whereby insulin resistance, obesity, and dyslipidemia—prevalent in approximately 75%, 50%, and 70% of PCOS patients respectively—exacerbate hyperandrogenemia, impair oocyte quality, and compromise embryo development. The TyG-BMI marker offers a cost-effective, accessible tool for early identification of high-risk PCOS patients and prediction of pregnancy complications, independent of direct insulin measurements. These results highlight the critical role of metabolic dysfunction in mediating poor reproductive outcomes in PCOS and support TyG-BMI as a valuable clinical biomarker for risk stratification in assisted reproductive technology.

title: Fast food consumption and risk of non-alcoholic fatty liver disease: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates that fast food consumption significantly increases non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) risk by 55%, with obesity and insulin resistance serving as key mechanistic intermediates. Fast food intake, characterized by high saturated fat, refined sugar, and sodium content, promotes NAFLD development through multiple pathological pathways including induction of insulin resistance, visceral adiposity accumulation, and gut microbiota dysbiosis. Refined sugars and trans fats impair insulin signaling, leading to increased hepatic fat accumulation and steatosis, while processed foods alter gut microbial composition, triggering endotoxemia and hepatic inflammation. Obesity itself acts as a critical driver of NAFLD via adipose tissue inflammation and ectopic fat deposition, with approximately 37% elevated obesity risk associated with high fast food consumption. Without intervention, NAFLD can progress insidiously to non-alcoholic steatohepatitis, cirrhosis, liver fibrosis, and hepatocellular carcinoma, representing a global health crisis affecting 25% of the population. These findings underscore the importance of dietary modification to mitigate metabolic risk factors underlying NAFLD pathogenesis.

title: Androgens drive SLC1A5-dependent metabolic reprogramming in polycystic ovary syndrome
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies SLC1A5, a glutamine transporter, as a central driver of polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) pathology through androgen-dependent metabolic reprogramming. Androgen excess activates SLC1A5 expression via androgen receptor binding to its promoter, leading to increased glutamine uptake in granulosa cells. This elevated glutamine flux is converted to α-ketoglutarate, which enhances HDAC5 expression and suppresses histone acetylation, ultimately downregulating genes essential for normal folliculogenesis. SLC1A5 overexpression alone is sufficient to induce PCOS-like phenotypes in mice, including hyperandrogenism, abnormal follicle development, reduced fertility, and meiotic failure. Importantly, pharmacological blockade of SLC1A5 with GPNA reverses these features, improving estrous cyclicity, reducing elevated androgens and anti-Müllerian hormone, and restoring glucose tolerance and insulin sensitivity. These findings establish glutamine metabolism as a key metabolic node linking androgen excess to PCOS pathogenesis and identify SLC1A5 inhibition as a potential therapeutic strategy.

title: Pathogenic variation in insulin resistance genes is common in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS): a strategy for causal gene discovery using whole-exome sequencing (WES) in complex traits
summary:
# Summary

This whole-exome sequencing study identifies pathogenic genetic variation in insulin resistance-related genes as a common feature of polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), establishing insulin resistance as a central mechanism linking genetic predisposition to disease pathology. Protein-altering variants in 40 insulin signaling genes, 12 obesity genes, and 10 dyslipidemia genes were significantly enriched in PCOS cases, with candidate genes including LMNA, LEPR, KCNJ11, ACACA, GCK, ABCC8, and others involved in glucose metabolism, lipid storage, and energy homeostasis. Four insulin signaling genes (CPT1A, PAX4, TH, CD36) achieved exome-wide significance, while LMNA and LEPR reached this threshold among dyslipidemia and obesity genes respectively, suggesting these may be causal loci. PCOS patients exhibited characteristic metabolic abnormalities including elevated testosterone, fasting insulin, LDL cholesterol, and triglycerides alongside reduced SHBG and HDL cholesterol, with over half of cases carrying protein-altering variants in significant genes. These findings support a genetic architecture model where variation in genes controlling insulin signaling and metabolic homeostasis predisposes to PCOS and its associated anovulatory infertility, obesity, dyslipidemia, and type 2 diabetes risk.

title: Identification and Validation of Genes Involved in Energy Metabolism in Polycystic Ovary Syndrome
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies and validates energy metabolism-related genes as critical biomarkers in polycystic ovary syndrome, addressing the incomplete understanding of metabolic dysregulation's role in PCOS pathogenesis. Through transcriptomic analysis of granulosa cells from PCOS and control samples, the researchers identified 159 differentially expressed genes and highlighted three key biomarkers: CD44 (upregulated), and HS2ST1 and GPC4 (both downregulated), which were localized to distinct cellular compartments including the plasma membrane, Golgi apparatus, and nucleus. Functional enrichment analysis revealed that these dysregulated genes participate in critical pathways including Fc gamma R-mediated phagocytosis, Ras signaling, and protein digestion and absorption, while immune cell infiltration patterns showed positive correlation with CD44 but negative correlation with HS2ST1 and GPC4. The dysregulation of energy metabolism pathways in granulosa cells directly implicates impaired energy metabolism in insulin resistance and metabolic disturbances observed in PCOS, connecting granulosa cell dysfunction to the syndrome's ovulatory and metabolic complications. Additionally, drug prediction analysis identified bisphenol A as a compound linked to all three biomarkers, suggesting potential environmental contributions to PCOS pathogenesis. These findings establish CD44, HS2ST1, and GPC4 as robust biomarkers for PCOS and provide mechanistic insights into how energy metabolism dysfunction drives the pathophysiology of this leading cause of female infertility.

title: Optimizing Ovarian Stimulation for IVF in PCOS Patients: A Novel Day 1 GnRH Antagonist Protocol
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that initiating GnRH antagonist treatment on day 1 of ovarian stimulation significantly improves in vitro fertilization outcomes in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) patients. PCOS, the leading cause of anovulation globally, is characterized by elevated luteinizing hormone, insulin resistance, hyperinsulinemia, and intra-ovarian hyperandrogenism that impairs follicle development and oocyte quality. The early GnRH antagonist protocol suppresses excessive LH levels during the early follicular phase, which corrects the ovarian microenvironment and restores follicular sensitivity to FSH. Patients treated with day 1 antagonists achieved significantly higher oocyte yields (11.47 vs. 8.95), superior proportions of top-quality day 3 embryos (77.5% vs. 63.4%), and improved cumulative clinical pregnancy rates (44% vs. 27.9% per embryo transfer) compared to the standard day 5/6 antagonist protocol. These results suggest that early GnRH antagonist administration offers meaningful clinical advantages for PCOS patients by counteracting the pathophysiological abnormalities underlying their reproductive dysfunction.

title: Potential impact of air pollution on polycystic ovary syndrome incidence: a global analysis of 171 countries and regions from 1990 to 2021
summary: This global analysis of 171 countries demonstrates a strong association between air pollution and polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) incidence from 1990 to 2021, with nitrogen dioxide (NO₂) emerging as the most significant pollutant, followed by sulfur dioxide, particulate matter, and ozone. Air pollutants contribute to PCOS pathogenesis by disrupting hormone levels and inducing insulin resistance through inflammatory mechanisms, with NO₂ exposure specifically affecting ovarian function and female reproductive health. The association between NO₂ and PCOS incidence was more pronounced in regions with middle to high economic development and higher sociodemographic indices. Temperature variations and fluctuations further facilitate PCOS occurrence by directly affecting pancreatic function and metabolic responses. Given that PCOS is closely associated with metabolic complications including type 2 diabetes, obesity, and cardiovascular disease, these findings suggest that improvements in air quality could substantially reduce the global disease burden of PCOS and its related comorbidities.

title: Insulin resistance as a determinant of fertilization efficiency in polycystic ovary syndrome patients undergoing IVF/ICSI: a retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

Insulin resistance (IR) emerges as a central pathophysiological determinant affecting 35–80% of polycystic ovary syndrome patients and significantly compromising fertilization efficiency during IVF/ICSI treatment. IR drives multiple reproductive dysfunctions through interconnected mechanisms: it downregulates glucose transporter 4 and sex hormone-binding globulin while upregulating theca cell androgen production and pituitary responsiveness to GnRH, collectively impairing endometrial receptivity and promoting hyperandrogenism-associated ovulatory dysfunction. At the oocyte level, IR-induced metabolic dysregulation disrupts folliculogenic and cytoplasmic maturation, reducing fertilization rates (82.02% vs. 83.86%), two-pronuclei formation rates (81.07% vs. 83.96%), and high-quality embryo rates (36.8% vs. 39.7%) compared to IR-negative patients. IR-positive PCOS patients require substantially higher gonadotropin doses and prolonged stimulation duration while exhibiting elevated miscarriage risk and decreased clinical pregnancy rates in population-level analyses. Notably, IR functions as an independent risk factor for impaired oocyte fertilization even in normal-weight PCOS patients, suggesting its pathogenic effects extend beyond BMI-related metabolic dysfunction. These findings underscore IR as a crucial therapeutic target for optimizing reproductive outcomes in PCOS patients undergoing assisted reproduction.

title: Investigation of the toxic mechanism of aspartame on female infertility using network toxicology and molecular docking approaches
summary:
# Aspartame and Female Infertility: A Network Toxicology Study

This study employs network toxicology and molecular docking to elucidate how aspartame impairs female fertility through multiple mechanistic pathways. Aspartame is metabolized into phenylalanine, aspartic acid, and methanol, with methanol further converting to formaldehyde, and these metabolites directly bind to key reproductive targets including interleukin-1 beta (IL-1β), angiotensin-converting enzymes (ACE and ACE2), and cathepsin S (CTSS). IL-1β emerges as a critical mediator, produced by stromal cells under oxidative stress, where it suppresses ovarian steroid production, induces granulosa cell apoptosis, causes follicular atresia, and impairs oocyte maturation—effects that are elevated in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) patients. ACE and ACE2 disruption dysregulates the renin-angiotensin system and hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis, promoting ovulatory abnormalities and follicular atresia, while reduced CTSS levels diminish antiapoptotic factors (BCL2, PCNA), precipitating follicular degeneration. The IL-17 signaling pathway represents a major inflammatory mechanism through which aspartame promotes chronic inflammation and autoimmune responses characteristic of infertility and PCOS. Collectively, chronic aspartame consumption poses reproductive toxicity risks through overlapping inflammatory and endocrine disruption pathways that converge on oocyte developmental failure and infertility.

title: To explore the relationship between endometrial hyperemia and polycystic ovary syndrome
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals a mechanistic link between polycystic ovary syndrome and chronic endometritis mediated by endometrial hypoxia and endoplasmic reticulum stress. PCOS patients exhibit significantly elevated prevalence of chronic endometritis (44.4% versus 22.3% in controls) and endometrial hyperemia, accompanied by upregulation of hypoxia-response genes including HIF-1α, VEGF, and erythropoietin in endometrial tissue. The hypoxic endometrial microenvironment activates endoplasmic reticulum stress pathways—characterized by increased PERK, ATF4, and CHOP expression—which subsequently trigger a pro-inflammatory cascade through elevated IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α secretion. Chronic endometritis in PCOS patients impairs endometrial receptivity as evidenced by reduced CD138+ plasma cell infiltration and substantially compromises reproductive outcomes, with PCOS patients demonstrating lower clinical pregnancy rates and higher abortion rates. This integrated mechanism demonstrates how PCOS-associated metabolic dysfunction and obesity-driven tissue hypoxia converge on the endometrium to produce chronic inflammation and reproductive failure through endoplasmic reticulum stress signaling.

title: Vascular imbalance in polycystic ovary syndrome: Insights into endothelial dysfunction
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates vascular dysfunction in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) by examining the dysregulation of vasoactive molecules that control vascular tone. PCOS patients demonstrated a critical imbalance in vasodilatory and vasoconstrictive regulators: elevated nitric oxide, apelin, and noradrenaline paired with significantly reduced prostacyclin levels compared to controls. This vascular tone imbalance disrupts endothelial function and contributes to increased cardiovascular risk in PCOS patients. The dysregulation appears multifaceted, with altered nitric oxide signaling implicated in abnormal angiogenesis, elevated apelin associated with follicular arrest and obesity, and increased noradrenaline activity driving sympathetic overactivity and disrupted ovarian function. Given that PCOS is characterized by chronic low-grade inflammation, insulin resistance, and endothelial dysfunction, these vasoactive molecule abnormalities represent a key mechanistic link between PCOS's metabolic and vascular complications, potentially explaining the elevated cardiovascular disease risk in this population.

title: Association of blood progesterone levels on trigger day and low oocyte retrieval in cases with polycystic ovary syndrome undergoing in-vitro fertilization: A prospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This prospective cohort study in PCOS patients undergoing IVF identified the estradiol-to-follicle ratio as a significant predictor of low oocyte retrieval rate (ORR), with a ratio ≥17 mm associated with reduced oocyte yield. Both abnormally high and low progesterone levels on the trigger day contributed to poor outcomes, including fewer embryos and increased cycle cancellations, though progesterone alone was not statistically significant in multivariate analysis. Low progesterone levels reflect inadequate granulosa cell proliferation while high levels promote premature ovulation, both mechanisms reducing oocyte quantity and subsequently compromising live birth rates in PCOS patients. The study demonstrated that patients with low ORR exhibited elevated BMI, antral follicle count, and gonadotropin stimulation duration alongside reduced estradiol-to-follicle ratios, suggesting impaired follicular steroidogenesis. These findings suggest that monitoring the estradiol-to-follicle ratio and maintaining optimal progesterone levels may help identify at-risk PCOS patients, and that interventions such as double trigger protocols warrant consideration to improve oocyte maturation and retrieval outcomes.

title: Mechanisms of c-Fos regulation of mTOR signaling via ERα/β in abnormal lipid metabolism of granulosa cells in PCOS
summary: This study reveals a critical regulatory mechanism linking elevated c-Fos to abnormal lipid metabolism in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) granulosa cells. In PCOS ovarian tissues, c-Fos is significantly upregulated and suppresses both estrogen receptor α and β (ERα/β), which normally inhibit mTOR signaling and lipid accumulation. The c-Fos-mediated downregulation of ERα/β leads to activation of mTOR and its downstream effectors, including SREBP-1C and PPARα, ultimately driving elevated triglyceride synthesis and dyslipidemia characteristic of PCOS. In contrast, 17β-estradiol treatment reverses this pathology by upregulating ERα/β expression, which suppresses mTOR signaling and reduces triglyceride content in granulosa cells. These findings establish the c-Fos/ERα/ERβ/mTOR axis as a central regulatory pathway controlling lipid metabolism in PCOS, and suggest that mTOR inhibition by rapamycin represents a viable therapeutic strategy for preventing anovulatory dysfunction and metabolic abnormalities in this disorder.

title: Fibroblast growth factor 19 regulates polycystic ovary syndrome progression via FGFR4-ERK-NRF2 pathway
journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that fibroblast growth factor 19 (FGF19), which is significantly elevated in PCOS patients and mouse models, drives polycystic ovary syndrome progression through the FGFR4-ERK-NRF2 signaling pathway in ovarian granulosa cells. FGF19 overexpression promotes granulosa cell viability and survival while inhibiting apoptosis by upregulating FGFR4, NRF2, and the antioxidant enzyme HO1, alongside increased ERK phosphorylation and anti-apoptotic BCL2 expression. The pathway protects ovarian cells from oxidative stress by activating the NRF2-mediated antioxidant response, as demonstrated by FGF19's ability to counteract hydrogen peroxide-induced cell death and reactive oxygen species accumulation—effects that depend on intact NRF2 function. These findings establish FGF19 as a key regulator of PCOS pathogenesis linked to oxidative stress and suggest that blocking this pathway with FGFR4 inhibitors may offer therapeutic potential for managing PCOS progression.

title: Molecular Insights into Elevated Autoantibodies in Polycystic Ovary Syndrome: Mechanisms and Clinical Implications
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome is characterized by hyperandrogenism, insulin resistance, anovulation, and irregular menstrual cycles, with women with PCOS demonstrating significantly elevated levels of multiple autoantibodies including anti-TPO, anti-TG, and antinuclear antibodies compared to healthy controls. This autoimmune phenotype reflects underlying chronic low-grade inflammation and immune dysfunction, with dysregulated Treg cells playing a central role in the pathogenic connection between PCOS and autoimmune conditions such as Hashimoto's thyroiditis, type 1 diabetes, rheumatoid arthritis, and Sjögren's syndrome. Anti-TPO antibodies have been detected in ovarian follicular fluid and correlate with elevated estradiol levels and abnormal estradiol-to-progesterone ratios in PCOS patients, suggesting a direct mechanism by which thyroid autoimmunity affects reproductive hormone homeostasis through follicle destruction. Genome-wide and epigenome-wide association studies have identified shared genetic susceptibility loci including FSHR and INSR, along with enrichment of type 1 diabetes and HLA region pathways in both ovarian tissue and whole blood from PCOS patients. These molecular insights demonstrate that PCOS should be understood as a systemic autoimmune-endocrine disorder extending beyond reproductive dysfunction to substantially increase risk for metabolic complications, obstetric complications, and multiple autoimmune diseases.

title: Risk of Abortion and Ectopic Pregnancy in Women with a History of Polycystic Ovary Syndrome: A Nationwide Population-Based Cohort Study
summary: This nationwide cohort study demonstrates that polycystic ovary syndrome significantly increases the risk of adverse pregnancy outcomes, with women with PCOS experiencing 1.47-fold higher abortion risk and 1.85-fold higher ectopic pregnancy risk compared to controls. The underlying pathophysiology involves functional ovarian hyperandrogenism driven by hyperinsulinemia, which causes excess androgen production that leads to premature luteinization and impaired ovulation. PCOS-related insulin resistance suppresses endometrial proteins including glycodelin and IGFBP-1, which are critical for endometrial receptivity and feto-maternal adhesion, thereby compromising early implantation and embryonic development. Elevated oxidative stress in PCOS, correlated with increased BMI and insulin levels, further contributes to pregnancy complications. The study identifies that advancing age exacerbates abortion risk in PCOS patients through increased metabolic burden, independent of obesity status, and notes that current interventions including metformin only partially address these risks. These findings emphasize that PCOS-associated pregnancy complications stem from multiple mechanistic defects in ovulation, endometrial function, and embryonic development rather than obesity alone.

title: The relationship between initial vitamin D levels and in vitro fertilization (IVF) outcomes in PCOS patients: a systematic review
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic Ovary Syndrome, characterized by hyperandrogenism and ovulatory dysfunction, is a common cause of infertility that is frequently associated with vitamin D deficiency. Higher baseline vitamin D levels show a strong positive correlation with improved IVF outcomes, including increased pregnancy and live birth rates, while vitamin D deficiency impairs fertility by reducing ovarian follicle development, compromising embryo quality, and diminishing endometrial receptivity. The active form of vitamin D, 25-hydroxyvitamin D, exerts its effects through vitamin D receptors expressed in the ovaries, endometrium, and placenta, where it promotes granulosa cell proliferation, regulates cell cycle progression through altered gene expression, and facilitates follicle development. Women with vitamin D deficiency require higher gonadotropin doses and produce fewer oocytes, underscoring its critical role in ovarian responsiveness during assisted reproduction. Vitamin D supplementation has been shown to improve chemical pregnancy rates and pregnancy outcomes in PCOS patients, establishing adequate vitamin D status as a protective factor for successful IVF outcomes and live birth, particularly in those with reproductive conditions such as endometriosis.

title: Does Bariatric Surgery Affect Pregnancy in Women in the Childbearing Period? A Retrospective Single Center Study
summary:
# Summary

Obesity impairs reproductive health in women of childbearing age through multiple mechanisms, including insulin resistance, hyperinsulinemia, elevated androgens, and inflammatory cytokine elevation, which collectively disrupt the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis and precipitate menstrual dysfunction, ovulatory disorders, polycystic ovarian syndrome, and infertility. Bariatric surgery effectively treats severe obesity and substantially improves fertility by normalizing hormonal imbalances, restoring ovulation, and regularizing menstrual cycles, with conception rates reaching 28.8% postoperatively. However, rapid postoperative weight loss creates significant risks including micronutrient deficiencies, anemia (affecting 54.9% of pregnant patients), and increased incidence of small-for-gestational-age infants and preterm birth. Pregnancy complications following bariatric surgery are particularly elevated when conception occurs early in the postoperative period and when multidisciplinary follow-up is inadequate, necessitating careful nutritional supplementation and specialized prenatal care. Women with preoperative infertility experienced the highest conception rates (75%) after surgery, suggesting bariatric surgery reverses obesity-related reproductive dysfunction most dramatically in this population.

title: Time To Cycle Regularity and Health Risks
journal: Current opinion in endocrinology, diabetes, and obesity
summary:
# Summary

The normal menstrual cycle depends on a coordinated hormonal cascade in which GnRH stimulates pituitary production of LH and FSH, which then drive ovarian steroid production and ultimately the LH surge that triggers ovulation and corpus luteum formation for progesterone production. Delayed establishment of cycle regularity during adolescence is associated with significantly elevated risk for PCOS and persistent menstrual dysfunction into adulthood, with approximately half of oligomenorrheic adolescents continuing to experience oligomenorrhea as adults. Prolonged time to cycle regularity is linked to multiple cardiometabolic comorbidities including obesity, type 1 and type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular events, likely mediated through anovulatory cycles that promote insulin resistance, elevated androgens, and unopposed estrogen exposure. Adolescents with menstrual irregularities demonstrate metabolic dysregulation including elevated insulin, glucose, triglycerides, and blood pressure by age 15, and irregular menstrual patterns in adolescence are associated with increased risk for endometrial and other hormone-sensitive cancers in adulthood. Demographic factors including ethnicity, age at menarche, body composition, and environmental exposures to endocrine-disrupting chemicals influence cycle regularity, while prolonged menstrual irregularity is independently associated with increased all-cause and premature mortality.

title: Research progress on the pathogenesis, clinical impact, and traditional Chinese medicine treatment of polycystic ovary syndrome complicated by insulin resistance
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome is fundamentally associated with insulin resistance, which is mediated by multiple interconnected mechanisms including hyperandrogenism, chronic low-grade inflammation, and gut microbiota dysbiosis. Hyperandrogenism drives insulin resistance through direct effects on insulin signaling pathways and indirect mechanisms involving circadian clock genes and the NAMPT/NAD/SIRT1 pathway, while genetic polymorphisms in genes such as TOX3, GALNT2, IRS1, and DENND1A contribute to individual susceptibility. Inflammatory cytokines like TNF-α suppress insulin signaling by inhibiting IRS phosphorylation, and dysbiotic microbiota compromise intestinal barrier function, allowing lipopolysaccharide translocation that further amplifies inflammation through NF-κB pathway activation. Treatment approaches include conventional pharmacotherapy with metformin and emerging evidence for traditional Chinese medicine, which exerts therapeutic effects by modulating gut microbiota composition, suppressing inflammatory reactions, and ameliorating hyperandrogenism through multiple signaling pathways. Restoration of beneficial bacteria such as Bifidobacterium longum and modulation of microbial metabolites offer complementary strategies to improve insulin sensitivity and reduce systemic inflammation in PCOS patients with insulin resistance.

title: Alteration of the follicular fluid amino acid profile reveals the important roles of several amino acids in embryo quality in patients with polycystic ovary syndrome
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that polycystic ovary syndrome is characterized by a marked dysregulation of follicular fluid amino acids, with nine amino acids significantly reduced compared to controls—including glutamine, taurine, arginine, histidine, and serine—which collectively associate with diminished embryo quality during IVF. Within PCOS patients, specific amino acids (taurine, aspartic acid, and threonine) are elevated in those producing top-quality embryos, suggesting that despite overall reduction, amino acid levels still modulate oocyte developmental competence. The amino acid deficit in PCOS is mechanistically linked to two pathogenic pathways: insulin resistance impairs amino acid uptake by follicular cells, while hyperandrogenism disrupts mitochondrial function and redox balance. Notably, insulin-resistant PCOS and high-BMI PCOS subgroups exhibit the most severe amino acid depletion, indicating metabolic dysfunction severity correlates with reproductive outcomes. These findings establish amino acid dysregulation as a critical mediator connecting PCOS metabolic abnormalities to impaired oocyte competence and poor embryo quality, with implications for understanding the PCOS-endometriosis metabolic overlap.

title: PTX3 as a key modulator of functional ovarian response in PCOS: evaluation alongside TSG-6 and ITI
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies pentraxin-3 (PTX-3) as a key modulator of ovarian dysfunction in polycystic ovary syndrome, demonstrating that elevated follicular fluid PTX-3 levels—particularly in lean PCOS patients—strongly predict reduced embryo quality and suboptimal follicular output rate (FORT), independent of body mass index. PTX-3 shows strong positive correlations with hyperandrogenism and antral follicle count, yet paradoxically associates inversely with high-quality embryo production and fertilization outcomes. The protein functions as part of an inflammatory complex in the ovarian microenvironment, interacting with TSG-6 and inter-alpha-trypsin inhibitor to maintain cumulus-oocyte integrity, and elevated levels indicate a pronounced local inflammatory milieu linked to oocyte maturation impairment. Multivariate analysis confirmed that follicular PTX-3 is the strongest independent negative predictor of FORT (β = −0.65), surpassing age and BMI, while insulin-resistant PCOS patients show particularly elevated inflammatory markers including PTX-3, TSG-6, and ITI. These findings suggest that PTX-3 serves as a biomarker of ovarian inflammation and compromised oocyte competence in PCOS, offering potential diagnostic and therapeutic targets for understanding how chronic low-grade inflammation disrupts reproductive outcomes in this disorder.

title: Successful Pregnancy in a PCOS Patient with Elevated Basal Luteinizing Hormone and Repeated Ovulation Induction Failures: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome causes chronic anovulation, hyperandrogenism, and infertility through multifactorial mechanisms including insulin resistance and neuroendocrine disturbances characterized by elevated basal luteinizing hormone (bLH) levels. This case report demonstrates that elevated bLH significantly reduces the efficacy of standard ovulation induction protocols, including conventional GnRH agonist and antagonist approaches, leading to poor ovarian response and repeated cycle cancellations in a 26-year-old PCOS patient. The patient achieved successful pregnancy following adoption of a progestin-primed ovarian stimulation (PPOS) protocol combined with letrozole, which circumvented the need for LH suppression and improved ovarian response and embryo development. The PPOS-letrozole strategy proved superior to conventional methods by stimulating endogenous gonadotropin release while avoiding the follicular atresia observed with aggressive LH suppression. This case highlights that PCOS patients with elevated bLH and prior ovulation induction failures may benefit from alternative stimulation protocols that accommodate rather than suppress the elevated LH environment. The patient delivered a healthy infant, demonstrating the clinical efficacy of this individualized treatment approach for managing PCOS-related infertility in the context of neuroendocrine dysfunction.

title: Molecular Signatures of Obesity-Associated Infertility in Polycystic Ovary Syndrome: The Emerging Role of Exosomal microRNAs and Non-Coding RNAs
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome is characterized by polycystic ovarian morphology, hyperandrogenism, and chronic anovulation, with obesity affecting 40–85% of PCOS patients and exacerbating insulin resistance while increasing circulating insulin levels. Exosome-derived microRNAs (ex-miRNAs) emerge as critical mediators of inter-tissue communication, with specific miRNAs (miR-93, miR-320a, miR-103) associated with dysregulated insulin signaling, inflammation, glucose metabolism, steroidogenesis, and folliculogenesis. The pathophysiology involves a cascade where hyperinsulinemia augments androgen synthesis in theca cells via insulin and IGF-1 receptor signaling while suppressing sex hormone-binding globulin, leading to elevated testosterone that causes anovulation and impedes follicular development. At the molecular level, chronic hyperinsulinemia induces serine/threonine phosphorylation of insulin receptor substrate through JNK and IKKβ pathways, while pro-inflammatory M1 macrophages in obesity secrete TNF-α, IL-6, and MCP-1, activating NF-κB signaling and reactive oxygen species production that further inhibits folliculogenesis. Adipose tissue-derived exosomes enriched with dysregulated miRNAs directly affect ovarian granulosa cells and reduce metaphase II oocyte yield, while DNA methylation alterations in key genes (INSR, IRS-1, CYP19A1, FSHR) and circular RNAs functioning as miRNA sponges compound the insulin-resistant and hyperandrogenic environment that impairs folliculogenesis and endometrial receptivity.

title: Probiotics mitigates ovarian angiogenic disturbance in letrozole-induced PCOS
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), a leading cause of female infertility associated with dyslipidemia, obesity, and metabolic dysfunction, involves dysregulated ovarian angiogenesis driven by elevated vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF). In a letrozole-induced PCOS rat model, pathological changes include increased VEGF expression, elevated histone deacetylase 2 (HDAC2), and decreased antioxidant defenses (NrF2, HIF-1a) and mitochondrial fusion protein mitofusin 2 (Mfn2), alongside elevated androgens and inflammatory markers (NF-κB, TNF-α, caspase-6). Probiotic treatment significantly reversed these ovarian metabolic and morphological disturbances by downregulating HDAC2, upregulating Mfn2, and restoring metabolic health through epigenetic modulation of histone acetylation. Probiotics also normalized sex hormone profiles by reducing testosterone and anti-Müllerian hormone while improving sex hormone binding globulin and estradiol levels, thereby improving reproductive function. These findings demonstrate that probiotics mitigate angiogenic dysfunction and metabolic complications in PCOS through gut microbiome-mediated epigenetic and mitochondrial mechanisms.

title: A sustainable public health framework for PCOS management in low- and middle-income countries: a narrative review
summary:
# PCOS Management in Low- and Middle-Income Countries: Key Findings

Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) represents a complex endocrine disorder characterized by hyperandrogenism, ovulatory dysfunction, and polycystic ovarian morphology, with clinical manifestations including oligomenorrhea, anovulation, infertility, and insulin resistance. Beyond reproductive dysfunction, PCOS is significantly associated with metabolic complications such as type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease, as well as substantial psychological burden including anxiety, depression, and body image concerns. Management strategies encompass both pharmacological interventions—metformin to reduce insulin resistance and androgen levels, alongside oral contraceptives and clomiphene citrate—and lifestyle modifications including exercise, dietary changes rich in whole grains and lean proteins, and stress-reduction techniques such as mindfulness and cognitive-behavioral therapy. Early screening using biomarkers such as anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH), inhibin A, and insulin-like peptide 3, combined with diagnostic criteria from Rotterdam Consensus or National Institutes of Health guidelines, is critical for preventing long-term complications including type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and infertility. Public health approaches in resource-limited settings should emphasize school-based education programs to normalize menstrual health discussions, reduce stigma, and promote preventive lifestyle behaviors, while ensuring affordable access to essential medications like metformin.

title: Quercetin modulates the interplay between DNMT gene expression, oxidative stress, and trace elements in DHEA-induced polycystic ovary syndrome rat model
summary: This study demonstrates that quercetin, a bioactive flavonoid, effectively reverses polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) pathology induced by DHEA in rats by targeting interconnected mechanisms involving oxidative stress, epigenetic modifications, and trace element homeostasis. PCOS induction severely compromised antioxidant defenses by reducing activities of key enzymes (glutathione peroxidase, catalase, superoxide dismutase, and glutathione reductase) and depleting protective molecules like glutathione and essential trace elements (zinc, copper, magnesium), while simultaneously elevating oxidative stress markers (malondialdehyde) and iron levels alongside increased DNA methyltransferase (DNMT1 and DNMT3a) expression. Quercetin treatment reversed these pathological changes by upregulating antioxidant enzyme activities by 67-98%, restoring trace element concentrations, and downregulating DNMT3a and oxidative stress markers. The study reveals that reactive oxygen species perpetuate a pathogenic cycle by inducing DNA hypermethylation, disrupting histone modifications, and exacerbating both hyperandrogenism and insulin resistance, while zinc deficiency and iron overload further amplify oxidative damage. These findings establish quercetin as a potential therapeutic agent for PCOS that acts through coordinated restoration of redox balance, epigenetic regulation, and mineral homeostasis.

title: Lipid accumulation product and visceral adiposity index in polycystic ovary syndrome: a systematic review and meta-analysis of observational studies
summary: Polycystic ovary syndrome is characterized by dual reproductive and metabolic dysfunction, with insulin resistance affecting approximately 75% of women regardless of body mass index. Visceral fat accumulation, which is closely associated with insulin resistance, drives chronic low-grade inflammation and worsening insulin sensitivity through a self-perpetuating cycle. Two emerging biomarkers—the lipid accumulation product (LAP) and visceral adiposity index (VAI)—are significantly elevated in PCOS women and effectively capture the metabolic dysfunction underlying the condition, with LAP particularly sensitive in overweight/obese populations and VAI showing elevation across both lean and obese phenotypes. VAI remains consistently elevated across PCOS phenotypes A, B, and D and is associated with insulin resistance, ovulatory dysfunction, androgen excess, and cardiometabolic risk. The insulin resistance and compensatory hyperinsulinemia characteristic of PCOS directly disrupts normal ovarian signaling and, combined with myo-inositol and D-chiro-inositol imbalances, increases androgen production while driving cycle irregularities and infertility. These findings support LAP and VAI as valuable clinical tools for identifying early metabolic abnormalities and cardiometabolic risk in PCOS women.

title: Shared senescence-associated gene networks in PCOS and T2DM: biomarker identification and functional validation
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome and type 2 diabetes mellitus represent interrelated metabolic disorders sharing a common pathogenic mechanism centered on cellular senescence. Cellular senescence drives disease progression in both conditions through the senescence-associated secretory phenotype, which induces chronic low-grade inflammation and oxidative stress that exacerbate insulin resistance, hyperandrogenism, and ovarian dysfunction. Key biomarkers including TUBA4A, RTN1, G6PD, and HP are elevated in peripheral blood of PCOS patients and correlate with distinct immune cell populations, suggesting their potential diagnostic and mechanistic value. These senescence-associated genes participate in inflammatory and metabolic pathways while modulating macrophage and dendritic cell populations, linking cellular aging to systemic immune dysregulation. The convergent pathways involve hyperinsulinemia stimulating luteinizing hormone and androgen secretion, reduced SHBG levels, and dysregulation of incretins (elevated GIP, reduced GLP-1) that collectively perpetuate metabolic dysfunction. These findings establish cellular senescence as a unifying pathogenic framework for PCOS-T2DM overlap and identify senescence-associated genes as potential therapeutic targets for both conditions.

title: Reduced mean platelet volume (MPV) as an inflammatory marker in Chinese women with polycystic ovary syndrome: a case-control study
summary:
# Summary

This case-control study identifies reduced mean platelet volume (MPV) as a novel inflammatory marker associated with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) in Chinese women. PCOS is characterized by chronic low-grade inflammation that contributes to ovarian dysfunction and metabolic disturbances, alongside elevated inflammatory markers including white blood cell counts, lymphocytes, and high-sensitivity CRP, while paradoxically showing decreased MPV and platelet distribution width. Women with PCOS exhibit significant metabolic dysregulation, including hyperandrogenism, insulin resistance, dyslipidemia, and elevated HOMA-IR, with MPV inversely correlated with total testosterone, free testosterone, and total cholesterol levels. The elevated platelet count despite reduced MPV suggests that pro-inflammatory cytokines, particularly interleukin-6, promote thrombocytosis in PCOS by enhancing thrombopoietin production, while dyslipidemia-induced platelet factor secretion mobilizes bone marrow progenitor cells. Decreased MPV was independently associated with PCOS occurrence, indicating this platelet parameter may serve as a practical biomarker for PCOS diagnosis and reflect the underlying inflammatory pathophysiology of the condition.

title: The Effects of
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that Chlorella vulgaris, a nutrient-rich microalga with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, effectively ameliorates polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) in a mouse model through multiple interconnected mechanisms. Chlorella treatment reduces elevated androgens and luteinizing hormone while downregulating steroidogenic genes (STAR, CYP19A1, CYP11A1) that are pathologically upregulated in PCOS, thereby normalizing the hypothalamus-pituitary-ovary axis and reducing cystic follicles. The supplement enhances antioxidant defense by suppressing KEAP1 and upregulating the NRF2 pathway, which increases cellular glutathione (GSH) while decreasing malondialdehyde (MDA) and reactive oxygen species levels. Additionally, Chlorella restores PCOS-associated gut dysbiosis by increasing beneficial bacteria (Rikenella, Parasutterella, Alloprevotella, Dubosiella, Parabacteroides) while reducing pathogenic species, and these microbial changes correlate with improved oxidative stress markers. These findings suggest that Chlorella's therapeutic effects on PCOS operate through coordinated rebalancing of ovarian hormones, activation of antioxidant defense pathways, and restoration of healthy microbiota composition.

title: Antinuclear Antibodies in Polycystic Ovary Syndrome: A Systematic Review of Observational Studies
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), the most common endocrine disorder in reproductive-age women, is characterized by menstrual irregularities, ovulatory dysfunction, androgen excess, and cardiometabolic complications including insulin resistance, obesity, and dyslipidemia. This systematic review identifies a mechanistic pathway wherein insulin resistance, chronic inflammation, and oxidative stress associated with PCOS collectively promote autoimmunity through dysregulated adaptive immunity and accelerated cellular apoptosis, which increases exposure to intracellular antigens. Several autoantibodies are elevated in PCOS patients, with anti-double-stranded DNA antibodies and anti-histone antibodies showing the most consistent elevation across studies, though antinuclear antibody (ANA) positivity varied between cohorts. Elevated ANA and other autoantibodies serve as serological markers suggesting generalized autoimmune activation in PCOS, potentially linking the condition to increased risk of autoimmune manifestations such as thyroid disease, which is already known to be more prevalent in this population. These findings underscore the autoimmune component of PCOS pathophysiology and suggest that immune perturbations may contribute to the reproductive complications, including subfertility, infertility, and early pregnancy loss, observed in affected women.

title: Association of Follicular Distribution Patterns in Polycystic
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome is characterized by menstrual disorders, hyperandrogenism, and infertility, with frequent associations to metabolic complications including diabetes and cardiovascular disease. The study identifies that peripheral cystic pattern follicular distribution represents a more severe PCOS phenotype, characterized by significantly elevated luteinizing hormone, anti-Müllerian hormone, total testosterone, and 17-OHP compared to general cystic pattern distribution. Patients with peripheral cystic pattern demonstrate heightened stromal density and vascular blood flow, which correlate with ovarian hyperandrogenism and clinical severity, and exhibit increased menstrual irregularities, hirsutism, and oocyte retrieval during controlled ovarian stimulation. Notably, peripheral cystic pattern patients show elevated risk for ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome during assisted reproductive procedures. The findings suggest that insulin resistance, follicle number, ovarian volume, and stromal characteristics are interconnected contributors to specific ovarian morphology patterns, supporting the use of follicular distribution patterns as a clinical stratification tool to guide personalized treatment approaches in PCOS management.

title: Serum brain-derived neurotrophic factor, micronutrient status, and inflammatory cytokines in type 2 diabetes with nephropathy: a case–control analysis
summary:
# Summary

This case-control study reveals that serum brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) is markedly reduced in type 2 diabetic patients with nephropathy and serves as a biomarker for diabetic kidney disease. BDNF levels correlate inversely with markers of glycemic control and kidney dysfunction, including HbA1c, fasting plasma glucose, serum creatinine, and urea. The study identifies multiple micronutrient deficiencies—including vitamin D, B12, folate, and zinc—in diabetic nephropathy patients, each contributing to disease pathology through distinct mechanisms: vitamin D and zinc deficiency impair insulin secretion and pancreatic β-cell protection, while B12 and folate deficiency elevate homocysteine causing endothelial dysfunction. A bidirectional inflammatory loop operates in poorly controlled diabetes, wherein elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-6 and IL-12 suppress BDNF expression, while BDNF feedback inhibits these same cytokines, and zinc supplementation can restore circulating BDNF levels. These findings suggest that comprehensive micronutrient repletion and restoration of BDNF signaling may represent therapeutic targets for managing diabetic nephropathy and its associated metabolic and inflammatory complications.

title: Genetic association between microRNA gene polymorphisms and polycystic ovary syndrome susceptibility: A systematic review and meta‐analysis
journal: International Journal of Gynaecology and Obstetrics
summary:
# Summary

This meta-analysis demonstrates that microRNA (miRNA) gene polymorphisms are significantly associated with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) susceptibility. Specifically, the GG genotype of miR-146a rs2910164 provides protection against PCOS by reducing miRNA expression and limiting inflammatory responses, while the C allele increases miR-146a expression in ovarian granulosa cells, promoting apoptosis and defective follicular development. The miR-196a-2 rs11614913 polymorphism similarly influences PCOS risk, with the TT genotype conferring increased susceptibility; the C allele of this polymorphism promotes cellular viability and migration in ovarian cells but is associated with metabolic complications including type 2 diabetes. Elevated miR-146a-5p expression correlates with key PCOS pathogenic features including anovulation, insulin resistance, and hyperandrogenism through TNF-α-mediated inflammatory pathways. Combined genotypes, such as miR-146a CG/miR-196a2 TC, demonstrate protective effects against PCOS-related clinical phenotypes and other reproductive disorders. These findings highlight miRNA polymorphisms as important genetic determinants of PCOS and suggest potential therapeutic targets for managing this prevalent endocrine disorder.

title: Effects of Low Molecular Weight Heparin on Early Pregnancy Loss in Women With Polycystic Ovary Syndrome
summary:
# Summary

Women with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) experience early pregnancy loss (EPL) at rates five times higher than the general population, driven by a prothrombotic state resulting from dyslipidemia, insulin resistance, and metabolic dysfunction. Low molecular weight heparin (LMWH) significantly reduced EPL rates in PCOS patients from 26.5% in controls to 5.3% in treated women, with mechanisms including anticoagulant and antithrombotic activity, regulation of placental microcirculation, and anti-inflammatory effects. PCOS itself—characterized by androgen excess, ovulatory dysfunction, and menstrual disorders—is the leading cause of anovulatory infertility, typically managed with ovulation induction agents like letrozole and clomiphene citrate, alongside metformin for insulin resistance. LMWH treatment during early pregnancy, potentially combined with aspirin, appears to restore pregnancy success in PCOS patients by addressing the underlying prothrombotic pathology that manifests as recurrent pregnancy loss. This study demonstrates that anticoagulation therapy offers a novel therapeutic approach to improve pregnancy outcomes in this high-risk population.

title: Molecular Impact of Metabolic and Endocrine Disturbance on Endometrial Function in Polycystic Ovary Syndrome
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome causes widespread endometrial dysfunction through multiple interconnected mechanisms spanning metabolic, endocrine, and inflammatory pathways. Insulin resistance and hyperinsulinemia activate the PI3K/AKT/MAPK and Wnt/β-catenin signaling pathways, altering endometrial epigenetic profiles, transcriptomics, and metabolomics, while chronic low-grade inflammation drives upregulation of multiple cytokines (IL-1, IL-2, IL-6, IL-8, IL-17, IL-18, and TNF-α) that disrupt local hormone networks and impair estrogen, progesterone, and insulin receptor signaling. These signaling defects compromise cell-to-cell communication and disrupt the finely tuned proliferative and secretory phase hormonal networks essential for normal endometrial function, resulting in altered bleeding patterns, excessive proliferation, and reduced implantation potential in reproductive-age women, impaired decidualization and trophoblast invasion in pregnancy, and elevated endometrial cancer risk in the post-menopausal period. Aberrant sex steroid hormone signaling through dysregulation of CYP19A1, COX-2, PGE2, HOXA10, and 11βHSD2 further promotes deleterious changes in the endometrial microenvironment, establishing PCOS as a systemic disorder with profound consequences for reproductive health across the lifespan.

title: Associations between serum 25– hydroxyvitamin D concentrations and assisted reproductive technology outcomes in women with polycystic ovary syndrome: a cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This cohort study demonstrates that vitamin D status significantly influences assisted reproductive technology (ART) outcomes in women with polycystic ovary syndrome, a condition characterized by high prevalence of vitamin D deficiency. Vitamin D regulates critical reproductive processes including ovarian steroidogenesis, folliculogenesis, granulosa cell proliferation, and endometrial receptivity through widespread vitamin D receptor distribution in female reproductive tissues. Women with serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D levels ≥68 nmol/L achieved substantially higher clinical pregnancy rates (75% vs. 52%), enhanced live birth probabilities, and reduced early miscarriage risks compared to those with deficient levels, with logistic regression confirming vitamin D as an independent predictor after adjusting for hormonal profiles. Vitamin D deficiency in PCOS patients is associated with hyperandrogenism, insulin resistance, oxidative stress, and proinflammatory environments that collectively impair ovulation rates and fertility outcomes. The identification of a 68 nmol/L threshold for vitamin D—characterized by 94.3% specificity for pregnancy prediction—suggests that optimizing vitamin D status prior to embryo transfer represents a modifiable clinical strategy to improve reproductive success in PCOS populations undergoing IVF.

title: Mechanisms and applications of mesenchymal stem cell exosomes in enhancing fertility
summary:
# Summary

Mesenchymal stem cell exosomes (MSC-Exos) represent a promising therapeutic approach for treating multiple forms of infertility affecting both male and female reproductive systems. Infertility arises from diverse etiologies including hormonal imbalances, reproductive disorders, and anatomical abnormalities, with approximately 33-41% attributed to female factors and 25-39% to male factors. MSC-Exos effectively treat specific conditions such as tubal infertility and diminished ovarian reserve by leveraging the inherent capacity of mesenchymal stem cells to promote tissue repair and inhibit fibrogenesis. The review particularly emphasizes conditions within the PCOS-endometriosis-insulin resistance cluster: PCOS (affecting ~70% of patients with infertility) associates with chronic anovulation, hyperandrogenemia, and metabolic dysfunction including insulin resistance and obesity, while endometriosis causes infertility through anatomical deformities, adhesions, and fibrosis. Bone marrow-derived MSCs (BM-MSCs) and adipose-derived stem cells (ADSCs) demonstrate remarkable efficacy in addressing pathologies including premature ovarian insufficiency, intrauterine adhesions, thin endometrium, and non-obstructive azoospermia through upregulation of growth factors (VEGF, IGF-1, TSP-1) and reduction of oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction. These findings support MSC-based therapeutics as a multifactorial treatment strategy for the complex, overlapping conditions driving female and male infertility.

title: Obesity results in lower integrin expression in women with polycystic ovarian syndrome during the window of implantation
summary: Obesity significantly downregulates αvβ3-integrin expression in women with polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS), particularly during the implantation window, thereby compromising endometrial receptivity. PCOS is characterized by hormonal imbalances, including elevated androgens and insulin levels, as well as ovarian dysfunction, with obesity serving as a major contributing factor through mechanisms involving insulin resistance and chronic inflammation. The study found that αvβ3-integrin expression was substantially reduced in obese PCOS women compared to non-obese controls, while surprisingly, other adhesion molecules such as mucin-1 and E-cadherin remained unchanged across all groups. Since αvβ3-integrin plays a critical role in cell adhesion, migration, and endometrial receptivity, its downregulation in obese PCOS patients may explain the reduced implantation success observed in this population. The findings suggest that obesity, rather than PCOS alone, is the primary driver of αvβ3-integrin downregulation and subsequent impaired endometrial function. These results highlight the importance of weight management in PCOS patients seeking pregnancy and provide insight into a molecular mechanism linking obesity to reproductive dysfunction.

title: Asian women with PCOS have enhanced ovarian reserve and ART outcomes, even at an advanced maternal age: a model for reproductive longevity?
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that Asian women with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) exhibit a delayed ovarian aging phenotype characterized by significantly elevated anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) levels and a slower age-related decline in AMH compared to normo-ovulatory women, resulting in an extended reproductive lifespan. Women with PCOS, particularly those aged 36 and older undergoing assisted reproductive technology, achieve substantially higher cumulative pregnancy rates, oocyte yield, and usable blastocyst numbers, with an adjusted relative risk for IVF pregnancy of 2.01 compared to controls. The mechanism underlying this reproductive advantage appears related to polycystic ovaries having a larger initial primordial follicle pool with reduced atresia, combined with AMH's role in maintaining follicle reserves and lower follicle-stimulating hormone levels that spare oocytes from depletion. Notably, ethnicity significantly impacts both AMH levels and PCOS prevalence, with Malays and Indians showing the highest PCOS rates, while Chinese women demonstrated elevated baseline AMH. These findings suggest that despite PCOS's association with anovulation, hyperandrogenism, and infertility in its reproductive years, women with this condition experience markedly improved assisted reproduction outcomes at advanced maternal age, potentially due to biological protection from accelerated ovarian aging.

title: Global trends and health system impact on polycystic ovary syndrome: a comprehensive analysis of age-stratified females from 1990 to 2021
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive global analysis reveals that polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is a major driver of female infertility, characterized by irregular menstruation, hyperandrogenism, and associated manifestations including acne and hirsutism. Beyond reproductive complications, PCOS significantly elevates the risk of serious long-term health conditions including type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, hypertension, dyslipidemia, metabolic syndrome, endometrial cancer, and psychiatric disorders such as anxiety and depression. The global burden of PCOS has escalated dramatically from 1990 to 2021, with the highest incidence rates among adolescents aged 10-19 years and peak prevalence in women aged 30-34 years, showing particularly sharp increases in Southeast Asia, East Asia, and South Asia alongside a positive correlation with sociodemographic index in high-income regions. Current therapeutic approaches remain limited to lifestyle modifications such as physical exercise combined with pharmacological interventions including hormonal medications, insulin sensitizers, and lipid-lowering agents, while emerging research explores plant-derived polysaccharides and bioactive extracts as potential therapeutic alternatives to address existing treatment gaps.

title: Investigating perceptions and usage of fertility supplements: a mixed methods analysis of a large online forum
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary:
# Summary

This mixed-methods analysis of an online fertility forum examined supplement usage among individuals undergoing assisted reproductive technologies, with particular relevance to conditions including endometriosis, PCOS, and diminished ovarian reserve. CoQ10 emerged as the most frequently discussed supplement, with evidence supporting its capacity to increase oocyte retrieval, fertilization rates, embryo quality, and clinical pregnancy rates through antioxidant mechanisms that counteract age-related oxidative stress in ovarian follicles. Beyond CoQ10, forum users reported using multiple supplements targeting distinct reproductive pathways, including myo-inositol for PCOS-related insulin resistance, vitamin D and calcium for oocyte maturation, and vasodilatory agents (low-dose aspirin, L-arginine, beet root juice) for endometrial thickness and receptivity. However, the analysis revealed considerable controversy surrounding certain supplements—notably DHEA, which can cause menstrual irregularities and hormonal elevation, and agents like Benadryl with potential adverse effects on implantation. The data demonstrate that while evidence-based supplements like CoQ10 and vitamin D generated both positive and negative user experiences, only folic acid supplementation aligns with American Society for Reproductive Medicine guidelines, highlighting a disconnect between widespread supplement use and clinical recommendations in fertility care.

title: Integrated Bioinformatics and Experimental Analysis Revealed Crosstalk Between IL-6, Autophagy, Ubiquitination, and Key miRNAs in Female Infertility: Insights from Ovarian Endometriosis and Polycystic Ovary Syndrome
summary:
# Summary

This integrated bioinformatics and experimental study reveals a complex crosstalk between IL-6, autophagy, ubiquitination, and microRNAs (miR-146a-5p and miR-9 family) that underlies female infertility in both ovarian endometriosis and PCOS. IL-6 drives chronic inflammation through the MAPK signaling pathway, leading to disrupted ovulation, impaired embryo implantation, and compromised endometrial receptivity in both conditions. Compensatory upregulation of miR-146a-5p in endometriotic tissues dampens this IL-6-driven inflammation by targeting TRAF6, IRAK1, and multiple downstream inflammatory genes including ICAM1 and CXCL8, while the miR-9 family regulates protein degradation and influences estrogen production through aromatase expression. The study demonstrates that autophagy and ubiquitination are tightly linked cellular mechanisms essential for ovarian health, with their dysregulation in PCOS contributing to oxidative stress, granulosa cell apoptosis, and follicular loss through impaired clearance of damaged organelles and dysregulated protein turnover. Key regulatory pathways including FoxO signaling, p53-mediated apoptosis, and ubiquitin-mediated proteolysis converge to control ovarian reserve and follicular development, identifying potential therapeutic targets for addressing the shared pathogenic mechanisms of endometriosis-associated and PCOS-related infertility.

title: Advancements in Sonication-Based Extraction Techniques for Ovarian Follicular Fluid Analysis: Implications for Infertility Diagnostics and Assisted Reproductive Technologies
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian follicular fluid serves as a dynamic biosensor that mirrors the oocyte microenvironment and contains diverse biomarkers including proteins, metabolites, lipids, hormones, and extracellular vesicles secreted by granulosa and theca cells. Sonication-based extraction techniques, which exploit acoustic cavitation, significantly improve the recovery and analysis of these fluid components by enhancing protein solubilization, metabolite release, and lipid recovery while disrupting abundant protein aggregates and lipid-protein complexes that interfere with traditional analysis methods. Follicular fluid composition reflects follicular health and predicts both oocyte quality and IVF outcomes, offering advantages over conventional morphological assessment which lacks necessary specificity and sensitivity. Age-related declines in key proteins such as hemopexin, serotransferrin, and kininogen—associated with reduced antioxidant defense and altered iron homeostasis—correlate with diminished oocyte quality and reduced IVF success, while specific metabolite alterations including citrate, pyruvate, succinate, ceramides, and branched-chain amino acids distinguish pathological conditions like PCOS and endometriosis, indicating dysregulated carbohydrate and amino acid metabolism. These findings establish advanced sonication-based proteomic and metabolomic analysis of follicular fluid as a promising diagnostic approach for improving infertility assessment and optimizing assisted reproductive technology outcomes across different reproductive conditions.

title: Pathogenesis and treatment of obesity-related polycystic ovary syndrome
summary:
# Summary: Pathogenesis and Treatment of Obesity-Related Polycystic Ovary Syndrome

Obesity drives PCOS pathogenesis through a cascade of interconnected metabolic and reproductive dysfunctions initiated by visceral adipose tissue-derived free fatty acids, which trigger insulin resistance and suppress sex hormone-binding globulin, leading to elevated androgens via insulin and IGF-1 activation of steroidogenic enzymes. This hyperandrogenemia establishes a pathological feedback loop by promoting visceral adipocyte differentiation and excessive autophagy in granulosa cells through suppression of the PI3K/AKT/mTOR axis and reactive oxygen species accumulation. Multiple cellular stress mechanisms converge to impair ovarian function, including endoplasmic reticulum stress (activated by free fatty acids and inflammatory cytokines), mitochondrial dysfunction, and dysregulated autophagy—processes further exacerbated by chronic low-grade inflammation driven by adipokine secretion and AMPK-mediated NLRP3 inflammasome activation. Defective autophagy markers correlate with insulin resistance severity in PCOS patients, while reduced mitochondrial quality control contributes to premature ovarian aging and granulosa cell dysfunction. The article identifies therapeutic targets including Wnt5a, which attenuates excessive autophagy, adropin-mediated M2 macrophage polarization, and restoration of immune tolerance through CD56+ NK cells and CD163+ macrophages in the endometrium. These findings establish obesity-related PCOS as a multisystem metabolic disorder amenable to targeted interventions addressing autophagy dysregulation, oxidative stress, and inflammatory pathways.

title: Up-regulated WNT5A in PCOS affects steroidogenesis, proliferation and apoptosis of granulosa cells through the PI3K/AKT pathway
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that WNT5A, which is elevated in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) patients and their granulosa cells, impairs ovarian function through activation of the PI3K/AKT signaling pathway. Elevated WNT5A suppresses the expression of key steroidogenic enzymes (CYP11A1, CYP19A1, and STAR) and hormone receptors (FSHR and LHR), resulting in decreased estradiol and progesterone secretion. Additionally, WNT5A promotes granulosa cell apoptosis via upregulation of pro-apoptotic proteins (BAX and cleaved caspase-3) and downregulation of anti-apoptotic BCL-2, while simultaneously inhibiting cell proliferation through S-phase arrest. The inflammatory milieu in PCOS, driven by lipopolysaccharide and chronic inflammation, upregulates WNT5A expression; critically, pharmacological inhibition of PI3K/AKT with LY-294002 reverses WNT5A-mediated effects on steroidogenesis. These findings establish WNT5A as a pathogenic mediator linking chronic inflammation to impaired steroid hormone production and compromised granulosa cell viability in PCOS.

title: The promoter T-413A variant and elevated enzyme levels of heme oxygenase-1 associated with an increased risk of polycystic ovarian syndrome
summary:
# Summary

Heme oxygenase-1 (HMOX1) plays a central role in regulating oxidative stress, ferroptosis, and glycolipid metabolism through its enzymatic degradation of heme into protective byproducts including biliverdin, bilirubin, and carbon monoxide. The study identified that the HMOX1 rs2071746T/A promoter polymorphism, particularly the TT genotype and dominant genetic models, is significantly associated with increased polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS) risk, with the effect independent of age, BMI, and recruitment year. Elevated circulating HMOX1 levels were correlated with slightly increased PCOS risk, and both the rs2071746T/A and (GT)n polymorphisms significantly affected obesity, oxidative stress, and endocrine abnormalities in PCOS patients. Women with PCOS exhibited higher plasma HMOX1 levels alongside elevated testosterone and acne severity, linking HMOX1 dysregulation to PCOS's metabolic and reproductive manifestations. These findings suggest that genetic variation in HMOX1 contributes to PCOS pathogenesis through altered regulation of oxidative stress and metabolic homeostasis, with potential implications for understanding the overlap between insulin resistance, endometriosis risk, and PCOS.

title: Effects of supplementation with two probiotic strains (Lactobacillus helveticus and Bifidobacterium longum) on hormonal status, oxidative stress, and clinical symptoms in women with polycystic ovary syndrome: a randomized clinical trial
summary:
# Summary

Eight weeks of supplementation with Lactobacillus helveticus and Bifidobacterium longum significantly improved multiple PCOS-related pathological markers in women with the disorder. The probiotic strains elevated sex hormone binding globulin (SHBG) and reduced the free androgen index (FAI), counteracting the hyperandrogenism characteristic of PCOS. Additionally, both probiotics enhanced systemic antioxidant capacity by increasing total antioxidant capacity (TAC) and superoxide dismutase (SOD) activity while simultaneously reducing oxidative stress markers including malondialdehyde (MDA) and the inflammatory marker C-reactive protein (CRP). These findings support a mechanistic model wherein dysbiosis contributes to PCOS pathogenesis through elevated oxidative stress and inflammation, and targeted probiotic intervention restores microbial balance to ameliorate both hormonal dysfunction and systemic inflammation. The results suggest probiotics may offer a therapeutic option for managing PCOS symptoms by simultaneously addressing insulin resistance, hormonal imbalance, and the oxidative-inflammatory milieu underlying the condition.

title: Unveiling the potential targets and mechanisms of DEHP/BPA exposure on PCOS: insights from network toxicology and molecular docking
summary:
# Summary

Environmental endocrine-disrupting chemicals, particularly di(2-ethylhexyl) phthalate (DEHP) and bisphenol A (BPA), are implicated in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) pathogenesis through multiple molecular mechanisms. Network toxicology and protein-protein interaction analyses identified six key hub proteins (PTGER3, SOX15, TOP2A, CCNB1, BCL2, and CYP19A1) whose dysregulation links chemical exposure to PCOS development. DEHP and BPA disrupt critical ovarian processes by binding estrogen receptors, suppressing steroidogenic enzymes, upregulating anti-apoptotic factors like BCL2, and dysregulating transcription factors such as SOX15, collectively impairing steroidogenesis, folliculogenesis, and glucose homeostasis. The compounds also induce insulin resistance and alter the luteinizing hormone-to-follicle-stimulating hormone ratio, while dysregulating microRNAs (miR-335-5p and miR-27a-3p) that suppress CYP19A1 and reduce aromatase activity by 42-68%. Epidemiological evidence demonstrates that elevated urinary metabolite levels of these chemicals correlate with increased PCOS severity, including menstrual irregularities and hyperandrogenemia, establishing endocrine-disrupting chemicals as critical environmental triggers in PCOS development.

title: The Endocrinological Basis for Polycystic Ovary Syndrome: An Evolutionary Perspective
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is characterized by a pathological triad of ovarian hyperandrogenism, insulin resistance, and preferential abdominal fat accumulation, with high heritability influenced by genetic variants such as DENND1A that promote androgen production. The elevated androgens in PCOS drive multiple metabolic abnormalities through androgen receptor signaling, including increased de novo lipid synthesis, impaired catecholamine-stimulated lipolysis, and altered adipocyte function that collectively exacerbate insulin resistance and promote visceral fat accumulation. Hyperandrogenism also impairs ovulation through direct ovarian effects and anti-müllerian hormone overproduction, leading to anovulatory infertility, while the associated visceral adiposity and insulin resistance substantially increase risk for type 2 diabetes, metabolic syndrome, hepatic steatosis, and cardiovascular disease. The underlying mechanisms involve convergence of insulin and TNF-alpha signaling on insulin receptor substrates, which paradoxically enhance both ovarian androgen production and systemic inflammatory responses despite insulin resistance. Notably, androgen suppression via receptor blockade or GnRH agonists reduces abdominal fat mass and insulin resistance, and the condition appears evolutionarily linked to enhanced athleticism and muscularity, suggesting PCOS traits may reflect a metabolic adaptation to ancestral environments that becomes maladaptive in modern sedentary, obesity-prone conditions.

title: The Association Between Polycystic Ovary Syndrome and Vitamin D Deficiency in Infertile Women: A Case‐Control Study
summary: This case-control study demonstrates a strong association between vitamin D deficiency and polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) in infertile women, with PCOS patients showing 2.37 times higher odds of vitamin D deficiency compared to controls. Vitamin D plays a critical role in reproductive physiology and insulin metabolism; deficiency contributes to insulin resistance, elevated androgen levels, and disrupted follicle development—key pathogenic mechanisms in PCOS. The study reveals that among PCOS patients, those with higher body mass index exhibit more severe vitamin D deficiency, establishing a metabolic link between these factors.
