Notably, vitamin D supplementation improves multiple PCOS-related outcomes, including insulin sensitivity, lipid profiles, menstrual regularity, and ovulation induction success rates, while also potentially reducing androgen levels. These findings suggest vitamin D deficiency as a modifiable contributing factor to PCOS pathogenesis and infertility, with supplementation representing a promising therapeutic intervention to improve metabolic and reproductive outcomes in affected women.

title: The effect of TPOAb on pregnancy outcomes among people with polycystic ovary syndrome with euthyroidism undergoing in vitro fertilization
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is a leading cause of infertility characterized by irregular menstrual cycles, hyperandrogenism, and metabolic and reproductive complications including reduced oocyte quality, abnormal fertilization, and dysregulation of sex hormone receptors and endometrial insulin resistance. Thyroid autoimmunity (TAI) frequently coexists with PCOS at a prevalence approximately three times higher than in non-PCOS populations, and the combination exacerbates metabolic and reproductive complications while being associated with poor treatment response. However, in euthyroid PCOS patients undergoing in vitro fertilization, the presence of anti-thyroid peroxidase antibodies (TPOAb) showed an unexpected beneficial association with early embryonic outcomes, including significantly higher fertilization rates, two-pronuclei fertilization rates, and cleavage rates after propensity score matching. Importantly, TPOAb positivity was not associated with adverse embryonic or pregnancy outcomes, with no significant differences observed in biochemical pregnancy rate, clinical pregnancy rate, miscarriage rate, or live birth rate between TPOAb-positive and TPOAb-negative groups. The authors propose that mild immune activation induced by TPOAb positivity may transiently enhance folliculogenesis and oocyte maturation without compromising downstream pregnancy outcomes in euthyroid PCOS patients.

title: Global, regional, and national burden of infertility attributable to polycystic ovary syndrome, 1990–2021: results from the Global Burden of Disease Study 2021
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome is the most prevalent cause of anovulatory infertility, accounting for up to 80% of cases, with elevated androgen levels disrupting the hypothalamic–pituitary–ovarian axis and leading to ovulatory dysfunction, arrested follicular development, and reduced oocyte quality. Between 1990 and 2021, the global burden of PCOS-related infertility increased substantially, driven by both population growth (55.3%) and epidemiological changes (43.7%), with secondary infertility cases rising more sharply than primary infertility cases. The disease burden exhibits significant geographic and demographic variation, with high sociodemographic index regions showing the highest age-standardized prevalence rates (1,137.85 per 100,000), while East Asia and South Asia reported the fastest annual growth rates of approximately 1.9%. PCOS-related infertility predominantly affects women in their 40s, and this burden is compounded by substantial psychological sequelae, including increased rates of anxiety, depression, and sexual dysfunction. Austria demonstrated the highest prevalence globally at 1,447.20 per 100,000 population, and women born in more recent cohorts face disproportionately higher risks, reflecting pronounced socioeconomic gradients in disease expression and outcome.

title: Waist-to-hip ratio and female infertility: An observational study and Mendelian randomization analysis
summary:
# Summary

This study establishes waist-to-hip ratio (WHR) as a causal risk factor for female infertility through observational and Mendelian randomization analyses. Each 0.1 unit increase in WHR elevates infertility risk by 46%, with women having WHR ≥0.85 demonstrating substantially increased susceptibility, particularly those aged 30-34 years. Central adiposity drives infertility through multiple mechanisms: obesity-induced oxidative stress elevates androgens and triggers ovarian inflammation, insulin resistance promotes hyperandrogenism that suppresses gonadotropin secretion, and elevated leptin disrupts hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis function while impairing preimplantation embryonic development and uterine receptivity. Anovulatory infertility represents the primary mechanism, with polycystic ovary syndrome being the most common underlying cause in anovulatory women. Importantly, weight loss through lifestyle modifications and glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists improves menstrual cyclicity, ovulation, and pregnancy rates, demonstrating that central obesity-related infertility is reversible and amenable to intervention.

title: Exploring the association between polycystic ovary syndrome and female sexual dysfunction: a cross-sectional study
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study examined the relationship between polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) and female sexual dysfunction (FSD), finding that PCOS itself was not directly associated with FSD when analyzed independently. However, women with PCOS demonstrated significantly elevated rates of obesity, anxiety, and depression compared to controls, and these psychological factors—particularly anxiety and depression—emerged as strong independent predictors of sexual dysfunction alongside younger age and relationship dissatisfaction. Among women with PCOS, sexual difficulties manifested as reduced satisfaction and increased pain during intercourse, accompanied by greater feelings of embarrassment and inferiority related to visible manifestations of hyperandrogenism such as hirsutism and acne. The study revealed that body image disturbances and psychological burden were more influential on sexual function than hormonal mechanisms alone, though lowering androgen levels through oral contraceptive use improved sexual satisfaction and orgasm. Notably, postmenopausal women with PCOS showed no significant differences in FSD rates compared to postmenopausal women without PCOS, suggesting that menopausal hormone therapy and aging-related factors overshadow PCOS-specific effects on sexual function in this population.

title: Association of the dietary anti-oxidant index with inflammatory marker such as interleukin-6 in women with and without polycystic ovarian syndrome: a comparative case‒control study
summary:
# Summary

This comparative case-control study demonstrates that women with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) exhibit significantly lower dietary antioxidant indices, particularly as measured by the FRAP method, compared to healthy controls, alongside elevated levels of the pro-inflammatory cytokine interleukin-6 (IL-6). PCOS pathophysiology involves a complex interplay of hyperandrogenism, insulin resistance, and oxidative stress, all of which are modulated by dietary factors through inflammatory and antioxidant pathways. Women with PCOS consume substantially less of key antioxidant micronutrients including selenium, zinc, vitamin E, and beta-carotene, which may contribute to increased oxidative stress and inflammatory responses. Dietary interventions emphasizing antioxidant-rich foods show promise in reducing PCOS incidence and improving metabolic parameters, while a healthy diet combined with physical activity and appropriate BMI significantly reduces infertility risk related to ovulation disorders. These findings underscore the critical role of dietary antioxidants in PCOS prevention and management, suggesting that nutritional intervention targeting oxidative stress and inflammation represents a first-line therapeutic approach for this metabolic and reproductive disorder.

title: Mitochondrial DNA Deletions and Plasma GDF-15 Protein Levels Are Linked to Hormonal Dysregulation and Multi-Organ Involvement in Female Reproductive Endocrine Disorders
summary: Mitochondrial DNA deletions and elevated plasma growth differentiation factor 15 (GDF-15) emerge as linked biomarkers of mitochondrial dysfunction in female reproductive endocrine disorders, particularly in the insulin resistance-polycystic ovary syndrome (IR-PCOS) overlap. Mitochondrial impairment in IR-PCOS is characterized by reduced ATP production, decreased mitochondrial membrane potential, and elevated reactive oxygen species, which collectively drive insulin resistance through accumulation of lipid metabolites and diminished mitochondrial plasticity. mtDNA deletions correlate with multi-organ involvement including endocrine, gastrointestinal, and neuropsychiatric symptoms, as well as accelerated reproductive aging marked by reduced anti-Müllerian hormone levels and impaired ovulatory function. GDF-15, a stress-responsive cytokine and the most robust plasma biomarker of mitochondrial dysfunction, integrates mitochondrial and metabolic stress signals to modulate systemic endocrine and inflammatory pathways, with thyroid function emerging as an independent predictor of GDF-15 levels. Patients with elevated GDF-15 frequently present with obesity, exercise intolerance, thermoregulation dysfunction, gastrointestinal complaints, and autoimmune disorders, indicating that mitochondrial stress represents a unifying pathophysiologic mechanism across reproductive, metabolic, and systemic complications in these disorders. These findings suggest that targeting mitochondrial dysfunction may offer a therapeutic opportunity to address the interconnected hormonal and metabolic abnormalities in female reproductive endocrine disorders.

title: The dual burden of obesity: decoding metabolism and female reproductive endocrinology
summary:
# Summary

Obesity disrupts female reproductive endocrinology through multiple interconnected metabolic and endocrine mechanisms. Excess adipose tissue dysregulates the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis and alters interactions with insulin, leptin, and sex hormones, leading to menstrual irregularities, ovulatory dysfunction, and infertility. High-fat diet exposure induces epigenetic modifications including altered histone acetylation and DNA methylation patterns in hypothalamic tissue, while obesity-associated genes such as FTO and Lep directly impair both lipid metabolism and reproductive function. Systemic inflammation and dysbiosis of the intestinal microbiota further contribute to reproductive endocrine dysfunction, and excess androgens promote abdominal obesity and insulin resistance, disrupting follicular development and ovulation—establishing a bidirectional relationship characteristic of polycystic ovary syndrome. Additionally, multiple metabolic peptides including GLP-1, adiponectin, and ghrelin regulate both feeding behavior and reproductive function, suggesting that metabolic regulation is fundamentally linked to fertility outcomes. These findings underscore obesity as a systemic condition affecting female reproduction through epigenetic, hormonal, and inflammatory pathways that converge on hypothalamic-pituitary function.

title: EGOI-PCOS survey on the prevalence of respiratory disorders associated with polycystic ovary syndrome
summary:
# Summary

This EGOI-PCOS survey reveals a substantial link between polycystic ovary syndrome and chronic respiratory diseases, with 27.4% of surveyed PCOS patients reporting respiratory issues and asthma as the most prevalent condition, affecting 61.5% of those with respiratory comorbidities. The association between PCOS and respiratory dysfunction involves multiple interconnected pathways: insulin resistance (present in ~80% of PCOS patients) impairs pulmonary function by altering fibroblast deposition and muscarinic modulation while reducing spirometry parameters including FEV1 and FVC; obesity and metabolic syndrome drive both PCOS and pulmonary affections through low-grade systemic inflammation and altered cytokine production; and hormonal alterations characteristic of PCOS regulate histamine release and immune polarization while downregulating beta-2 adrenergic receptors, thereby reducing airway conductance and lung capacity. Menstrual disturbances, elevated androgen levels, and asthma cluster together in PCOS, with bidirectional associations where young girls with asthma show higher risk of developing menstrual disorders. Independent risk factors for respiratory disease in PCOS patients include older age, higher body weight, and positive family history of chronic respiratory disease, with acute respiratory failure and uncontrolled asthma attacks representing primary causes of non-gynecological hospitalization in this population.

title: Effect of vitamin D deficiency on clinical pregnancy outcomes in women with polycystic ovary syndrome undergoing in vitro fertilization
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that vitamin D deficiency significantly reduces clinical pregnancy rates in women with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) undergoing in vitro fertilization, with serum vitamin D levels ≥20 ng/mL independently associated with improved pregnancy outcomes. Vitamin D deficiency is highly prevalent in PCOS patients and correlates with insulin resistance, obesity, and reduced ovulation rates during ovarian stimulation, though it does not affect oocyte retrieval, embryo quality, or live birth rates. The biological mechanisms involve vitamin D receptor signaling in reproductive tissues (ovary, endometrium, decidua, placenta), where vitamin D regulates critical processes including decidualization, implantation, and human chorionic gonadotropin secretion, partly through modulation of the HOXA10 gene essential for endometrial receptivity. Vitamin D deficiency impairs trophoblast function by decreasing hCG expression while increasing pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, IFN-γ), thereby compromising implantation potential. Vitamin D supplementation improves multiple reproductive outcomes including menstrual cycle regularity, follicular development, fertilization rates, and metabolic parameters (fasting glucose, HOMA-IR, testosterone), while reducing elevated AMH levels associated with vitamin D insufficiency in PCOS.

title: Exploring the Role of Kisspeptin in Polycystic Ovary Syndrome and Its Associated Pregnancy Complications
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) represents a complex endocrine disorder characterized by hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis dysfunction, disordered follicle development, hyperandrogenism, and insulin resistance, with substantially elevated risks of gestational diabetes mellitus and preeclampsia during pregnancy. Kisspeptin, a peptide hormone widely distributed across the hypothalamus, gonads, pancreas, liver, and placenta, serves as a critical upstream regulator of gonadotropin-releasing hormone neurons and plays an essential role in normal pregnancy physiology through its placental expression. In PCOS patients, abnormally elevated kisspeptin levels directly impair early embryo implantation and placental development, while also increasing the risk of pregnancy complications by dysregulating insulin and growth factor signaling. The pathophysiology involves a vicious cycle wherein insulin resistance promotes androgen production and reduces sex hormone-binding globulin levels, with chronic inflammation from elevated tumor necrosis factor-α, C-reactive protein, and interleukin-6 further exacerbating metabolic dysfunction and adipose tissue dysfunction. In gestational diabetes mellitus, elevated insulin and IGF-II abnormally stimulate matrix metalloproteinase expression in the fetus, contributing to placental hypervascularization and aberrant placental development that increases preeclampsia risk through insufficient spiral artery remodeling and reduced placental blood flow. This work establishes kisspeptin as a potential therapeutic target linking PCOS pathogenesis to pregnancy complications through interconnected metabolic, endocrine, and placental mechanisms.

title: What role do miRNAs play in the injured heart of the infertile female?
summary:
# Summary

This article examines how dysregulated microRNAs (miRNAs) contribute to cardiovascular damage in infertile women, revealing critical connections between reproductive and cardiac pathology. Specific miRNAs including miR-155, miR-185, miR-21, and miR-451 are dysregulated in both cardiovascular disease and female infertility, promoting inflammation, cardiac hypertrophy, and heart failure through altered gene expression and impaired vascular repair. The dysregulation affects multiple pathological processes including angiogenesis, cell proliferation, and vascular integrity; notably, downregulation of certain miRNAs impairs endothelial progenitor cell function, compromising the heart's capacity to repair ischemic damage. Infertility and cardiovascular disease share common mechanistic pathways mediated by overlapping endocrine and metabolic conditions—particularly polycystic ovary syndrome, metabolic syndrome, endometriosis, and vitamin D deficiency—as well as psychological stress as a unifying factor. Circulating exosomal miRNAs serve as mediators of paracrine signaling between tissues, making miR-21 and miR-451 potential therapeutic targets for simultaneously addressing both infertility and cardiac pathology in vulnerable populations.

title: Reproductive prognosis factors in women with infertility and polycystic ovary syndrome undergoing in vitro fertilization techniques
summary:
# Summary

This study examined reproductive outcomes in women with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) undergoing in vitro fertilization, identifying anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) level and age as critical predictive factors. PCOS, characterized by elevated AMH, testosterone, luteinizing hormone, and obesity, is strongly associated with infertility through mechanisms including impaired oocyte quality and compromised endometrial competence. Women with elevated AMH demonstrated improved ovarian response to stimulation—requiring lower gonadotropin doses while achieving higher follicle counts and embryo yields—resulting in increased live birth rates when under age 35. However, women over 35 with elevated AMH levels paradoxically exhibited significantly lower live birth and cumulative live birth rates despite producing more embryos, potentially due to age-related oocyte quality decline and elevated progesterone at trigger. Additionally, overweight women showed reduced fertilization rates, underscoring the complex interplay between metabolic factors, hormonal status, and reproductive success in PCOS patients pursuing assisted reproduction.

title: Cellular senescence and polycystic ovary syndrome: mechanisms and therapeutic strategies from a new perspective
summary:
# Summary

Cellular senescence—characterized by cell cycle arrest, senescence-associated secretory phenotype (SASP), and epigenetic changes—emerges as a key mechanism contributing to polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), a disorder defined by polycystic ovaries, elevated testosterone, obesity, and insulin resistance. Both replicative senescence (driven by telomere shortening) and stress-induced premature senescence (triggered by oxidative stress) promote PCOS pathology, particularly impairing follicular development and causing endometrial hyporeceptivity in obese, hyperandrogenic, or insulin-resistant patients. The p53/p21 signaling pathway and genes such as LMNB1, ARID1A, and ANGPTL4 regulate cell cycle arrest in granulosa cells, reducing their proliferation and causing follicular atresia. Senescent cells produce proinflammatory mediators including IL-6, IL-1β, and GDF-15 that recruit immune cells and amplify systemic inflammation. Novel therapeutic approaches using senolytic and senomorphic drugs that selectively eliminate or reprogram senescent cells offer promise for PCOS management by targeting this underlying pathological mechanism.

title: Editorial: Nutrition, inflammation and oxidative stress in obstetrics and gynecology
summary:
# Summary

Oxidative stress and chronic low-grade inflammation are pivotal pathophysiological mechanisms underlying infertility and pregnancy complications in obstetrics and gynecology. Nutritional factors critically modulate these inflammatory and oxidative cascades, with specific biomarkers—including elevated serum ferritin, uric acid, and dietary insulin load—associated with adverse reproductive outcomes such as pre-eclampsia, endometriosis, and diminished ovarian reserve. Vitamin D deficiency contributes to early-onset pre-eclampsia through impaired anti-inflammatory and antioxidant pathways, while polyphenol-rich, plant-based, and omega-3-rich diets demonstrate protective effects against endometriosis. Importantly, high consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages, energy drinks, and alcohol increases risk for endometrial polyps and other gynecological pathology. These findings collectively establish nutrition as a critical mediator of the oxidative-inflammatory balance in reproductive health, supporting diet-based strategies as adjunctive therapeutic approaches across obstetric and gynecological disorders.

title: Impact of Metformin therapy on miR-9, miR-223, and miR-132 and inflammasome-related gen expression in obese and non-obes PCOS patients: A comparative study with healthy controls
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that metformin improves PCOS-associated chronic inflammation and insulin resistance through microRNA-mediated regulation of inflammasome activation. PCOS patients exhibit dysregulated expression of miR-9, miR-223, and miR-132, which normally suppress the NLRP3 inflammasome and downstream production of pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-1β and IL-18. Metformin treatment upregulates miR-223 and miR-132 while downregulating miR-9, leading to reduced NLRP3 inflammasome activation and decreased IL-1β and NLRP3 expression in both obese and non-obese PCOS patients. In addition to modulating microRNA expression, metformin ameliorates metabolic dysfunction by reducing insulin resistance, suppressing androgen production, normalizing the LH/FSH ratio, and lowering AMH levels. These findings indicate that metformin's therapeutic efficacy in PCOS involves coordinated regulation of microRNA-inflammasome signaling, which simultaneously addresses the chronic inflammation and metabolic derangements characteristic of this disorder.

title: Diabetes, Obesity, and Endometrial Cancer: A Review
summary:
# Summary

Diabetes and obesity represent independent yet synergistic risk factors for endometrial cancer, with their combined effect significantly elevating cancer-specific mortality and surgical complications including thromboembolic events. The pathophysiological mechanisms involve multiple interconnected pathways: unopposed estrogen exposure due to anovulation, polycystic ovary syndrome, and increased adipose tissue aromatase activity; chronic hyperinsulinemia from insulin resistance, which promotes endometrial cell proliferation through activation of PI3K/Akt and MAPK signaling cascades; and hyperglycemia-driven metabolic reprogramming that enhances glycolysis via AMPK/mTOR/S6 and MAPK pathways. Additionally, diabetes-associated chronic low-grade inflammation and oxidative stress contribute to carcinogenesis, while elevated fasting insulin, HOMA-IR, and C-peptide levels serve as important biomarkers for endometrial cancer risk. Clinical interventions show promise, with metformin reducing overall mortality and recurrence-free survival in endometrial cancer patients, whereas insulin therapy paradoxically increases cancer death risk. The review integrates molecular findings on Type I tumors (CTNNB1, KRAS, POLE, PTEN mutations) and Type II tumors (TP53, HER2/neu alterations), along with hereditary cancer syndromes including Lynch syndrome and Cowden syndrome, to provide a comprehensive framework for understanding metabolic drivers of endometrial oncogenesis.

title: Association between alpha-1-acid glycoprotein and infertility in the NHANES 2015–2020
summary:
# Summary

This NHANES-based study identifies alpha-1-acid glycoprotein (AGP), an acute-phase protein synthesized by the liver and peripheral tissues, as a significant biomarker associated with female infertility, with a 1.97-fold increased infertility risk per 1 g/L increase in AGP levels. AGP contributes to infertility through its role in inflammatory processes, particularly in conditions like polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) and endometriosis, where aberrant fucosylation of AGP upregulates pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6 and TNF-α) and impairs endometrial receptivity by downregulating vascular endothelial growth factor and integrin expression. In PCOS patients, AGP levels above 0.85 g/L correlate with a threefold higher infertility risk, with the pathophysiology mediated partly through insulin resistance-induced glycoprotein hypersecretion. The study demonstrates that chronic low-grade inflammation accelerates ovarian aging through TNF-α-induced mitochondrial dysfunction and affects both endometrial function and the ovarian microenvironment. Therapeutic interventions including anti-inflammatory dietary strategies (Mediterranean diet) and metformin therapy for insulin resistance represent evidence-based approaches to reduce AGP-associated infertility risk, particularly in women over 35 years with AGP concentrations near 0.76 g/L.

title: Adipose-androgen crosstalk in polycystic ovary syndrome: mechanisms and therapeutic implications
summary:
# Adipose-Androgen Crosstalk in Polycystic Ovary Syndrome

This article elucidates a bidirectional pathogenic cycle linking visceral adipose tissue dysfunction and hyperandrogenism in PCOS, where dysfunctional visceral fat intensifies insulin resistance and stimulates ovarian androgen overproduction through inflammatory pathways including TNF-α and IL-6 activation of NF-κB signaling. Conversely, elevated androgens perpetuate adipose tissue dysfunction by promoting central obesity, suppressing lipogenesis and lipolysis, reducing protective adiponectin while elevating pro-inflammatory leptin, and upregulating androgen-metabolizing enzymes like AKR1C3 in adipose tissue. Brown adipose tissue dysfunction emerges as a critical contributor to PCOS pathology, with reduced BAT activity correlating directly to insulin resistance severity, while BAT activation enhances insulin sensitivity and glucose homeostasis through thermogenic and metabolic mechanisms. Established therapeutic approaches including metformin, GLP-1 receptor agonists, and anti-androgens target this adipose-ovarian axis, while emerging therapies such as BAT-activating strategies (cold exposure, exercise, mirabegron), SGLT-2 inhibitors, and anti-TNF-α treatments offer novel opportunities to break this pathogenic cycle. The complex interplay of adipocyte hypertrophy, mitochondrial dysfunction, endoplasmic reticulum stress, and inflammatory mediator release cascades throughout the body, explaining PCOS-associated metabolic complications including cardiovascular disease, type 2 diabetes, and endometrial cancer.

title: Global, Regional, and National Burden of Endometriosis, PCOS, and Unexplained Infertility and Their Attribution to Infertility, 1990–2021: Global Burden of Disease Study 2021
summary:
# Summary

This Global Burden of Disease Study 2021 examined the prevalence and disease burden of endometriosis, polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS), and unexplained infertility from 1990–2021, revealing that PCOS and endometriosis are the leading causes of female infertility, with PCOS showing higher age-standardized prevalence rates than endometriosis. While endometriosis causes infertility primarily through structural alterations and is more prevalent in low- and middle-income regions (particularly Oceania and Eastern Europe), PCOS-associated infertility is more common in high-income regions and exhibits correlations with rising obesity prevalence. Endometriosis burden peaks in women aged 25–29 years and is effectively treated with surgery and assisted reproductive technologies, whereas PCOS-related infertility remains a greater overall disease burden. Unexplained infertility contributed the highest burden across most regions and showed associations with delayed childbearing age, environmental factors, and reduced access to diagnostic resources in low-income areas. Geographic and socioeconomic disparities strongly influence the distribution of these conditions, with female factors accounting for 70–80% of infertility cases overall, underscoring the need for region-specific interventions to address diagnostic delays and healthcare access barriers.

title: Glycolysis in polycystic ovary syndrome: pathogenesis and treatment
summary:
# Summary

Glycolysis plays a central role in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) pathogenesis, with enhanced aerobic glycolysis observed in both granulosa cells and endometrial tissue through elevated expression of key glycolytic enzymes including HK2, PFKP, PKM2, and LDHA. Insulin resistance, a hallmark of PCOS, triggers metabolic reprogramming toward glycolysis while hyperinsulinemia activates the IGF-1/PI3K-Akt-mTOR signaling cascade in target tissues, promoting cell proliferation and inhibiting apoptosis. Elevated androgens induce mitochondrial damage and anaerobic glycolysis, leading to lactic acid accumulation that decreases intracellular pH and promotes granulosa cell apoptosis, while the hyperandrogenic environment shifts macrophage polarization toward the pro-inflammatory M1 phenotype characterized by high glycolytic activity. Glycolysis correlates with multiple PCOS features including hyperandrogenism, inflammation, ovulatory dysfunction, and diminished endometrial receptivity, contributing to infertility. Therapeutic interventions including N-acetylcysteine effectively alleviate oxidative stress, mitigate mitochondrial dysfunction, and reduce lactate levels and apoptosis, suggesting that targeting the glycolytic shift represents a promising treatment approach for PCOS.

title: Development and validation of an explainable machine learning and nomogram model for early detection and risk stratification of polycystic ovary syndrome: a multicenter study
summary:
# Summary

This multicenter study developed and validated an interpretable machine learning framework combining XGBoost modeling with SHAP analysis to enable early detection and risk stratification of polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS). PCOS is characterized by hyperandrogenism, ovulatory dysfunction, polycystic ovarian morphology, and insulin resistance, leading to infertility and significant cardiometabolic risk. The model identified anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH), dehydroepiandrosterone sulfate (DHEAS), and testosterone as the strongest positive predictors, while elevated lipoprotein(a) and follicle-stimulating hormone showed inhibitory associations; younger age and elevated triglycerides also contributed positively to PCOS risk. The XGBoost model achieved excellent discrimination (AUC = 0.919 internally, 0.923 externally), outperforming alternative machine learning approaches, while a simplified nomogram based on routine hormonal and metabolic biomarkers provided clinically practical risk assessment (AUC = 0.887–0.901). These findings underscore that PCOS development involves integrated reproductive hormonal dysregulation and metabolic disturbances, and the proposed framework offers an accurate, transparent, and interpretable tool for individualized PCOS screening and risk assessment in clinical practice.

title: Elevated serum chromogranin A levels are independently associated with metabolic and inflammatory disturbances in polycystic ovary syndrome
summary:
# Summary

Chromogranin A (CgA), a neuroendocrine glycoprotein produced by pancreatic beta cells and other endocrine tissues, is significantly elevated in patients with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) and independently associates with metabolic and inflammatory disturbances. Serum CgA levels correlate strongly with body mass index, fasting glucose, insulin, insulin resistance (HOMA-IR), and high-sensitivity C-reactive protein, reflecting the metabolic-inflammatory burden characteristic of PCOS rather than the disease itself. A key mechanism involves pancreastatin, a CgA-derived peptide that impairs glucose-stimulated insulin secretion and induces insulin resistance, thereby contributing to the hyperglycemia and hyperinsulinemia observed in PCOS patients. These findings suggest CgA serves as a novel biomarker for metabolic risk stratification in PCOS, particularly in identifying patients with concurrent obesity, insulin resistance, and chronic low-grade inflammation. The elevated CgA levels in PCOS likely reflect dysregulation of neuroendocrine hormone secretion and storage, connecting neuroendocrine dysfunction to the metabolic complications that increase cardiometabolic and reproductive morbidity in this disorder.

title: Identification of the Shared Gene Signatures and Pathways Between Polycystic Ovary Syndrome and Endometrial Cancer Using Bioinformatics and Mendelian Randomization Analyses
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies shared genetic and molecular mechanisms linking polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) to endometrial cancer (EC), establishing a causal relationship between these conditions through Mendelian randomization analysis. Eight key genes—IL-10, CXCL8, IFNG, MMP9, PECAM1, CYBB, MYD88, and IRF4—are associated with both disorders and may serve as biomarkers connecting these diseases. Type I interferon signaling emerges as the most important common pathway, alongside genomic instability, cellular senescence, and cell cycle dysregulation. The mechanisms linking PCOS to EC include elevated androgen-driven endometrial hyperplasia, obesity and anovulation-induced estrogen elevation, and progesterone resistance, with endometrial hyperplasia serving as a potential intermediate step toward malignant transformation. These findings suggest that shared inflammatory and immune response pathways, particularly cytokine-cytokine receptor interactions, represent therapeutic targets for reducing EC risk in PCOS patients.

title: The Investigation of Vitamin D and Menstrual Cycles Trial (the inVitD Trial): a clinical trial of vitamin D supplementation on the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis
summary: The inVitD Trial investigates whether vitamin D supplementation can improve reproductive outcomes by modulating the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis and menstrual cycle hormones. Vitamin D deficiency is associated with multiple reproductive dysfunctions including long menstrual cycles, delayed ovulation, reduced fertility, anovulation, and shortened luteal phases with compromised progesterone and estradiol production. The mechanistic pathway involves vitamin D's role in promoting robust follicle development indicated by consistent pre-ovulatory estrogen rises, adequate luteinizing hormone surges, and adequate luteal progesterone production—all markers of ovulatory competence and fertile cycles. Low 25-hydroxyvitamin D levels correlate with irregular menstrual cycles and prolonged follicular phases, while higher vitamin D status (>40 ng/ml) reduces odds of follicular phase prolongation and supports normal cycle length. This trial tests whether vitamin D supplementation can serve as a low-cost, non-hormonal intervention to regulate menstrual cycles and enhance fertility through normalization of ovulatory function and reproductive hormone dynamics.

title: Efficacy of sequential letrozole and gonadotropin therapy for ovulation induction in women with polycystic ovary syndrome: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome causes significant reproductive and metabolic dysfunction, including anovulatory infertility, hyperandrogenism, menstrual irregularities, and metabolic complications such as insulin resistance and increased cardiovascular disease risk. This meta-analysis of eight randomized controlled trials demonstrates that sequential letrozole and gonadotropin therapy effectively increases ovulation rates and endometrial thickness in women with PCOS compared to alternative ovulation induction approaches. Letrozole functions as an aromatase inhibitor that downregulates estradiol and VEGF levels, promoting favorable follicular development while reducing ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome risk and supporting mono-follicular growth superior to clomiphene citrate. The combined sequential therapy with gonadotropins yields higher clinical pregnancy rates and live births than letrozole monotherapy or traditional gonadotropin protocols alone. However, despite improvements in ovulation parameters and endometrial receptivity, the treatment does not significantly improve overall pregnancy outcomes compared to control interventions. These findings suggest sequential letrozole-gonadotropin therapy optimizes reproductive function in PCOS-related infertility through its hormonal mechanisms, though further investigation is warranted regarding clinical pregnancy achievement.

title: Causality and potential drug targets for hyperandrogenism and anovulation-related infertility: two-sample Mendelian randomization and colocalization analyses
summary:
# Summary

This Mendelian randomization and colocalization study identifies hyperandrogenism as a causal driver of anovulation-related infertility through multiple pathways. Excessive androgens promote premature follicle activation, granulosa cell apoptosis, and abnormal folliculogenesis, while sex hormone-binding globulin (SHBG) acts as a protective factor by binding testosterone and regulating its bioavailability. Three genes—CD74, CYP11A1, and HLA-DRB1—emerge as potential therapeutic targets, with existing drugs (repotrectinib, mitotane, and apolizumab) already capable of targeting them. At the cellular level, the study reveals that elevated androgens in polycystic ovary syndrome upregulate multiple signaling pathways (PI3K/AKT, MAPK1/ERK2, TGF-β, and AMH) in granulosa cells while simultaneously increasing reactive oxygen species, DNA damage, and caspase-3-mediated apoptosis in oocytes. Additionally, hyperandrogenism stimulates ovarian macrophage activation and pro-inflammatory cytokine release, which further disrupts normal granulosa cell function and promotes premature luteinization, creating a self-perpetuating inflammatory cycle that contributes to anovulation-related infertility.

title: Women's health and female fertility: current evidence and knowledge gaps in the Asia–Pacific region
journal: The Lancet Regional Health: Western Pacific
summary:
# Summary

This article examines dietary, lifestyle, and psychosocial factors affecting female fertility and reproductive outcomes in the Asia-Pacific region. Nutritional interventions—particularly Mediterranean diets, folate supplementation, vitamin D sufficiency, and omega-3 polyunsaturated fats (DHA/EPA)—show consistent associations with improved fertility outcomes, reduced miscarriage risk, and higher pregnancy and live birth rates. Conversely, trans fat consumption contributes to insulin resistance and inflammation, negatively impacting fertility. Physical activity enhances insulin sensitivity, metabolic health, and cardiometabolic function, with moderate-intensity exercise improving fertility in women with polycystic ovary syndrome; however, excessive training loads cause relative energy deficiency, disrupting sex hormones and inducing amenorrhea and anovulatory cycles. Chronic psychological stress elevates corticotropin-releasing hormone, cortisol, and prolactin, disrupting the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis and causing menstrual irregularities and ovulatory dysfunction, while depression, anxiety, and perceived stress independently reduce fecundability and live birth probability. Circadian rhythm disruption from sleep deprivation further compromises reproductive hormone regulation, underscoring the importance of integrated lifestyle management in optimizing female fertility.

title: Comparison of Embryo and Egg Quality in Polycystic Ovary Syndrome Patients Having Regular vs Irregular Menstrual Patterns
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that menstrual cycle regularity fundamentally influences reproductive outcomes in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) patients undergoing assisted reproduction. PCOS patients with irregular cycles exhibit a more severe metabolic and endocrinological phenotype characterized by hyperandrogenism, insulin resistance, and hormonal dysregulation, which requires higher gonadotropin doses for stimulation. The systemic abnormalities in irregular cycle patients alter the intrafollicular environment, inhibiting oocyte maturation, spindle formation, and mitochondrial competence, ultimately resulting in reduced blastocyst-stage embryo development and compromised embryo quality. In contrast, PCOS patients with regular menstrual cycles maintain milder endocrine disruption and demonstrate superior preimplantation embryo development, with significantly higher availability of good-quality blastocysts that substantially increases clinical pregnancy odds. The findings highlight that menstrual regularity serves as a key phenotypic marker predicting reproductive competence in PCOS, suggesting that interventions such as lifestyle modification and weight reduction to improve cycle regularity should be prioritized prior to fertility treatment.

title: Luteinizing Hormone β-subunit Gene Polymorphisms and Androgen Levels are Less Predictive of Ovarian Response in Polycystic Ovary Syndrome In vitro Fertilisation Women: A Nested Case–Control Study
summary:
# Summary

This nested case-control study examined whether luteinizing hormone β-subunit gene polymorphisms (rs1800447 and rs34349826) and androgen levels predict ovarian response in PCOS patients undergoing in vitro fertilization. Although these genetic variants—which encode amino acid changes (Trp8Arg and Ile15Thr respectively) that alter LH β-protein conformation and immunoreactivity—have been associated with increased androgen production and folliculogenesis impairment in previous studies, the investigators found that neither the polymorphisms nor circulating testosterone, SHBG, or free testosterone index significantly predicted controlled ovarian hyperstimulation outcomes in their PCOS cohort. The findings suggest that LH β-subunit gene variants and baseline androgen measurements are less reliable biomarkers for identifying hyporesponders or hyperresponders to gonadotropin stimulation in PCOS women undergoing IVF, despite the known physiological role of LH in follicular growth and maturation. This discordance highlights the complex, multifactorial nature of ovarian response in PCOS, where genetic and hormonal factors may be less predictive than other clinical or metabolic parameters.

title: Adverse effects of dyslipidemia on embryo quality and reproductive outcomes in specific subgroups undergoing first IVF/ICSI cycles: A case–control study
summary: Dyslipidemia significantly impairs reproductive outcomes in women undergoing IVF/ICSI, particularly those under 35 years old, by reducing oocyte retrieval rate, cumulative clinical pregnancy rate, and cumulative live birth rate. The detrimental effects operate through multiple mechanisms: lipid-rich follicular fluid triggers oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction, which compromise oocyte maturation and embryo quality, while dyslipidemia also impairs the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis, reducing ovarian responsiveness to gonadotropins and requiring higher hormone doses. Elevated triglycerides and total cholesterol inversely correlate with oocyte quantity and quality, whereas HDL cholesterol shows protective associations with favorable reproductive outcomes. In obese patients (BMI ≥28), dyslipidemia further reduces cleavage rates and disrupts ATP production necessary for early embryonic development. The study highlights that dyslipidemia compounds metabolic disorders common in PCOS, including insulin resistance and obesity, while also increasing pregnancy complications such as gestational diabetes, preeclampsia, and intrahepatic cholestasis, underscoring the importance of lipid management in fertility preservation.

title: Amelioration of polycystic ovarian morphology by Tokishakuyakusan in a PCOS rat model: association with bone morphogenetic protein 4
summary:
# Summary

Tokishakuyakusan (TSS), a traditional Japanese medicine, ameliorates polycystic ovary syndrome through downregulation of bone morphogenetic protein 4 (BMP4) signaling in a rat model of PCOS induced by prenatal dihydrotestosterone exposure. BMP4, a key regulator of follicular growth and steroidogenesis, is abnormally elevated in PCOS and correlates with pathological follicle development and reduced progesterone production. TSS treatment significantly decreased BMP4 and inhibin-βA expression in ovarian tissues and granulosa cells while simultaneously upregulating steroidogenic genes StAR and HSD3B to enhance FSH-induced progesterone synthesis. These molecular changes translated to functional improvements including restoration of regular estrous cycles, reduction of atretic cyst-like follicles, and normalization of ovarian morphology comparable to healthy controls. The findings establish BMP4 signaling modulation as a central mechanism underlying TSS efficacy in PCOS, with potential relevance to the clinical overlap between PCOS and related reproductive disorders including endometriosis.

title: An exploratory study on the impact of endocrine and infectious comorbidities on reproductive outcomes after Palmer-type neosalpingostomy
journal: Romanian Journal of Morphology and Embryology
summary: This study examined how endocrine and infectious comorbidities affect reproductive outcomes following Palmer-type neosalpingostomy, a fertility-preserving surgical procedure for hydrosalpinx. Hydrosalpinx, a major cause of female infertility, produces embryotoxic fluid that reduces implantation and pregnancy rates; however, neosalpingostomy can successfully treat this condition by restoring tubal patency. The analysis identified multiple comorbidity pathways that compromise fertility outcomes: PCOS drives anovulation through hyperandrogenism and insulin resistance while impairing endometrial receptivity, thyroid dysfunction (particularly subclinical hypothyroidism) reduces conception and increases miscarriage risk, and metabolic disorders including obesity and diabetes impair fertility through chronic inflammation and hormonal disruption. Genital tract infections—particularly Chlamydia trachomatis, Ureaplasma, and Mycoplasma species—cause severe tubal damage by inducing chronic inflammation, fibrosis, and fimbrial dysfunction that compromises tubal transport and endometrial receptivity, with coinfections associated with more severe damage and reduced conception rates. Additional comorbidities including endometriosis, uterine fibroids (especially submucosal variants), and microbiome imbalance further reduce pregnancy likelihood after neosalpingostomy. The authors conclude that comprehensive screening and management of these overlapping endocrine and infectious comorbidities is essential to optimize fertility outcomes in women undergoing tubal reconstruction.

title: Cardiovascular Dysfunction in Polycystic Ovary Syndrome: Mitochondrial and Inflammatory Mechanisms
summary:
# Summary: Cardiovascular Dysfunction in Polycystic Ovary Syndrome

Polycystic ovary syndrome significantly increases cardiovascular disease risk through interconnected mitochondrial and inflammatory mechanisms. Mitochondrial dysfunction in PCOS impairs ATP production and calcium homeostasis while elevating reactive oxygen species (ROS), which collectively drive endothelial dysfunction and vascular pathology. These mitochondrial abnormalities activate the NLRP3 inflammasome and trigger chronic low-grade inflammation characterized by elevated TNF-α, IL-6, and CRP, creating a vicious cycle that perpetuates oxidative stress and cardiovascular injury. Insulin resistance and compensatory hyperinsulinemia amplify this pathology by upregulating NADPH oxidase, promoting excessive ROS generation, and driving endocrine dysregulation through increased androgen synthesis and dyslipidemia. The resulting metabolic dysfunction—manifesting as dyslipidemia, hypertension, and impaired nitric oxide bioavailability—combines with oxidative damage to mitochondrial DNA and cardiomyocyte injury, including myocardial hypertrophy and cardiac fibrosis. Thus, oxidative stress represents the central mechanistic link between PCOS and cardiovascular disease, connecting metabolic dysfunction, mitochondrial defects, and chronic inflammation into an integrated pathogenic pathway.

title: Significance of Follicle-Stimulating Hormone Receptor Gene Single-Nucleotide Polymorphism rs6165/rs6166 Analysis for Infertility-Associated Ovarian Disease Susceptibility Prediction and Optimized Individualized Ovulation Induction/Ovarian Stimulation
summary:
# Summary

The follicle-stimulating hormone receptor (FSHR) is expressed on granulosa cells and the endometrium, where it binds FSH to regulate follicle development, granulosa proliferation, and estrogen synthesis. Two FSHR gene single-nucleotide polymorphisms, rs6165 and rs6166, show significant associations with multiple infertility-related ovarian diseases, including polycystic ovary syndrome, premature ovarian insufficiency, endometriosis, and ovarian cancer, though associations vary by population and genotype. The rs6166 GG genotype is associated with higher FSH levels, lower estradiol, and longer follicular phases, while conferring protective effects against endometriosis but increased ovarian cancer risk in certain populations. The rs6165 AA and rs6166 GG genotypes together represent a high-risk haplotype for ovarian cancer, and rs6166 AA genotype increases endometriosis risk, whereas rs6166 GG is protective. These genetic polymorphisms may enable individualized prediction of disease susceptibility and optimization of ovulation induction therapy using agents such as clomiphene citrate, letrozole, or metformin.

title: NLRP3 Inflammasome and Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS): A Novel Profile in Adipose Tissue
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome is characterized by chronic low-grade inflammation and strong associations with insulin resistance and obesity, conditions in which adipose tissue plays a central role as a source of pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-1β and IL-18. The NLRP3 inflammasome, a multiprotein complex that activates caspase-1 to process these cytokines, represents a key mediator linking metabolic stress to both systemic inflammation and impaired insulin signaling; notably, metabolic danger signals including reactive oxygen species, lipotoxicity, ceramide, and ATP activate NLRP3. Surprisingly, this study revealed significantly lower NLRP3 and caspase-1 expression in PCOS adipose tissue compared to non-PCOS controls, suggesting that while the NLRP3 pathway is implicated in PCOS pathogenesis, alternative inflammatory mechanisms or compensatory downregulation following chronic activation may be operative in this condition. Interestingly, insulin itself inhibits NLRP3 activation through blocking ASC speck formation and modulating downstream signaling pathways, raising the possibility that hyperinsulinemia in PCOS paradoxically suppresses local adipose inflammasome activity despite systemic inflammatory manifestations. These findings suggest that the NLRP3 inflammasome may not be the primary driver of adipose tissue inflammation in PCOS, and that understanding tissue-specific and cell-type-specific inflammasome regulation is critical for targeting inflammatory pathways in this condition.

title: Changes in Metabolites and Microbial Communities in Follicular Fluid Associated With Ovarian Function in Patients With Polycystic Ovary Syndrome
summary: This study reveals that polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is characterized by significant alterations in follicular fluid metabolites and microbial communities that correlate with ovarian dysfunction. The analysis identified eight upregulated metabolites in PCOS patients, particularly D-glucose, alongside four downregulated metabolites including histidine and quinolinic acid, indicating disrupted glucose and amino acid metabolism. Microbial profiling showed increased abundance of genera such as Aeromonas, Raoultella, and Rhodococcus in PCOS follicular fluid, while glucose-utilizing species including Alicyclobacillus and Terrimonas were significantly reduced. Notably, the hyperandrogenic hormones testosterone and androstenedione were negatively correlated with beneficial glucose-utilizing bacteria and positively associated with over 50 percent of differentially abundant metabolites. Anti-mullerian hormone (AMH) and antral follicle count (AFC), key markers of ovarian reserve, were significantly associated with the altered metabolite and microbial profiles, suggesting that microbial dysbiosis and metabolic perturbations directly impair oocyte quality and reproductive outcomes in PCOS. These findings establish follicular fluid microbiota and metabolites as important mediators linking endocrine dysfunction to impaired ovarian function in PCOS.

title: The interactions between autophagy and immune in the liver-adipose-ovary circuit of polycystic ovary syndrome
summary:
# Summary

This article examines how dysregulated autophagy across a liver-adipose-ovary circuit drives polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) pathogenesis through interconnected metabolic and immune mechanisms. Abnormal autophagy in adipose tissue induces insulin resistance and inflammatory cytokine release, while hepatic autophagy impairment exacerbates non-alcoholic fatty liver disease and hyperandrogenemia, and ovarian autophagy dysfunction disrupts folliculogenesis—together accounting for PCOS's hallmark features of hyperandrogenemia, insulin resistance, and ovulatory dysfunction. In hypertrophied adipocytes characteristic of PCOS, downregulation of autophagy genes (LAMP1, LAMP2, Atg5) coincides with elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α) and NLRP3 inflammasome activation, which perpetuates insulin resistance through TNF-α-mediated JNK/IKKβ signaling and IRS-1 serine phosphorylation. Additionally, adiponectin dysregulation in PCOS patients (decreased levels, elevated leptin) further impairs autophagy flux and insulin sensitivity. The authors identify therapeutic potential in autophagy-modulating agents such as quercetin and metformin, which could interrupt this pathological circuit and improve metabolic and reproductive outcomes in PCOS.

title: sc-eQTL unveil immunogenetic architecture of polycystic ovary syndrome
summary: This study employs single-cell expression quantitative trait loci (sc-eQTL) analysis integrated with genome-wide association studies to uncover the immunogenetic architecture underlying polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), revealing that immune dysregulation is a central pathogenic mechanism. The research identifies NK cells, CD8+ T cells, and CD4+ T cells as key immune players in PCOS pathogenesis, with dysregulated CD4+/CD8+ T cell ratios and elevated NK cell frequencies contributing to disease development. Single-cell eQTL colocalization analysis identifies IRF1 and MAPRE1 as causal susceptibility genes with cell-type-specific regulatory effects, with IRF1 regulating interferon and inflammatory signaling pathways across multiple T cell subsets, while MAPRE1 controls microtubule dynamics and immune cell migration with notably reduced expression in NK cells of PCOS patients. Additionally, the study confirms metabolic-related genes including DENND1A and THADA as contributors to PCOS through insulin signaling and thyroid hormone pathways, along with established reproductive genes like FSHB that regulate FSH-dependent ovarian function. The integration of these findings suggests that PCOS pathogenesis involves a complex interplay between immune dysregulation driven by IRF1 and MAPRE1, impaired NK cell migration, and metabolic dysfunction, providing molecular insights into the disease's multifactorial nature and potential therapeutic targets.

title: Elevated plasma glucose links intrafollicular bile acids to altered embryological outcomes in IVF patients
summary: Elevated fasting plasma glucose in IVF patients drives increased intrafollicular bile acid concentrations, particularly glycine-conjugated forms like glycochenodeoxycholic acid, while reducing secondary bile acids such as deoxycholic and ursodeoxycholic acids. This altered bile acid profile correlates with impaired ovarian response, including reduced follicle and zygote counts and diminished blastocyst formation, indicating compromised oocyte quality and embryonic development. Paradoxically, patients with higher intrafollicular bile acid levels demonstrated improved clinical pregnancy rates following frozen embryo transfer, suggesting differential metabolic effects on fresh versus cryopreserved embryos. The findings implicate glucose-dependent dysregulation of bile acid homeostasis as a metabolic mechanism linking insulin resistance to reproductive dysfunction, with bile acids acting as signaling molecules that influence both ovarian folliculogenesis and endometrial receptivity. These results suggest that the PCOS-insulin resistance-endometriosis overlap may be mediated partly through altered bile acid metabolism, pointing toward potential therapeutic targets through glycemic control or bile acid modulation to improve fertility outcomes.

title: Gut microbiota and metabolic dysregulation in polycystic ovary syndrome: effects of acupuncture as an adjunct to in vitro fertilization on gut dysbiosis, metabolism, and oocyte quality
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome is characterized by gut microbiota dysbiosis and systemic metabolic dysregulation, including insulin resistance, dyslipidemia, obesity, and hyperandrogenism, with specific dysbiotic bacteria such as Lachnospiraceae, Blautia, and Escherichia-Shigella correlating with elevated fasting glucose, body mass index, and waist circumference. Gut dysbiosis perpetuates metabolic disturbances that trigger chronic inflammation, oxidative stress, and mitochondrial dysfunction, ultimately compromising oocyte maturation and quality. Acupuncture combined with in vitro fertilization significantly reduces pathogenic bacteria (particularly Escherichia-Shigella), improves glucose and lipid metabolism, and decreases body weight and total cholesterol more effectively than IVF alone. Additionally, acupuncture enhances oocyte quality and embryonic developmental potential, likely through regulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis and modulation of energy metabolism and apoptosis in ovarian granulosa cells. These findings demonstrate that acupuncture functions as an effective adjunctive therapy for PCOS by simultaneously addressing gut dysbiosis, metabolic dysfunction, and reproductive outcomes in infertile patients undergoing assisted reproductive technologies.

title: Effects of supplementation with two probiotic strains on metabolic profile, hormonal status, oxidative stress, and quality of life in women with polycystic ovary syndrome: A study protocol for a randomized clinical trial
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome is characterized by insulin resistance, ovulatory dysfunction, hyperandrogenism, and chronic inflammation, with associated risks for type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease. This randomized clinical trial evaluates whether supplementation with Lactobacillus helveticus R0052 and Bifidobacterium longum R0175 can improve metabolic and hormonal profiles in women with PCOS by addressing gut dysbiosis, a condition linked to hormonal disturbances, insulin resistance, and obesity. The two probiotic strains exert therapeutic effects through multiple mechanisms: producing short-chain fatty acids that reduce insulin resistance and inflammation while regulating androgen levels, possessing beta-glucuronidase enzymes for estrogen metabolism, and demonstrating direct antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Additionally, these probiotics enhance intestinal barrier integrity and improve serotonin signaling pathways, thereby alleviating anxiety and depression commonly associated with PCOS. The study protocol aims to comprehensively assess whether probiotic intervention can modulate the gut microbiota to normalize metabolic, hormonal, and oxidative stress parameters while improving quality of life in PCOS patients.

title: Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) affects relative embryo morphokinetics observed by time-lapse imaging: an observational study
summary: PCOS alters the morphokinetic development of preimplantation embryos during in vitro culture, as revealed by time-lapse imaging analysis. PCOS patients exhibited significantly elevated anti-Müllerian hormone levels and demonstrated impaired embryo cleavage synchronicity, manifested by prolonged intervals during cell cycle 2 (s2) and cell cycle 3 (s3), along with delayed transitions from the 4- to 8-cell stage. These morphokinetic alterations resulted in a characteristic pattern of lower CS2-8 ratios but higher CS2-4 ratios in PCOS embryos, indicating asynchronous cell division that may compromise developmental potential and blastocyst quality. Since synchronicity parameters such as s2 and s3 intervals and their ratios (CS2-8, CS2-4, CS4-8) are established markers associated with successful implantation and blastocyst development, the observed delays in PCOS embryos suggest reduced embryo competence. The findings suggest that hyperandrogenaemia in PCOS may drive these early morphokinetic alterations during oocyte maturation and early cleavage stages, contributing to the reproductive challenges faced by PCOS patients undergoing assisted reproductive technologies.

title: Evaluating the role of GnRH agonists in frozen embryo transfer cycles among polycystic ovary syndrome patients: a randomized clinical trial
summary:
# Summary

This randomized clinical trial evaluated whether gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists (GnRHa) improve frozen embryo transfer (FET) outcomes in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) patients. GnRHa pretreatment successfully downregulates the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis by reducing FSH, LH, and estradiol levels, and theoretically enhances endometrial receptivity through pituitary downregulation and modulation of pro-implantation cytokines including IL-6, IL-11, and αvβ3 integrin expression. However, despite these mechanistic benefits and the known pathophysiology of PCOS—wherein hyperandrogenism and insulin resistance disrupt gonadal function and impair endometrial development—GnRHa pretreatment did not significantly improve chemical pregnancy rates (48.12% vs. 45.98%), clinical pregnancy rates (42.84% vs. 41.59%), or ongoing pregnancy rates (28.60% vs. 30.70%) compared to control cycles. Notably, the GnRHa group showed a trend toward higher miscarriage rates (33.3% vs. 26.3%), which aligns with the elevated miscarriage risk inherent to PCOS patients. These findings suggest that while GnRHa achieves hormonal suppression beneficial for FET cycles, this endocrine optimization alone is insufficient to overcome the reproductive disadvantages in PCOS populations, highlighting the need for additional therapeutic strategies targeting underlying metabolic and inflammatory pathways.

title: Glycomic Insights in Gynecological Disease: From Molecular Mechanisms to Precision Diagnostics and Therapeutics
summary:
# Glycomic Mechanisms in PCOS and Endometriosis: Diagnostic and Therapeutic Implications

This review elucidates how dysregulated glycosylation serves as a central molecular mechanism linking metabolic and reproductive dysfunction in polycystic ovary syndrome and endometriosis. In PCOS, elevated inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α and IL-6) upregulate sialyltransferases and branching enzymes like GnT-V, which increase N-glycan complexity and create "don't eat me" signals through hypersialylation that evade immune surveillance while sequestering growth factor receptors. Endometriosis similarly exhibits dysregulated estrogen signaling with reduced ZMIZ1 expression, leading to altered glycosylation patterns on adhesion molecules, growth factors, and cytokines that impair endometrial receptivity. The metabolic dysfunction underlying PCOS—enhanced glycolysis and dysregulated glucose-lipid metabolism—depletes nucleotide sugar donors (UDP-glucose, UDP-GlcNAc, CMP-sialic acid), systematically altering glycan biosynthesis. The article establishes that specific glycomic signatures, including fucosylation and sialylation patterns on CA-125 and exosomal proteins, enable precision diagnostics for gynecological malignancies and could inform PCOS/endometriosis phenotyping when integrated with classical endocrine markers. These findings position glycosylation as both a biomarker and mechanistic target for developing diagnostic panels and therapeutics targeting the shared pathophysiology across these gynecological conditions.

title: Correlation of blood lipid levels with the severity of polycystic ovary syndrome and its predictive value for pregnancy outcome
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that dyslipidemia is a significant feature of polycystic ovary syndrome, with elevated total cholesterol, triglycerides, low-density lipoprotein cholesterol, and apolipoprotein B all strongly correlated with PCOS severity. These lipid abnormalities show positive correlations with elevated androgens (testosterone), gonadotropins (LH and FSH), and anti-müllerian hormone, while displaying negative associations with estradiol levels, highlighting the endocrine dysfunction characteristic of PCOS. Notably, elevated triglycerides, LDL-cholesterol, and apolipoprotein B emerged as independent risk factors for pregnancy failure following ovulation induction therapy, with each demonstrating high predictive accuracy (area under curve >0.83) for identifying patients at risk of adverse pregnancy outcomes. The lipid metabolism abnormalities in PCOS appear mechanistically linked to insulin resistance and obesity, suggesting that dyslipidemia may contribute to infertility through oxidative stress pathways. These findings indicate that lipid profiling, particularly triglycerides and apolipoprotein B measurements, could serve as valuable biomarkers for stratifying PCOS severity and predicting reproductive outcomes in women undergoing fertility treatment.

title: Variability of menstrual cycles by age, polycystic ovary syndrome, and early-life cycle irregularity in the Apple Women’s Health Study
journal: American journal of obstetrics and gynecology
summary:
# Summary

This Apple Women's Health Study examined menstrual cycle variability across reproductive ages in women with PCOS, early-life cycle irregularity, and regular cycles. PCOS was characterized by oligomenorrhea, androgen excess, and prolonged irregular menstrual cycles, with 62% of the PCOS group having obesity compared to 35-36% in control groups. Menstrual cycle length and variability decreased linearly with advancing age across all groups through ages 45-49, driven by age-related follicular pool depletion that reduces inhibin production, elevates FSH, and accelerates dominant follicle recruitment. Early-life cycle irregularity emerged as a significant risk factor for future PCOS diagnosis and was associated with serum markers consistent with PCOS phenotype. Women with irregular cycles at ages 29-46 demonstrated substantially elevated risks for type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease, highlighting the clinical importance of menstrual cycle regularity as a biomarker for long-term metabolic and cardiovascular health outcomes.

title: Within-individual changes in BMI and menstrual irregularity: a cohort study using real-world data
summary: This cohort study demonstrates that within-individual changes in body mass index are strongly associated with menstrual irregularity, with transitions to overweight or obesity increasing risk while weight reduction to normal BMI significantly alleviates symptoms. The relationship between excess adiposity and menstrual dysfunction is mediated through metabolic mechanisms including hyperinsulinemia and elevated peripheral estrogen production, which directly impair ovulatory function. Notably, individuals who achieved long-term BMI reduction to the normal range experienced substantial improvements in menstrual regularity, whereas those transitioning from normal BMI to overweight or obesity developed new-onset irregularities. The findings underscore menstrual cyclicity as a vital health indicator reflecting underlying endocrine and metabolic disturbances, particularly relevant to conditions like PCOS and diabetes mellitus that affect reproductive health. Sustainable weight management through gradual reduction and improved lifestyle factors including regular exercise and better eating habits emerges as a key clinical intervention for enhancing menstrual health and reproductive outcomes in individuals with obesity or overweight status.

title: A mixed-method analysis of health literacy and indicators of well-being in women with polycystic ovary syndrome across the lifespan
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome is a complex, heterogeneous endocrine disorder characterized by irregular menses, hirsutism, polycystic ovarian morphology, and elevated androgens and insulin levels, with significant associations to metabolic complications including obesity, insulin resistance, diabetes, and cardiovascular disease risk. Beyond reproductive manifestations, PCOS substantially impacts psychological well-being, with pre-menopausal women experiencing higher anxiety and depression symptoms compared to post-menopausal women, ultimately leading to decline in health-related quality of life. A critical gap exists in health literacy among women with PCOS, particularly regarding metabolic health impacts and the long-term consequences of the condition beyond infertility, with women demonstrating poor nutrition knowledge, disordered eating patterns, and increased sedentary behavior. Pre-menopausal women actively sought symptom management resources but expressed dissatisfaction with provider education, while post-menopausal women largely viewed PCOS as no longer relevant to their health. Both groups expressed strong desires for personalized, evidence-based, patient-centered resources including life-stage-specific support groups and improved provider engagement, with primary care providers requiring training to effectively educate patients about PCOS's multisystemic impacts and first-line management through weight management, dietary intervention, physical activity, and stress reduction.

title: Role of insulin-regulated aminopeptidase as potential biomarker in
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates insulin-regulated aminopeptidase (IRAP) as a potential biomarker for insulin resistance and polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS). IRAP is a key component of the insulin-GLUT4 glucose uptake pathway, where insulin stimulation normally triggers translocation of both IRAP and GLUT4 to the cell membrane, enabling cellular glucose uptake and maintaining glucose homeostasis. The research demonstrates that serum IRAP levels are significantly lower in PCOS patients, particularly those with insulin resistance, and show strong negative correlations with multiple metabolic markers including fasting glucose, insulin, glycated hemoglobin, and HOMA-IR (a measure of insulin resistance). In insulin-resistant states, the normal process of IRAP cleavage and secretion into the bloodstream is disrupted, resulting in reduced circulating IRAP levels. These findings suggest that decreased serum IRAP may serve as a novel biomarker for identifying insulin resistance and metabolic dysfunction in PCOS, and potentially linking the overlapping pathophysiology of insulin resistance, hyperandrogenemia, and reproductive dysfunction characteristic of this condition.

title: The Intersection of m6A Methylation and Immune Response in PCOS: A Bioinformatics Perspective
summary:
# Summary

This bioinformatics study investigates N6-methyladenosine (m6A) RNA methylation as an epigenetic mechanism linking immune dysfunction to polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS). The researchers identified five m6A regulator genes—WTAP, METTL14, ZC3H13, PCIF1, and RBM15—as key diagnostic markers, constructing a predictive model with 89% accuracy for PCOS identification. In PCOS patients, dysregulated m6A regulators correlate with increased immune cell infiltration in ovarian tissue and altered expression of methylation-controlling genes, with CBLL1 and FTO downregulated while NXF1 is upregulated. The pathogenic cascade involves hormonal imbalance (low progesterone and high androgens) triggering aberrant immune activation, producing inflammatory cytokines and autoantibodies that directly impair ovulation. Gene interaction networks revealed coordinated dysregulation of m6A writer, reader, and eraser complexes, while pathway enrichment analysis highlighted involvement of extracellular matrix remodeling and chemokine signaling in PCOS pathogenesis. These findings establish m6A methylation as a critical epigenetic regulator of the immune-endocrine dysfunction underlying PCOS, offering potential diagnostic biomarkers and therapeutic targets.

title: Micronutrients in polycystic ovary syndrome: molecular pathways, deficiencies, and therapeutic potential
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome is a complex endocrine-metabolic disorder characterized by hyperandrogenism, anovulation, insulin resistance, and chronic low-grade inflammation that increases long-term risks for type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and infertility. Micronutrient deficiencies—particularly in vitamin D, B-complex vitamins, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and antioxidant vitamins—exacerbate insulin resistance, oxidative stress, and hormonal dysregulation through multiple molecular pathways. Vitamin D deficiency specifically links to ovulatory dysfunction and hyperandrogenism by disrupting gene transcription and steroidogenic enzyme expression via the vitamin D receptor, while supplementation improves insulin sensitivity and reduces testosterone and inflammatory markers. B-complex vitamins regulate homocysteine metabolism through one-carbon pathways and DNA methylation; their deficiency elevates homocysteine levels associated with cardiovascular and metabolic complications. Antioxidant vitamins E and C reduce oxidative stress-induced follicular damage by upregulating Nrf2 signaling and antioxidant enzyme expression, counteracting the suppressed Nrf2 levels observed in PCOS patients with hyperandrogenism. Strategic micronutrient supplementation therefore offers therapeutic potential to modulate insulin signaling, inflammatory pathways, and reproductive hormone regulation in PCOS management.

title: Effects of inositol in women with polycystic ovary syndrome: an umbrella review of meta-analyses from randomized controlled trials
summary:
# Summary

This umbrella review demonstrates that inositol, particularly myo-inositol and myo-inositol combined with folic acid, significantly improves multiple metabolic and reproductive outcomes in women with polycystic ovary syndrome. Inositol reduces circulating levels of luteinizing hormone, free testosterone, total testosterone, and fasting insulin while enhancing sex hormone-binding globulin and improving insulin resistance markers including HOMA-IR, thereby addressing the core pathophysiology of PCOS characterized by persistent anovulation, hyperandrogenism, and insulin resistance. These metabolic improvements translate to clinically meaningful reproductive benefits, with inositol treatment increasing ovulation rates and pregnancy rates approximately 2-3 fold compared to placebo or folic acid alone. The mechanism of action involves inositol's participation in insulin signaling, gonadotropin signaling, and follicular growth, supporting both metabolic homeostasis and fertility restoration. Myo-inositol monotherapy or combined formulations demonstrated superior efficacy compared to D-chiro-inositol alone and comparable or complementary effects to metformin, establishing inositol as an evidence-based nutritional supplement for managing PCOS and anovulatory infertility, which accounts for approximately 70% of PCOS-related infertility cases.

title: Building a foundation to promote Women’s health research in Dubai. Study protocol for a project to investigate the feasibility of establishing a dedicated Women’s health biobank
summary: This study protocol describes the establishment of the Dubai Women's Health Study, a dedicated biobank initiative designed to investigate four interconnected gynecological and obstetric conditions: PCOS, endometriosis, pre-eclampsia, and endometrial cancer. Women with PCOS demonstrate significantly elevated risks for cardiovascular disease, endometrial cancer, and asthma, while those with endometriosis face increased risks for both breast and endometrial cancers. Pre-eclampsia during pregnancy is itself associated with elevated cardiovascular disease risk in later life. The biobank will collect comprehensive biological samples including peripheral blood components, peritoneal and endometrial biopsies, and stool samples, alongside detailed metabolic and dietary assessments using standardized diagnostic criteria and food frequency questionnaires. By supporting longitudinal research on endometrial cancer risk in both PCOS and endometriosis populations, and investigating Co-enzyme A's role in pre-eclampsia, this resource aims to advance precision medicine, biomarker discovery, and epidemiological insights into women's health conditions that contribute to the UAE's major public health challenges.

title: Impact of Polycystic Ovary Syndrome Status on Changes in Reproductive Function During a Hypocaloric Dietary Intervention
summary:
# Summary

This study examined how hypocaloric dietary intervention affects reproductive function in women with and without polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), revealing the complex interplay between obesity, insulin resistance, and reproductive dysfunction. Obesity drives menstrual irregularity and anovulatory infertility through a well-characterized mechanism: excess visceral fat causes insulin resistance, leading to compensatory hyperinsulinemia, which suppresses hepatic sex hormone-binding globulin (SHBG) production and increases free androgen bioavailability, thereby promoting hyperandrogenism. PCOS further compounds these effects through hypersecretion of luteinizing hormone and ovarian theca cell dysfunction, resulting in elevated androgens, prolonged follicular phases, and increased endometrial cancer risk. While hypocaloric dietary intervention successfully reduced body weight in both groups and modestly improved certain parameters (reduced follicular phase length in PCOS women, increased mid-luteal progesterone and endometrial thickness in controls), women with PCOS showed remarkable resistance to reproductive improvements despite weight loss. Specifically, PCOS women demonstrated persistent menstrual cycle irregularity, elevated androgens, reduced SHBG, and impaired endometrial development regardless of weight loss magnitude, suggesting that PCOS-related reproductive dysfunction requires interventions beyond simple caloric restriction. These findings indicate that women with PCOS may need prolonged or more intensive dietary interventions to achieve clinically meaningful improvements in menstrual cyclicity and ovulatory function.
