title: Upregulation of PLAU in granulosa cells disrupts steroid hormone synthesis and promotes apoptosis by activating NF-κB signaling pathway in PCOS
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies PLAU (plasminogen activator, urokinase) as a critical mediator of granulosa cell dysfunction in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS). PLAU is significantly upregulated in PCOS granulosa cells, where it activates the NF-κB signaling pathway, subsequently suppressing the expression of steroidogenic enzymes (CYP11A1, CYP19A1, and STAR) that are essential for estrogen synthesis. This PLAU-driven NF-κB activation triggers granulosa cell apoptosis through increased expression of pro-apoptotic factors including Bax and cleaved caspase-3. The research establishes PLAU as a novel pathogenic factor in PCOS by demonstrating that silencing PLAU reverses these effects, restoring steroid hormone synthesis and reducing apoptotic signaling. These findings implicate aberrant PLAU-mediated NF-κB signaling as a key mechanism linking the hormonal imbalance and granulosa cell dysfunction characteristic of PCOS, offering potential therapeutic targets for this reproductive endocrine disorder.

title: The efficacy and safety of Cangfu Daotan decoction in the treatment of infertility associated with polycystic ovary syndrome: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome causes infertility through ovulatory dysfunction and hormonal imbalance, accounting for 6-15% of infertility cases. This meta-analysis of 19 randomized controlled trials involving 2,181 women demonstrates that Cangfu Daotan decoction (CFDTT), a traditional Chinese medicine formula, significantly improves ovulation and pregnancy rates when combined with conventional pharmacotherapy such as clomiphene, metformin, or Diane-35. CFDTT acts through multiple mechanisms including drying dampness, regulating qi flow, transforming phlegm, and draining excess moisture via its component herbs, thereby addressing the underlying pathophysiology of PCOS. The combination therapy proved superior to monotherapy alone in improving ovarian morphology (increased dominant follicle count and ovarian volume), endometrial thickness, and reducing adverse events including ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome and luteinized unruptured follicle syndrome. While CFDTT monotherapy showed comparable efficacy to conventional drugs alone, the synergistic effects of combined treatment support its use as an adjunctive therapy offering a holistic, multi-target approach to PCOS-related infertility management.

title: Integrative bioinformatics, single-cell and experimental evidence for a BPA–m6A–apoptosis axis in granulosa cell dysfunction in polycystic ovary syndrome
summary: This integrative study identifies a mechanistic axis linking bisphenol A (BPA) exposure to polycystic ovary syndrome pathogenesis through dysregulation of N6-methyladenosine (m6A) modification and granulosa cell apoptosis. Elevated BPA in PCOS patients directly binds to five hub genes (LIFR, LMNA, BCL2, ADM, S100P) involved in ovarian steroidogenesis, oxidative stress, and apoptosis, disrupting the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis and promoting hyperandrogenism, insulin resistance, and ovarian dysfunction. Single-cell analysis revealed that LIFR downregulation coupled with LMNA upregulation characterizes granulosa cell clusters exhibiting high m6A dysregulation and elevated apoptosis scores. In vitro experiments confirmed that BPA reduces granulosa cell viability and induces apoptosis through BAX and Caspase-3 activation, while m6A modifiers (YTHDF2, IGF2BP2) and the FTO m6A eraser—genetically associated with PCOS susceptibility—regulate steroid hormone synthesis pathways. These findings establish granulosa cell dysfunction and apoptosis as a critical mechanistic nexus integrating BPA-induced endocrine disruption with PCOS-associated reproductive pathology.

title: Tet methylcytosine dioxygenase 2(TET2)-dependent epigenetic regulation in the pathogenesis of polycystic ovary syndrome
journal: Cellular and Molecular Life Sciences: CMLS
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that elevated TET2 expression and 5-hydroxymethylcytosine (5hmC) levels in PCOS granulosa cells drive disease pathogenesis through epigenetic dysregulation of critical signaling pathways. TET2 catalyzes 5hmC formation at specific genomic loci enriched in MAPK signaling, gap junction communication, and oocyte meiosis genes, while promoting excessive autophagy under hyperandrogenic conditions via a USP45-PRKAA1 axis that stabilizes the autophagy initiator PRKAA1. This pathological autophagy dysregulation compromises cellular integrity and depletes ATP, ultimately impairing folliculogenesis and contributing to anovulation and infertility characteristic of PCOS. Pharmacological TET2 inhibition effectively rescues autophagic abnormalities, suggesting TET2 as a therapeutic target. Additionally, specific 5hmC modification patterns at four genomic loci demonstrate high predictive accuracy for PCOS diagnosis, establishing epigenetic biomarkers for clinical identification of the disorder.

title: Follicular fluid from women with polycystic ovary syndrome induces granulosa cells metabolic dysfunction that is exacerbated by obesity
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that follicular fluid from women with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) induces metabolic dysfunction in granulosa cells, with obesity acting as a significant exacerbating factor. Follicular fluid from PCOS patients is characterized by elevated androgens (testosterone and Δ4-androstenedione), disrupted lipid and amino acid metabolism, increased oxidative stress, and a pro-inflammatory environment. When granulosa cells were exposed to follicular fluid from women with PCOS and obesity, glycolytic capacity was markedly reduced through downregulation of key metabolic enzymes (GLUT1, HK2, and LDHA), and mitochondrial function was impaired, evidenced by decreased maximal respiration and reduced mitochondrial membrane potential. These bioenergetic disturbances represent a hallmark feature of PCOS that contributes to follicular arrest and reduced fertility. The findings suggest that obesity intensifies the metabolic dysfunction observed in PCOS through altered modulation of granulosa cell metabolism, ultimately contributing to the reduced fertility and lower assisted reproductive treatment success rates observed in obese women with PCOS.

title: Exercise Attenuates Polycystic Ovary Syndrome Development via Improved Mitochondrial Proteostasis
journal: American journal of physiology. Endocrinology and metabolism
summary:
# Summary

Exercise attenuates polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) development through restoration of mitochondrial proteostasis and improved metabolic regulation. The study demonstrates that exercise restores estrous cycling, reduces ovarian cyst formation, and alleviates ovarian fibrosis and inflammation in a PCOS model. Mechanistically, exercise activates AMPK signaling and upregulates the mitochondrial protease LONP1, which normalizes the steroidogenic proteins StAR and CYP11A1, thereby reducing the excessive androgen biosynthesis that characterizes PCOS. Exercise further enhances mitochondrial biogenesis through increased PGC-1α and PPARα expression, restoring mitochondrial proteolytic capacity and preventing the accumulation of steroidogenic proteins that drive hyperandrogenism. These findings establish mitochondrial proteostasis as a critical regulatory mechanism linking exercise to PCOS improvement and identify AMPK-LONP1 signaling as a key therapeutic target for managing this disorder.

title: Sleep Disruption and Shift Work Associate with Increased Risk of Reproductive Endocrine Disorders: Evidence from UK Biobank and Mendelian Randomization
summary: This study demonstrates that sleep disruption and shift work significantly increase reproductive endocrine disorder (RED) risk through multiple interconnected mechanisms. Both short (≤5 hours) and long (≥9 hours) sleep duration independently elevated RED risk, with night shift work conferring the highest risk (1.40-fold), particularly when combined with severe sleep restriction (1.72-fold). Mendelian randomization analyses confirmed causal effects of short sleep duration, insomnia, and daytime napping on REDs including PCOS, endometriosis, and menstrual irregularities. Mechanistically, sleep disturbances disrupt the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis and circadian signaling through reduced melatonin secretion, impaired kisspeptin neuron function, and altered metabolic hormone sensitivity, while simultaneously triggering chronic low-grade inflammation via elevated CRP, interleukin-6, and neutrophil levels that damage ovarian function and endometrial receptivity. In PCOS specifically, sleep-induced hyperinsulinemia stimulates ovarian androgen production, promoting visceral adiposity and systemic inflammation that perpetuates metabolic dysregulation. These findings establish sleep quality and circadian alignment as critical modifiable factors influencing reproductive health across the PCOS-endometriosis-insulin resistance disease spectrum.

title: Exosomes for Polycystic Ovary Syndrome Treatment: Mechanisms and Therapeutic Potential
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is a major reproductive and metabolic disorder characterized by infertility, insulin resistance, hyperandrogenism, and chronic low-grade inflammation involving dysregulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis and adipose tissue dysfunction. Exosomes, nanotransport vesicles derived from mesenchymal stem cells (adipose-derived, menstrual blood-derived, bone marrow-derived, and umbilical cord-derived sources), represent an emerging therapeutic strategy for PCOS by suppressing inflammatory markers (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-18, and C-reactive protein), enhancing mitochondrial function, and inhibiting pancreatic β-cell apoptosis. These exosomes carry multiple bioactive molecules including proteins, lipids, mRNA, miRNAs, and lncRNAs, and exhibit lower immunogenicity than stem cells themselves while effectively reversing insulin resistance and reducing metabolic dysfunction. Notably, mesenchymal stem cell-derived exosomes synergistically reduce pro-inflammatory and pro-fibrotic factors (TNF-α and TGF-β) when combined with estrogen, offering a promising alternative to conventional treatments like metformin, oral contraceptives, and ovulation-induction agents. The therapeutic potential of exosomes in PCOS addresses multiple pathogenic mechanisms simultaneously, positioning them as a novel and multifaceted treatment approach for this complex endocrine disorder.

title: Effects of different acupuncture methods on polycystic ovarian syndrome: a systematic review and network meta-analysis
summary: This systematic review and network meta-analysis evaluates the efficacy of three acupuncture methods—body acupuncture, electroacupuncture, and acupoint catgut embedding (ACE)—in treating polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS) and its associated metabolic and endocrine complications. Acupoint catgut embedding emerged as the most effective intervention, with the highest preference scores for reducing testosterone levels (SUCRA = 81.7%), body mass index (SUCRA = 90.9%), waist-hip ratio (SUCRA = 95.3%), luteinizing hormone, and the LH/FSH ratio. All acupuncture modalities significantly reduced key PCOS markers including testosterone, triglycerides, and anthropometric measures, while electroacupuncture showed particular promise for reducing fasting blood glucose and low-density lipoprotein. Notably, acupuncture increased pregnancy rates (SUCRA = 76.6%), likely by regulating the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis and enhancing insulin sensitivity. The prolonged stimulation provided by acupoint catgut embedding appears to offer superior therapeutic benefits compared to the short-term effects of manual acupuncture or the sustained but shorter-duration effects of electroacupuncture, positioning it as an optimal treatment option for PCOS-related hyperandrogenism, metabolic dysfunction, and infertility.

title: Variations in serum ferritin levels in women with polycystic ovarian syndrome undergoing assisted reproduction
journal: Journal of Taibah University Medical Sciences
summary:
# Summary

This study examined serum ferritin levels in women with polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS) undergoing assisted reproduction, investigating the intersection of iron metabolism and reproductive dysfunction. PCOS is characterized by hyperandrogenism, insulin resistance, obesity, and menstrual irregularities, with emerging evidence that elevated serum ferritin—an iron storage marker—contributes to disease pathophysiology. Iron overload in PCOS is associated with oxidative stress, ferroptosis activation in granulosa cells, and impaired ovarian function, while decreased hepcidin, a key iron regulator, correlates with increased insulin resistance and hyperandrogenism. Although PCOS patients exhibited expected endocrine alterations including elevated anti-müllerian hormone and luteinizing hormone with reduced follicle-stimulating hormone, serum ferritin levels showed only weak correlations with reproductive outcomes and metabolic markers. The findings suggest that while iron dysregulation is involved in PCOS pathophysiology through ferroptosis-related mechanisms, ferritin levels alone have limited predictive value for assisted reproductive success in PCOS patients.

title: The role and underlying mechanisms of Qi Gong Wan in enhancing the endometrial receptivity of a rat model with polycystic ovary syndrome
summary:
# Summary

Qi Gong Wan (QGW), a traditional herbal formula, enhances endometrial receptivity and treats infertility in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) rats with insulin resistance by upregulating critical endometrial receptivity markers including HOXA10, HOXA11, integrin-β3 (ITGβ3), leukemia inhibitory factor (LIF), GLUT4, and IGF1, while simultaneously downregulating IGFBP1 and pro-inflammatory IL-6. Beyond its effects on endometrial function, QGW restores regular estrous cycles, reduces androgen and insulin levels, improves ovarian morphology, promotes pinopode formation, and alleviates the chronic inflammatory state characteristic of PCOS-IR. The flavonoid compound wogonin, identified through molecular docking studies, shows strong binding affinity to core targets HOXA10, HOXA11, and IGFBP1, suggesting it is a key active constituent mediating QGW's therapeutic effects. These findings demonstrate that QGW addresses both ovulatory and endometrial dysfunction in PCOS-associated infertility, offering a comprehensive mechanism to restore fertility through restoration of endometrial receptivity and improvement of systemic metabolic and hormonal abnormalities.

title: Physical Activity, Metabolic Dysfunction, and the Kynurenine Pathway in Endometriosis and Polycystic Ovary Syndrome: A Literature Review
summary: Endometriosis and polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) are both major causes of female infertility and share overlapping pathophysiological mechanisms centered on insulin resistance, chronic low-grade inflammation, and metabolic dysfunction. Insulin resistance drives these conditions by promoting inflammation and disrupting sex hormone homeostasis, while chronic inflammation and psychological stress activate the kynurenine pathway, leading to immune dysregulation, oxidative stress, and impaired ovarian function. Dysregulated kynurenine metabolism is associated with insulin resistance, mood disturbances, and reproductive impairment, creating interconnected pathogenic cycles. Physical activity emerges as a multifaceted therapeutic intervention that improves insulin sensitivity, reduces inflammation, modulates the kynurenine system, and shifts tryptophan metabolism toward neuroprotective pathways. Various exercise modalities—including aerobic, resistance, and mind-body exercises—demonstrate beneficial effects in endometriosis by reducing pelvic pain, improving quality of life, and attenuating metabolic and androgenic dysfunction. This integrated mechanistic framework highlights how lifestyle interventions targeting physical activity can simultaneously address multiple pathogenic pathways in both conditions.

title: Low-Carbohydrate (Ketogenic) Diet in Children with Obesity: Part 2—Hormonal Effects of the Ketogenic Diet
summary:
# Summary

A well-formulated ketogenic diet produces substantial hormonal improvements in obese children and adolescents with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), primarily through enhanced insulin sensitivity and reduced basal insulinemia. Within a four-month intervention period, the diet significantly decreased fasting insulin levels, testosterone, morning cortisol, and thyroid T3, while increasing adiponectin levels and restoring menstrual cycle regularity. All eight adolescent girls with PCOS experienced spontaneous menstrual bleeding within one to two months of initiating the diet, accompanied by marked reductions in hyperinsulinemia and hyperandrogenism. Beyond hormonal effects, the ketogenic diet reduces oxidative stress, promotes mitochondrial biogenesis, and induces anti-inflammatory and epigenetic effects. These findings suggest the ketogenic diet may be incorporated as part of comprehensive therapeutic management for PCOS and metabolic disorders associated with insulin resistance in pediatric populations, though monitoring of thyroid function is advisable given potential long-term effects on thyroid hormone metabolism.

title: The Association Between Vitamin D and Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS) in Women: A Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary

Vitamin D deficiency is highly prevalent in women with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) and is associated with the condition's key pathophysiologic features, including insulin resistance, excess ovarian androgen production, luteinizing hormone hypersecretion, and chronic anovulation, which collectively contribute to menstrual irregularities and infertility. Calcitriol, the active form of vitamin D, exerts its effects through vitamin D receptors expressed in reproductive and metabolic organs, where it regulates ovarian steroidogenesis, follicular development, and insulin sensitivity. Observational studies demonstrate robust inverse correlations between serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D levels and multiple metabolic parameters including BMI, insulin resistance, hyperinsulinemia, and androgen levels, while positive correlations exist with sex hormone-binding globulin and HDL cholesterol. Randomized controlled trials show that vitamin D supplementation produces modest but variable improvements in insulin sensitivity, menstrual regularity, ovulation, and lipid profiles, with mechanistic studies revealing upregulation of insulin and LDL receptors and reductions in TGF-β1 bioavailability. However, vitamin D supplementation alone should not be considered a stand-alone therapy for PCOS, as lifestyle modification remains the cornerstone of treatment for improving insulin sensitivity and ovulatory function.

title: Natural Products in the Metabolic and Endocrine Modulation of Polycystic Ovary Syndrome: Current Perspectives
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome is a prevalent endocrine and metabolic disorder characterized by insulin resistance, reproductive dysfunction, hyperandrogenism, and oligo-anovulation, with strong associations to type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular morbidity, and infertility. Natural products including quercetin, resveratrol, berberine, curcumin, and inositol improve PCOS pathology by enhancing insulin sensitivity, reducing oxidative stress and chronic inflammation, and modulating key signaling pathways such as AMPK, SIRT1, and NF-κB. At the molecular level, these compounds target ovarian dysfunction by suppressing androgen-synthesizing enzymes like CYP17A1 while upregulating aromatase activity through mechanisms involving berberine-mediated AMPK activation and quercetin-mediated NF-κB inhibition. Additionally, natural products counteract excessive oxidative stress and endoplasmic reticulum stress, which otherwise activate apoptotic cascades and ferroptosis in ovarian granulosa cells, thereby promoting follicular development and restoring ovulation. Combined with lifestyle interventions such as high-fiber, low-glycemic-index diets and regular physical activity, natural product-based approaches offer metabolically favorable alternatives to conventional pharmacotherapy for improving hormonal balance and reproductive outcomes in PCOS patients.

title: Epidemiological study and clinical consequences of endometriosis and PCOS co-occurrence in a clinical population of women: a cross-sectional study
summary: This cross-sectional study demonstrates that the coexistence of endometriosis and polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) creates substantially worse clinical outcomes than either condition alone, with women having both disorders showing significantly higher rates of infertility (25.6% vs. 15.4%), severe symptom severity, pain intensity, dyspareunia, and dramatically reduced quality of life compared to those with endometriosis only. The shared pathophysiological mechanisms underlying this dangerous overlap include insulin resistance, chronic inflammation, immune dysfunction, and hormonal dysregulation—specifically, insulin resistance in PCOS enhances local aromatase activity to increase estrogen production that promotes endometriotic lesion growth, while ectopic endometrial lesions secrete inflammatory mediators (TNF-α and IL-6) that perpetuate a persistent inflammatory state. The study reveals that comorbid endometriosis-PCOS significantly increases the odds of infertility and poor quality of life even after age adjustment, with symptom severity driven by excessive endometrial proliferation, ectopic tissue growth, and chronic pelvic inflammation that collectively compromise endometrial receptivity and implantation rates. Multiple biological factors—including oxidative stress, immune dysregulation, and hormonal imbalances—jointly contribute to the heightened infertility risk in women with both conditions by impairing oocyte quality and reproductive function. These findings underscore the critical clinical need to recognize and manage endometriosis-PCOS coexistence as a distinct syndrome requiring integrated therapeutic approaches addressing both the inflammatory and metabolic components of the disease.

title: Global Trends and Burden of Non-Neoplastic Gynecologic Diseases Among Women of Childbearing Age, 1990–2021: a GBD 2021 Analysis
summary:
# Summary

This Global Burden of Disease analysis examined non-neoplastic gynecological diseases affecting women of childbearing age from 1990–2021, revealing significant public health impacts across multiple conditions. Polycystic ovary syndrome (affecting 8–13% of women) and endometriosis (5–10%) emerged as major contributors to female infertility, a condition affecting 186 million individuals globally, with hormonal imbalances and excessive inflammation identified as common pathophysiological mechanisms. The burden of these diseases peaked in women aged 35–44 years, with notable geographic variation—North Africa and the Middle East showed the highest prevalence and disability-adjusted life years, while PCOS demonstrated an unusual inverse relationship with socioeconomic development. While endometriosis and genital prolapse showed declining prevalence trends over the study period, female infertility and PCOS exhibited increasing trends, highlighting evolving epidemiological patterns that warrant targeted clinical and public health interventions.

title: An endometrial tissue-based predictive model for polycystic ovary syndrome constructed from immuno-metabolic dysregulation features mediated by ACO1
summary: PCOS is a multifactorial endocrine disorder characterized by reproductive and metabolic abnormalities, including hyperandrogenism, ovulatory dysfunction, insulin resistance, and increased risk of type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease. The study identified ACO1 as a key immunometabolic regulator that is consistently downregulated in PCOS endometrial tissue and inversely correlated with CD8⁺ T cell infiltration. Low ACO1 expression shifts the metabolic profile away from oxidative phosphorylation and mTOR signaling toward immune activation pathways, contributing to altered T cell dynamics characterized by reduced CD8⁺ T cells, elevated M1 macrophages, and activated NK cells in PCOS. ACO1 downregulation appears to mediate endometrial dysfunction and local immune suppression by linking iron metabolism, oxidative stress, and T cell exhaustion. A predictive model incorporating ACO1, CHPF, and STOML1 achieved strong discriminatory performance (AUC = 0.800) for identifying PCOS cases based on endometrial immuno-metabolic features. These findings suggest that ACO1 functions as an immunometabolic coupling factor, and restoring its expression may represent a therapeutic approach to address the dysregulated immune microenvironment and endometrial pathology underlying PCOS.

title: Nicotinamide mononucleotide attenuates hyperandrogenism in a polycystic ovary syndrome-like rat model by suppressing NLRP3 inflammasome–mediated granulosa-cell pyroptosis
summary:
# Nicotinamide Mononucleotide Attenuates PCOS-Associated Hyperandrogenism via NLRP3 Inflammasome Suppression

Nicotinamide mononucleotide (NMN) effectively treats hyperandrogenism in a rat model of polycystic ovary syndrome by suppressing NLRP3 inflammasome-mediated pyroptosis in granulosa cells. In PCOS, dysregulated steroidogenesis and chronic low-grade inflammation converge within the ovary: elevated expression of NLRP3, caspase-1, gasdermin D, and pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-18, TNF-α, IL-6) activate pyroptotic pathways that amplify androgen biosynthesis through upregulation of steroidogenic enzymes (CYP11A1, CYP17A1, 3β-HSD). NMN administration significantly reduced testosterone levels, body weight, and restored estrous cyclicity by suppressing this entire inflammasome-pyroptosis cascade, thereby reducing the pro-inflammatory microenvironment that otherwise blunts follicular function and FSH responsiveness. The therapeutic mechanism involves NMN's elevation of intracellular NAD⁺, which modulates mitochondrial function, redox homeostasis, and inflammatory signaling to protect granulosa cells and improve oocyte quality. These findings establish NLRP3 inflammasome activation as a central amplifier of PCOS pathogenesis and identify NMN as a potential therapeutic agent targeting the intersection of metabolic dysfunction and ovarian inflammation underlying PCOS-associated reproductive failure.

title: Evaluating the PCOS risk algorithm (PriskA): a pilot study
summary:
# Summary

This pilot study evaluated PriskA, a digital risk algorithm for polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), which integrates clinical and biochemical parameters including age, BMI, menstrual history, anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH), testosterone, and sex hormone-binding globulin (SHBG). Women with PCOS exhibited significantly elevated levels of AMH, testosterone, and free androgen index alongside reduced SHBG, higher BMI, increased modified Ferriman-Gallwey scores, and greater total follicle counts compared to controls. The PriskA algorithm demonstrated high diagnostic accuracy with an area under the curve of 0.89, superior to individual variables alone, and achieved a sensitivity of 0.98 and improved specificity of 0.67 when refined. By leveraging these key biochemical and morphological markers associated with PCOS's diverse reproductive manifestations (irregular cycles, subfertility, ovulatory dysfunction) and metabolic features (insulin resistance, obesity, type 2 diabetes risk), PriskA has potential to reduce diagnostic delays and improve clinical management through earlier identification of at-risk individuals, while appropriately distinguishing PCOS from other causes of hyperandrogenism and anovulation.

title: Global Burden of Polycystic Ovary Syndrome: A Systematic Analysis of Pre- and Post-Pandemic Trends from the 2021 Global Burden of Disease Study (1990–2021)
summary:
# Summary

This Global Burden of Disease analysis reveals that polycystic ovary syndrome affects 10–13% of reproductive-age women and exhibits a complex pathophysiology involving insulin resistance, obesity, cardiometabolic disease, hyperandrogenism, and immune dysfunction. The condition shows bidirectional relationships with psychological factors, where PCOS leads to anxiety and depression, which in turn increase risk for infertility and endometriosis, while anxiety itself emerges as a causal risk factor for PCOS development. Global PCOS burden peaked in the 15–49 year age group, with highest disease rates in high-income regions, particularly High-income Asia Pacific (Italy and Japan), and notably increased substantially post-2015 with accelerated growth following the COVID-19 pandemic. The pandemic exacerbated PCOS burden globally, with high-income North America and high-SDI regions showing the largest increases, while PCOS patients' metabolic and cardiometabolic risk factors overlap with severe COVID-19 vulnerability. These findings underscore PCOS as a multisystem disorder with significant psychological comorbidity and emerging pandemic-related epidemiological trends across developed nations.

title: Changes in reproductive system in Indian female students in middle altitude conditions
summary:
# Summary

This study examined reproductive changes in Indian female students relocated to the mid-altitude city of Cholpon Ata (1600 meters), revealing substantial disruption of the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis in response to chronic mild hypoxia and acclimatization stress. Approximately 40% of participants developed menstrual irregularities, with elevated FSH (35%) and LH (30%) levels indicating impaired ovarian function, while decreased estradiol (25%) and progesterone (10%) suppressed follicular development and luteal phase competence. Environmental hypoxia and acclimatization stress collectively triggered these hormonal imbalances—elevated prolactin in 20% of students caused menstrual disruption, and increased ovarian volume in 20% reflected dysfunctional follicle dynamics. A strong positive correlation between duration of residence and LH elevation (r=0.72) suggests that prolonged altitude exposure progressively compromises reproductive endocrine function, with potential implications for ovulatory capacity and endometrial receptivity.

title: Metabolic profiling of a polycystic ovary syndrome-like organoid model reveals the critical role of glutamine in local endometrial dysregulation related to implantation failure
journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), affecting 6-20% of women globally, is characterized by hyperandrogenism and insulin resistance that disrupt ovulation and fundamentally alter the endometrial microenvironment, leading to implantation failure and increased risk of endometrial cancer. Using a PCOS-like organoid model, researchers identified glutamine metabolism as the critical metabolic pathway dysregulated in the endometrial microenvironment, where glutamine abnormally enhances endometrial stromal cell decidualization while simultaneously inducing altered mitochondrial function. Excessive androgen exposure promoted pathological cell proliferation and migration while simultaneously triggering cellular damage and apoptosis, mechanisms that increase endometrial cancer risk in PCOS patients. The disrupted metabolic state, particularly altered glutamine and amino acid metabolism in the fluid surrounding organoids, contributes to immune cell activation and heightened inflammation that directly impairs embryo implantation. These findings reveal how PCOS-associated hyperandrogenism creates a hostile endometrial microenvironment through both metabolic dysfunction and immune dysregulation, providing mechanistic insight into PCOS-related reproductive failure and cancer susceptibility.

title: Role of Lifestyle and Nutrition in Menstrual Cycle Regularity: Associations with Body Composition and Dietary Habits
summary: This study demonstrates that nutritional status and lifestyle factors are key modulators of menstrual cycle regularity and reproductive health in young women. Both undernutrition and excess adiposity are associated with hormonal imbalances and irregular menstrual cycles, while metabolic disturbances such as insulin resistance and chronic low-grade inflammation disrupt the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis through multiple mechanisms including altered androgen production and reduced sex hormone-binding globulin synthesis. Notably, lower meat consumption was significantly associated with menstrual irregularity, likely because meat provides bioavailable iron, zinc, and high-quality proteins essential for ovarian function and steroidogenesis. The research highlights that dietary patterns influence endometrial physiology through modulation of estrogen metabolism and inflammatory pathways, with broader implications for hormone-dependent gynecological conditions including endometriosis, adenomyosis, and polycystic ovary syndrome. Adherence to healthier dietary models such as the Mediterranean diet combined with regular physical activity improves metabolic profiles and reduces menstrual disorders. These findings underscore that menstrual cycle regularity is mediated by nutritional and metabolic factors independent of major anthropometric differences, suggesting that dietary quality and nutrient intake represent modifiable targets for optimizing reproductive health.

title: Italian Expert Consensus on Women’s Nutrition Across the Life Course: A Modified Delphi Study
summary: This Italian expert consensus study establishes personalized nutritional strategies as fundamental to women's health across the entire life course. Italian experts reached strong consensus on stage-specific micronutrient interventions, including individualized folate supplementation (400 µg/day) and active folates for assisted reproduction, universal DHA supplementation (200–300 mg/day) during pregnancy, and vitamin D and calcium supplementation in menopause. For gynecological conditions including endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), and gestational diabetes, personalized dietary approaches combined with targeted supplementation—particularly inositols for PCOS and gestational diabetes—received overwhelming agreement from the expert panel. The consensus emphasizes that even physiological pregnancies warrant personalized nutritional patterns based on individual dietary deficiencies, while postpartum iron supplementation is recommended specifically for non-breastfeeding women. Vitamin D's recognized roles in fetal skeletal development, calcium metabolism, immune regulation, and placental function underscore its clinical importance, with dosing adjusted based on clinical context. Overall, this modified Delphi study reinforces that nutrition represents a critical modifiable factor for optimizing reproductive health and managing gynecological conditions throughout women's lives.

title: Triglyceride–glucose index and assisted reproductive outcomes in non-PCOS women: evidence from a propensity score–weighted cohort of 9,903 patients
summary: This propensity score–weighted study of 9,903 non-PCOS women examined whether the triglyceride–glucose (TyG) index, a simple surrogate marker of insulin resistance, predicts outcomes in assisted reproductive technology. Insulin resistance is well established to impair multiple reproductive processes including oocyte quality, embryo development, follicular growth, steroidogenesis, and endometrial receptivity, ultimately reducing pregnancy outcomes and contributing to reduced reproductive potential particularly in PCOS patients. The study found that after statistical adjustment, the TyG index showed minimal association with embryologic outcomes (oocyte retrieval, maturation, fertilization, and blastocyst formation) or clinical pregnancy and live birth rates in non-PCOS women, with only a modest non-linear pattern suggesting slightly higher live birth rates at the third quartile. These findings suggest that while insulin resistance mechanisms significantly compromise fertility through multiple biological pathways, the TyG index as a surrogate marker has limited clinical utility for predicting ART outcomes in non-PCOS women specifically. The results indicate that subclinical metabolic disturbances affecting non-PCOS women may not translate to measurable reductions in ART success, despite documented effects of insulin resistance on reproductive physiology.

title: Regulatory risk loci link disrupted androgen response to the pathophysiology of polycystic ovary syndrome
summary:
# Summary

PCOS-risk genetic variants disrupt binding sites for critical transcription factors—NR4A1/2, NHLH2, FOXA1, and WT1—that regulate gonadotropin signaling, folliculogenesis, and steroidogenesis, leading to the elevated androgen levels characteristic of the disease. HPG axis disruption results in abnormal LH:FSH ratios and reproductive abnormalities including anovulation, oligomenorrhea, and infertility, while also contributing to endometriosis pathogenesis. The study highlights how specific variants, such as rs11031006 disrupting SF1 binding in the FSHB enhancer and rs28441318/rs10117455 regulating DENND1A expression, mechanistically link regulatory disruptions to androgen dysregulation. Additionally, PCOS-associated hyperinsulinemia worsens hyperandrogenism and drives metabolic comorbidities including obesity and type II diabetes, demonstrating how regulatory variants in tissue-specific regulatory elements—including the IRX3-FTO locus affecting fetal brain, pancreas, adipocytes, and endothelial cells—converge on multiple pathways underlying PCOS pathophysiology.

title: Comparing Ovulation Outcomes of Letrozole-Tamoxifen-Estradiol with and without Vitamin E in Infertile Women with Polycystic Ovary Syndrome: A Double-Blind, Randomized Clinical Trial
summary:
# Summary

This double-blind randomized clinical trial demonstrates that vitamin E supplementation significantly enhances ovulation outcomes when added to a letrozole-tamoxifen-estradiol protocol for treating infertility in women with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS). By day 7 of ovulation induction, the vitamin E group showed substantially larger follicles (14.15 mm versus 10.90 mm), increased follicle counts, and improved endometrial thickness compared to controls, with these advantages persisting through day 12. Mechanistically, vitamin E acts as a potent antioxidant that neutralizes free radicals, protects cell membranes, preserves oocyte mitochondrial function, and improves endometrial receptivity—effects particularly valuable given that oxidative stress contributes to PCOS-related infertility and poor reproductive outcomes. The study also highlights vitamin E's broader therapeutic benefits for PCOS, including improvements in insulin resistance, lipid profiles, and reduction of menstrual-related symptoms like dysmenorrhea and pelvic pain. Although the pregnancy rate was numerically higher in the vitamin E group (11.1% versus 6.7%), this difference did not reach statistical significance, suggesting that larger-scale studies are warranted to confirm clinical pregnancy benefits. These findings support incorporating vitamin E into fertility treatment protocols for PCOS patients seeking to optimize follicular development and endometrial preparation.
